topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT601 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction - Classification of Engineering Industry - Manufacturing Processes - Hot Working - Foundry Operations - Furnace and Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Engineering Industry?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> An **Engineering Industry** is an economic establishment where raw materials (metals, polymers, ceramics, composites) are systematically transformed into finished or semi-finished engineering goods through controlled manufacturing processes involving human labour, machinery, energy, and technology, governed by safety, quality, and productivity standards.

In the context of **Hazard Control in Industries**, this definition is critical because every transformation step — from a raw ingot to a finished gear — introduces **mechanical, thermal, chemical, electrical, and ergonomic hazards** that must be identified, assessed, and mitigated.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy — The Kitchen-to-Factory Analogy

Imagine a professional kitchen preparing a meal:

- The **chef** = The **engineer** (decision-maker, controls process variables)
- The **recipe** = The **manufacturing process** (casting, forging, welding)
- The **ingredients** = **Raw materials** (ingots, billets, scrap)
- The **stove** = The **furnace** (heat source for hot working)
- The **knife/cutting board** = **Machine tools** (lathes, mills, drills)
- The **kitchen hazards** (fire, cuts, burns) = **Industrial hazards** (fire, mechanical injury, burns)

Just like a chef must understand the recipe before cooking, a safety engineer must **understand the manufacturing process** before designing hazard controls. You cannot control what you do not understand.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Engineering Industry

The **KTU 2024 Scheme** classifies engineering industries based on multiple parameters. The most academically rigorous classification is:

### 📊 Table 1.1 — Classification of Engineering Industries

| Classification Basis | Category | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Based on Product** | Heavy Engineering | Ships, locomotives, turbines |
| | Light Engineering | Consumer goods, instruments |
| | Medium Engineering | Machine tools, pumps |
| **Based on Raw Material** | Ferrous | Iron & Steel plants |
| | Non-Ferrous | Aluminum, Copper, Zinc smelters |
| **Based on Process Type** | Primary Processing | Smelting, Refining |
| | Secondary Processing | Casting, Forging, Machining |
| | Tertiary Processing | Assembly, Surface finishing |
| **Based on Ownership** | Public Sector | BHEL, SAIL, ONGC |
| | Private Sector | Tata Steel, L&T |
| | Multinational | Ford, Toyota, GE |
| **Based on Output Volume** | Job Shop | Custom prototypes |
| | Batch Production | Limited-run parts |
| | Mass Production | Automotive, electronics |
| | Continuous Production | Petrochemical, steel mills |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** From a **Hazard Control** perspective, the **process-based classification** is most important because each process (casting, forging, welding) carries **distinct hazard profiles**. The syllabus specifically focuses on these process-induced hazards.

---

## 1.3 Manufacturing Processes — The Universal Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Manufacturing Process** is a sequence of scientific and engineering operations applied to raw material to alter its **geometry, properties, or appearance** to produce a useful engineering product.

### The Three Universal Categories

1. **Primary Shaping Processes** — Form the initial shape from raw material
   - Casting, Forging, Rolling, Extrusion, Drawing

2. **Secondary Shaping Processes** — Refine the shape and dimensions
   - Machining (Turning, Milling, Drilling, Grinding)
   - Cold working operations

3. **Tertiary Processes** — Improve properties, finish, or join
   - Heat treatment, Surface finishing, Assembly

### 🔍 The Clay-Analogy (Universal Intuition)

Take a lump of clay (raw material):

- **Hot Working** = Warm up the clay → it's soft → easy to shape (forging analogy)
- **Casting** = Pour molten clay into a mold → it solidifies (foundry analogy)
- **Machining** = Once cooled, scrape/cut it to precision
- **Joining** = Stick two pieces together with adhesive

Every manufacturing process maps to one of these clay transformations!

---

## 1.4 Hot Working — The Heart of Heavy Manufacturing

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Hot Working** is a metal forming process in which plastic deformation is carried out at a temperature **above the recrystallization temperature** of the metal, such that the deformed grains are immediately replaced by new strain-free grains, preventing strain hardening.

### Key Conditions

- Temperature > **Recrystallization Temperature** (≈ **0.6 × Tₘ**, where Tₘ is the melting point in Kelvin)
- No work hardening
- Lower flow stress
- Higher ductility
- Better formability for large deformations

### 🔍 Real-World Analogy — Hot Bread vs Cold Dough

Imagine working with bread dough:

- **Cold dough** (cold working) → tough, cracks, snaps, requires more force
- **Warm dough** (hot working) → soft, pliable, easy to mold into any shape

Similarly, hot steel behaves like soft dough at high temperatures, allowing massive deformations (rolling into sheets, forging into turbine shafts) with much less force.

---

## 1.5 Foundry Operations — The Casting Universe

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Foundry** is a manufacturing facility where molten metal is poured into a mold of the desired shape, allowed to solidify, and then extracted to produce a metal part called a **Casting**.

### The Foundry is the Birthplace of Industrial Components

Almost every metal component — from a simple machine bracket to a complex engine block — begins life in a foundry. Foundries are also among the **most hazard-prone industries** due to:
- Extreme temperatures (**1500 °C molten metal**)
- Toxic fumes and dust
- Heavy material handling
- Explosive dust (silica)
- Burns, radiant heat exposure

---

## 1.6 Furnace and Equipment — The Heat Engine

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Furnace** is an enclosed chamber or structure designed to generate and maintain high temperatures through controlled combustion of fuel or through electrical resistance/induction for industrial heating purposes.

### Why Furnaces Matter in Hazard Control

Furnaces are the **primary ignition sources** in industrial fires and explosions. They handle:
- **Liquid fuels** (furnace oil, diesel) — fire/explosion hazard
- **Gaseous fuels** (LPG, natural gas) — explosion hazard
- **Solid fuels** (coal, coke) — dust explosion, CO poisoning
- **Electrical energy** — arc flash, electrocution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Safety Focus:** Every furnace must comply with **Indian Factories Act 1948**, **IS 2825** (furnace safety), and **NFPA 86** (Ovens and Furnaces) standards for hazard control.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Engineering Industry — Operational Architecture

The engineering industry operates on the **Input → Process → Output** model:

$$\text{Raw Material} + \text{Energy} + \text{Labour} + \text{Machinery} \xrightarrow{\text{Manufacturing Process}} \text{Finished Product} + \text{Waste/By-products}$$

### Why This Matters for Safety

Each **input** is a potential hazard source:
- **Raw materials** → toxic dust, reactive metals
- **Energy** → thermal, electrical, kinetic
- **Labour** → ergonomic, psychological stressors
- **Machinery** → mechanical, noise, vibration

Each **output by-product** is also a hazard:
- **Solid waste** → slag, swarf, dust
- **Liquid waste** → coolants, oils, effluents
- **Gaseous waste** → CO, SO₂, NOₓ, VOCs

---

## 2.2 Manufacturing Process — Detailed Taxonomy

### A. Primary Forming Processes

**1. Casting (Foundry Process)**
- Sand casting, Die casting, Investment casting, Centrifugal casting
- **Hazard profile**: Burns, fume inhalation, explosions, dust

**2. Forming (Bulk Deformation)**
- Forging, Rolling, Extrusion, Drawing
- **Hazard profile**: Impact injuries, forging hammer hazards, entrapment

**3. Powder Metallurgy**
- Compaction + Sintering
- **Hazard profile**: Dust explosion, fine particulate toxicity

### B. Material Removal Processes (Machining)

- Turning, Milling, Drilling, Grinding, Boring
- **Hazard profile**: Flying chips, sharp tools, coolant exposure

### C. Joining Processes

- Welding (Arc, Gas, Resistance), Brazing, Soldering, Riveting
- **Hazard profile**: UV radiation, fumes, electric shock, fire

### D. Surface Treatment

- Painting, Galvanizing, Anodizing, Plating
- **Hazard profile**: Chemical exposure, VOC, flammability

---

## 2.3 Hot Working — The Deep Theory

### The Physics of Hot Working

When a metal is deformed at room temperature (**Cold Working**):
- Dislocations multiply → **strain hardening**
- Strength increases, ductility decreases
- Cracks propagate easily

When deformed above recrystallization temperature (**Hot Working**):
- New grains nucleate continuously → **dynamic recrystallization**
- Strength is restored, ductility is maintained
- No residual work hardening

### The Three Hot Working Sub-Categories

| Sub-Process | Description | Equipment | Temperature Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hot Rolling** | Reducing thickness by passing between rolls | Rolling mill, reheating furnace | 900–1300 °C (steel) |
| **Hot Forging** | Shaping by compressive impact/pressure | Hammer, Press, Die | 800–1250 °C (steel) |
| **Hot Extrusion** | Forcing through a die orifice | Hydraulic press, container | 400–500 °C (Al), 1100–1300 °C (steel) |

### The Hot Working Equation (Homologous Temperature)

$$T_H = \frac{T_{\text{working}}}{T_m}$$

Where:
- $T_H$ = **Homologous temperature** (dimensionless)
- $T_{\text{working}}$ = Working temperature in Kelvin
- $T_m$ = Melting point in Kelvin

**Rule**: For true hot working, $T_H \geq 0.6$ (or 0.5 for non-ferrous)

---

## 2.4 Foundry Operations — Process Flow Analysis

### The Complete Foundry Workflow

```
Pattern Making → Mold Making → Core Making → Melting → Pouring →
Solidification → Shakeout → Cleaning → Inspection → Heat Treatment
```

### Step-by-Step Hazard Mapping

| Step | Process | Primary Hazards | Control Measures |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pattern Making** | Wood/Metal pattern fabrication | Wood dust, noise | Local exhaust ventilation |
| **Mold Making** | Sand + binder + water | Silica dust, ergonomic | PPE, dust masks |
| **Melting** | Furnace heating to 1400–1600 °C | Burns, CO, fume | Furnace interlocks, PPE |
| **Pouring** | Transfer of molten metal | Splashes, radiant heat | Face shields, aprons |
| **Shakeout** | Casting separation from sand | Dust, noise, vibration | Automated shakeout |
| **Fettling** | Grinding of castings | Sparks, dust | Wet grinding, exhaust |
| **Heat Treatment** | Controlled heating/cooling | Furnace atmosphere | Gas monitoring |

### Key Foundry Material — Molding Sand

Molding sand is a carefully engineered mixture of:
- **Silica sand** (SiO₂): 85–95%
- **Clay** (bentonite): 5–10% (binder)
- **Water**: 2–8% (activator)
- **Additives**: coal dust, cereals

> [!WARNING]
> **Silica dust is a Category 1A Carcinogen** (IARC). Prolonged inhalation causes **silicosis**, an irreversible lung disease. This is one of the **top 5 hazards** in foundries globally.

---

## 2.5 Furnace and Equipment — The Master Classification

### A. Based on Heat Source

#### 1. Fuel-Fired Furnaces

| Furnace Type | Fuel | Application | Key Hazard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cupola Furnace** | Coke + metal | Iron melting | CO, dust |
| **Reverberatory Furnace** | Coal/oil/gas | Non-ferrous melting | Flame backfire |
| **Crucible Furnace** | Gas/oil | Small foundries | Spillage |
| **Open Hearth Furnace** | Gas/oil | Steel making | High volume emissions |
| **Rotary Furnace** | Oil/gas | Aluminum | Tilt failure |

#### 2. Electric Furnaces

| Furnace Type | Heating Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Arc Furnace (EAF)** | Electric arc | Steel scrap melting |
| **Induction Furnace** | Electromagnetic induction | Specialty steel, alloys |
| **Resistance Furnace** | Resistance heating | Heat treatment |
| **Vacuum Arc Furnace** | Arc under vacuum | Titanium, superalloys |

### B. Based on Use

- **Melting Furnaces** — Convert solid metal to liquid
- **Reheating Furnaces** — Heat solid metal to plastic state
- **Heat Treatment Furnaces** — Annealing, normalizing, quenching
- **Holding Furnaces** — Maintain molten metal at temperature
- **Drying Furnaces** — Remove moisture from molds, cores

### C. Based on Operating Atmosphere

- **Air Furnace** — Normal atmosphere
- **Controlled Atmosphere Furnace** — Inert gas (N₂, Ar) or reducing gas (H₂)
- **Vacuum Furnace** — Pressure below atmospheric

### D. Based on Construction

- **Batch Furnace** — Load, heat, unload (intermittent)
- **Continuous Furnace** — Steady material flow (e.g., walking beam, pusher)

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Table** — Memorize these for ESE and internal assessments.

| Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_H = \dfrac{T_{\text{working}}}{T_m}$ | Homologous temperature | dimensionless | Hot working condition |
| $\Delta H = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat | kJ | Heating load of furnace |
| $\eta_{\text{furnace}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{Q_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100$ | Thermal efficiency | % | Furnace performance |
| $C_y = \dfrac{W_{\text{casting}}}{W_{\text{metal poured}}} \times 100$ | Casting yield | % | Foundry productivity |
| $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless | Fluid flow in furnace |
| $P_{\text{theory}} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$ | Forging power (ideal) | kW | Hot forging estimation |
| $\text{SF} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\tau_{\text{applied}}}$ | Safety factor | dimensionless | Furnace structural design |
| $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat transfer rate | W | Furnace wall losses |
| $T_m(\text{°C}) = 0.6 \cdot T_m(\text{K}) - 273$ | Recrystallization estimate | °C | Approx. hot work temp |
| $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | Mass flow rate | kg/s | Pouring rate in casting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on $\sigma_f \cdot \ln(A_0/A_f)$**: This is the **true stress × true strain** relation in forging. $\sigma_f$ is flow stress (MPa), $A_0$ is initial cross-section, $A_f$ is final cross-section.

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility — Real-World Applications

### Why These Concepts Matter

1. **For designing safety interlocks on furnaces** — knowing the operating temperature helps select the right temperature-resistant PPE and interlocks.

2. **For foundry dust control** — understanding that silica dust causes silicosis drives the design of local exhaust ventilation (LEV) systems.

3. **For ergonomic hazard assessment** — Job shops vs mass production lines have different repetitive strain injury (RSI) profiles.

4. **For emergency planning** — A continuous process plant (steel mill) requires different fire suppression than a batch job shop.

5. **For regulatory compliance** — Indian Factories Act, OSHA 29 CFR 1910, NFPA standards all reference process types.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Homologous Temperature & Hot Working Limit

### Problem Statement

For pure iron, melting point $T_m = 1538\ °C = 1811\ K$. Find the **minimum hot working temperature**.

### Given Data

- Melting point: $T_m = 1811\ K$
- Hot working threshold: $T_H \geq 0.6$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1**: Recall the definition of homologous temperature.

$$T_H = \frac{T_{\text{working}}}{T_m}$$

**Step 2**: Set the threshold condition for hot working.

$$T_H \geq 0.6$$

$$\frac{T_{\text{working}}}{T_m} \geq 0.6$$

**Step 3**: Solve for the working temperature.

$$T_{\text{working}} \geq 0.6 \times T_m$$

**Step 4**: Substitute $T_m = 1811\ K$.

$$T_{\text{working}} \geq 0.6 \times 1811\ K$$

**Step 5**: Perform the multiplication.

$$T_{\text{working}} \geq 1086.6\ K$$

**Step 6**: Convert to Celsius for practical use.

$$T_{\text{working}}(°C) \geq 1086.6 - 273$$

$$T_{\text{working}}(°C) \geq 813.6\ °C$$

**Step 7**: Apply practical safety margin (industry uses 0.65–0.70).

$$T_{\text{working}} \approx 900\ °C \text{ (practical forging temperature for iron/steel)}$$

### Final Answer

> **Minimum hot working temperature for pure iron ≈ 900 °C** (practical industrial value, considering safety margin and alloying effects).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Furnace Heat Load Calculation

### Problem Statement

A furnace heats **1000 kg of steel** from **30 °C to 1200 °C**.
- Specific heat of steel: $c_p = 0.67\ kJ/(kg·K)$
- Furnace efficiency: $\eta = 40\%$

Calculate: **(a)** Useful heat required, **(b)** Total fuel energy input.

### Given Data

- Mass: $m = 1000\ kg$
- Initial temperature: $T_1 = 30\ °C$
- Final temperature: $T_2 = 1200\ °C$
- Specific heat: $c_p = 0.67\ kJ/(kg·K)$
- Efficiency: $\eta = 0.40$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1**: Calculate temperature difference.

$$\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 1200 - 30 = 1170\ K$$

**Step 2**: Apply sensible heat formula.

$$Q_{\text{useful}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 3**: Substitute values.

$$Q_{\text{useful}} = 1000 \times 0.67 \times 1170$$

**Step 4**: Compute intermediate product.

$$1000 \times 0.67 = 670\ kJ/K$$

**Step 5**: Multiply by $\Delta T$.

$$Q_{\text{useful}} = 670 \times 1170\ kJ$$

**Step 6**: Final computation.

$$Q_{\text{useful}} = 783{,}900\ kJ = 783.9\ MJ$$

**Step 7**: Apply furnace efficiency formula.

$$\eta = \frac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{Q_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100$$

**Step 8**: Rearrange for fuel input.

$$Q_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{\eta} = \frac{783{,}900}{0.40}$$

**Step 9**: Compute final answer.

$$Q_{\text{fuel}} = 1{,}959{,}750\ kJ \approx 1959.75\ MJ$$

### Final Answer

- **(a)** $Q_{\text{useful}} = 783.9\ MJ$
- **(b)** $Q_{\text{fuel}} = 1959.75\ MJ$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Hazard Control:** A furnace of this size requires **~1960 MJ of fuel energy**. Any uncontrolled release of this energy (e.g., fuel leak + ignition) would cause a catastrophic fire/explosion. This justifies strict **furnace isolation, flame monitoring, and fuel cut-off interlocks**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Casting Yield Calculation

### Problem Statement

A foundry pours **500 kg of molten aluminum** into molds. After shakeout and fettling, the **finished castings** weigh **380 kg**.
- Runner and riser weight: 90 kg
- Sprue weight: 30 kg

Calculate: **(a)** Casting yield, **(b)** Process efficiency.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1**: Define casting yield formula.

$$C_y = \frac{W_{\text{casting}}}{W_{\text{metal poured}}} \times 100$$

**Step 2**: Substitute values.

$$C_y = \frac{380}{500} \times 100$$

**Step 3**: Compute ratio.

$$\frac{380}{500} = 0.76$$

**Step 4**: Convert to percentage.

$$C_y = 76\%$$

**Step 5**: Define process efficiency (actual castings vs metal not in runner/riser/sprue).

$$W_{\text{usable}} = W_{\text{casting}} + W_{\text{runner}} + W_{\text{sprue}}$$
$$W_{\text{usable}} = 380 + 90 + 30 = 500\ kg\ (\text{all metal accounted})$$

**Step 6**: Effective yield (excluding runner/riser/sprue waste if remelted).

If runner/riser/sprue is **remelted and reused** (typical in industry):
$$C_{y,\text{effective}} = \frac{W_{\text{casting}}}{W_{\text{poured}}} \times 100 = 76\%$$

If considered as waste:
$$\eta_{\text{process}} = \frac{380}{380 + 90 + 30} \times 100 = 76\%$$

### Final Answer

- **Casting Yield = 76%**
- **Process Efficiency = 76%** (no loss; runner/riser typically recycled)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Typical industry benchmarks — Sand casting yield: **50–70%**; Die casting yield: **85–95%**; Investment casting yield: **70–90%**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Hazard Profile

### Master Comparison Table: Manufacturing Processes & Their Hazards

| Process | Temperature | Main Hazards | Severity Rating | Key Safety Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sand Casting** | 30–1600 °C | Silica dust, burns, explosions | **HIGH** | IS 8973, OSHA silica |
| **Die Casting** | 300–700 °C | High-pressure injection, burns | **HIGH** | OSHA 1910.217 |
| **Hot Forging** | 800–1250 °C | Hammer impact, scale, burns | **VERY HIGH** | IS 2825, ANSI B11 |
| **Hot Rolling** | 900–1300 °C | Pinch points, scale, noise | **HIGH** | NFPA 86 |
| **Induction Furnace** | 1500 °C+ | Electromagnetic, water leak | **HIGH** | IEEE 844 |
| **Arc Furnace** | 1700 °C+ | Arc flash, melt-through | **VERY HIGH** | NFPA 70E |
| **Welding** | 1500–6000 °C | UV, fumes, electric shock | **HIGH** | IS 818, ANSI Z49.1 |
| **Machining** | 25–800 °C | Chips, coolant, entanglement | **MEDIUM** | IS 1891, OSHA 1910.213 |
| **Heat Treatment** | 200–1100 °C | Atmospheres, quench cracks | **MEDIUM-HIGH** | NFPA 86 |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Forging Force Estimation

### Problem Statement

A cylindrical steel billet of cross-section **$A_0 = 10{,}000\ mm^2$** is forged to **$A_f = 4{,}000\ mm^2$**.
- Flow stress at forging temperature: $\sigma_f = 80\ MPa$
- Friction factor: $k_f = 1.5$

Calculate the **theoretical forging force**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1**: Apply the ideal forging equation (no friction).

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = \sigma_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

**Step 2**: Substitute values.

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = 80 \times 10000 \times \ln\left(\frac{10000}{4000}\right)$$

**Step 3**: Compute the ratio.

$$\frac{10000}{4000} = 2.5$$

**Step 4**: Take natural logarithm.

$$\ln(2.5) = 0.9163$$

**Step 5**: Multiply through.

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = 80 \times 10000 \times 0.9163\ N$$

**Step 6**: Compute.

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = 800{,}000 \times 0.9163 = 733{,}040\ N$$

**Step 7**: Apply friction factor.

$$P_{\text{actual}} = P_{\text{ideal}} \times k_f = 733{,}040 \times 1.5$$

**Step 8**: Final value.

$$P_{\text{actual}} = 1{,}099{,}560\ N \approx 1.1\ MN$$

### Final Answer

**Theoretical forging force ≈ 1.1 MN (110 tonnes-force)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Hazard Implication:** A forging press developing 110 tonnes creates **catastrophic risk of entrapment or crush injury**. This justifies guarding per **ANSI B11.19** and light curtains per **EN ISO 13849**.

---

## 3.6 KTU Numerical Quick-Solve Pattern

For any **Hot Working Temperature** problem:

$$\boxed{T_{\text{hot}} \geq 0.6 \times T_m}$$

For any **Furnace Heat Load** problem:

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{\eta}}$$

For any **Casting Yield** problem:

$$\boxed{C_y(\%) = \frac{W_{\text{finished casting}}}{W_{\text{metal poured}}} \times 100}$$

For any **Forging Force** problem:

$$\boxed{P = k_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)}$$

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Foundry Operation Flow with Hazard Mapping


# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **"Classify engineering industries based on (a) raw material and (b) process type. Give two examples for each category."**

### 📝 Model Answer (3 Marks)

**(a) Based on Raw Material (2 marks):**

- **Ferrous Industries** — Industries dealing with iron and steel as primary raw materials.
  - *Examples*: Integrated steel plants (SAIL, Tata Steel), Iron foundries
- **Non-Ferrous Industries** — Industries dealing with metals other than iron.
  - *Examples*: Aluminum smelters (NALCO, Hindalco), Copper refineries

**(b) Based on Process Type (1 mark):**

- **Primary Processing** — Smelting, refining of ores
- **Secondary Processing** — Casting, forging, rolling, machining
- **Tertiary Processing** — Assembly, surface finishing

**Mark Distribution:**
- Classification explained — 2 marks
- Examples — 1 mark

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **"Differentiate between hot working and cold working processes. Give two advantages of hot working."**

### 📝 Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Parameter | Hot Working | Cold Working |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Above recrystallization temp | Below recrystallization temp |
| Strength | Lower flow stress | Higher flow stress |
| Ductility | Higher | Lower |
| Surface finish | Poor (oxide scale) | Good |
| Work hardening | Absent | Present |

**Two Advantages of Hot Working (2 marks):**

1. **Lower deformation forces** — Yield strength drops at high temperature, reducing energy consumption
2. **Greater ductility** — Allows large deformations without cracking (e.g., rolling a 200 mm thick ingot to 2 mm sheet)
3. **No residual work hardening** — Material remains soft for further processing

**Mark Distribution:**
- Tabular comparison — 1 mark
- Advantages (any two) — 2 marks

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of manufacturing processes** with neat sketches/diagrams. Discuss the role of each process family with **suitable engineering examples**.
> 
> **(b)** With the help of a **flow diagram**, describe the **complete foundry operation** from pattern making to final inspection. List **at least five major hazards** encountered in a foundry and state the relevant **safety control measures** for each.

### 📝 Part (a) Model Solution — 7 Marks

Manufacturing processes are classified into **four major families**:

**1. Primary Forming Processes (Casting & Bulk Deformation)**
- **Casting** — Producing shapes by pouring molten metal into molds
  - Examples: Sand casting of machine beds; Investment casting of turbine blades; Die casting of aluminum cylinder heads
- **Bulk Deformation** — Shape change through plastic deformation
  - Examples: Forging of crankshafts; Hot rolling of steel plates; Extrusion of aluminum window frames; Wire drawing of electrical cables

**2. Material Removal Processes (Machining)**
- Conventional: Turning, Milling, Drilling, Grinding, Boring
- Non-conventional: EDM, Laser cutting, Water jet, Abrasive jet
- **Example**: CNC turning of a precision shaft for a centrifugal pump

**3. Joining Processes**
- Welding: Arc, Gas, Resistance, Friction stir
- Mechanical: Riveting, Bolting
- **Example**: Submerged arc welding of pressure vessel shells in petrochemical industry

**4. Surface Treatment & Finishing**
- Coating, Plating, Painting, Anodizing, Powder coating
- **Example**: Galvanizing of structural steel to prevent corrosion

**Closing Statement (1 mark):**
Each family has distinct **hazard profiles** — casting has thermal/dust hazards, machining has mechanical hazards, welding has radiation/fume hazards, and surface treatment has chemical hazards. The selection depends on **material, design, production volume, and acceptable hazard level**.

---

### 📝 Part (b) Model Solution — 7 Marks

### Foundry Operation Flow:

```
Pattern Making → Mold Preparation → Core Making → Melting →
Pouring → Solidification → Shakeout → Fettling → Inspection → Dispatch
```

### Step-by-Step Explanation:

1. **Pattern Making** — Wooden/metal pattern of the casting is fabricated (allowance for shrinkage)
2. **Mold Making** — Silica sand + bentonite + water is compacted around the pattern
3. **Core Making** — Sand cores are placed inside the mold to create internal cavities
4. **Melting** — Metal is melted in a furnace (Cupola / Induction / Arc) to 1400–1600 °C
5. **Pouring** — Molten metal is transferred via ladles into the mold cavity
6. **Solidification** — Metal cools and solidifies inside the mold
7. **Shakeout** — The casting is separated from the sand mold
8. **Fettling** — Excess metal (risers, runners, gates) is cut/ground off
9. **Inspection** — Visual, dimensional, NDT (radiography, ultrasonic) checks
10. **Dispatch** — Quality-passed castings are sent for further processing

### Five Major Foundry Hazards & Controls:

| # | Hazard | Source | Control Measure |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Burns** | Molten metal splashes at 1500 °C | Aluminized PPE, face shields, aprons; ladle preheating |
| 2 | **Silicosis** | Respirable crystalline silica dust | Wet methods, local exhaust ventilation, respirators |
| 3 | **Fume inhalation** | CO, SO₂ from furnaces | Stack monitoring, fume extraction, gas detectors |
| 4 | **Explosion** | Molten metal + water contact | Dry molds, no water near ladles, spark arrestors |
| 5 | **Noise & Vibration** | Shakeout, grinders, hammers | Acoustic enclosures, hearing PPE, isolation |

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

> **(a)** With neat diagrams, classify **furnaces** used in industry based on **heat source** and **application**. Explain the working principle of a **cupola furnace** with a labeled diagram. List **five hazards** of furnace operation.
>
> **(b)** Define **hot working**. Derive the condition for hot working using the **homologous temperature concept**. A steel billet with melting point **1500 °C** is to be hot forged. Calculate the **minimum hot working temperature**. Take the hot working threshold as **$T_H \geq 0.6$**.

### 📝 Part (a) Model Solution — 7 Marks

### Furnace Classification:

**By Heat Source:**

**Fuel-Fired Furnaces:**
- **Cupola Furnace** — Vertical shaft furnace using coke + limestone; used for melting cast iron
- **Reverberatory Furnace** — Flame reflected off roof onto metal bath; non-ferrous melting
- **Crucible Furnace** — Metal held in crucible; small foundries
- **Open Hearth Furnace** — Shallow hearth, regenerative system; bulk steel
- **Rotary Furnace** — Tiltable cylinder; aluminum melting

**Electric Furnaces:**
- **Arc Furnace (EAF)** — Three-phase graphite electrodes; scrap steel melting
- **Induction Furnace** — Coil induces eddy currents; alloys, stainless steel
- **Resistance Furnace** — Heating coils; heat treatment
- **Vacuum Furnace** — Used for titanium, superalloys

**By Application:**
- **Melting Furnace** — Converts solid to liquid
- **Reheating Furnace** — Heats solid metal to plastic state
- **Heat Treatment Furnace** — Annealing, normalizing, hardening
- **Holding Furnace** — Maintains molten metal temperature

### Cupola Furnace — Working Principle:

The cupola is a **vertical cylindrical shaft furnace** lined with refractory bricks. Alternate layers of **coke (fuel), limestone (flux), and metal charge** are loaded from the top. **Blast air** is introduced through tuyeres near the bottom.

**Zones (top to bottom):**
1. **Charging zone** — Top, materials added
2. **Preheating zone** — Charge descends, gets heated by rising hot gases
3. **Melting zone** — Above tuyeres, metal melts (~ 1400–1500 °C)
4. **Combustion zone** — At tuyere level, coke burns with air blast
5. **Well/Tapping zone** — Bottom, molten metal collects and is tapped periodically

**Slag** floats on top and is tapped separately.

### Five Furnace Hazards:

1. **Fire/Explosion** — Fuel leak + ignition
2. **Burns** — From molten metal, hot surfaces, radiant heat
3. **CO poisoning** — Incomplete combustion in fuel-fired furnaces
4. **Electric shock/Arc flash** — In EAF and induction furnaces
5. **Water/molten metal explosion** — If water leaks into molten bath

**Control measures:**
- Flame scanners + fuel cutoff (NFPA 86)
- PPE (aluminized suits, face shields)
- CO monitors with alarms
- Earth fault protection (for electric)
- Dry storage of charge materials

---

### 📝 Part (b) Model Solution — 7 Marks

### Definition of Hot Working (1 mark):

> **Hot Working** is a metal forming process in which plastic deformation of the metal is carried out at a temperature **above the recrystallization temperature**, so that the metal undergoes dynamic recrystallization and does not strain-harden.

### Derivation of Hot Working Condition (3 marks):

**Step 1**: Define homologous temperature.

$$T_H = \frac{T_{\text{working}}}{T_m}$$

where $T_m$ is the melting temperature (in Kelvin).

**Step 2**: State the empirical rule for hot working.

For most metals, hot working occurs when:
$$T_H \geq 0.6$$
(For non-ferrous metals, $T_H \geq 0.5$)

**Step 3**: Rearrange to find minimum working temperature.

$$T_{\text{working, min}} = 0.6 \times T_m$$

**Step 4**: Convert to °C if needed.

$$T_{\text{working, min}}(°C) = 0.6 \times (T_m + 273) - 273$$

### Numerical Calculation (3 marks):

**Given:**
- Melting point: $T_m = 1500\ °C = 1773\ K$
- Hot working threshold: $T_H = 0.6$

**Step 1**: Apply the hot working condition.

$$T_{\text{working, min}} = 0.6 \times T_m = 0.6 \times 1773\ K$$

**Step 2**: Compute.

$$T_{\text{working, min}} = 1063.8\ K$$

**Step 3**: Convert to Celsius.

$$T_{\text{working, min}}(°C) = 1063.8 - 273 = 790.8\ °C$$

### Final Answer:

> **Minimum hot working temperature ≈ 790.8 °C**
>
> In industrial practice, forging is done at **0.65 to 0.70 × Tₘ** ≈ **900–950 °C** for steel, providing safety margin and accounting for alloying elements and heat losses.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "hot working" with "hot machining"** — Hot working is a **forming** process (no chips produced), while hot machining is a **material removal** process. (Loss: 2 marks)
>
> 2. **Wrong unit in homologous temperature** — The formula $T_H = T_{working}/T_m$ **REQUIRES** both temperatures in **Kelvin**. Converting Celsius before dividing will give wrong answers. (Loss: 3 marks)
>
> 3. **Missing furnace hazards** — Students often list only **fire** and **burns**. You MUST mention **CO poisoning, arc flash, dust explosion, and molten metal–water explosion** for full marks.
>
> 4. **No diagram in Part B questions** — KTU examiners explicitly ask for "neat diagram/sketch." A missing diagram = **2–3 marks deducted**.
>
> 5. **Casting yield formula confusion** — Yield is on the basis of **metal poured**, not the **total metal charged** (which includes the charge itself, not additions).
>
> 6. **Furnace efficiency ≠ Combustion efficiency** — Furnace efficiency includes stack losses, wall losses, and load losses; combustion efficiency considers only the burner side.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> **MUST-KNOW POINTS — KTU PCSFT601 Module 1**

### ✅ Definitions to Memorize

- ☐ **Engineering Industry** — establishment transforming raw material to finished goods via manufacturing
- ☐ **Manufacturing Process** — sequence of operations to alter geometry/properties of material
- ☐ **Hot Working** — deformation above recrystallization temperature ($T_H \geq 0.6$)
- ☐ **Foundry** — facility for casting metals via molten metal pouring into molds
- ☐ **Furnace** — enclosed chamber for generating and maintaining high temperatures

### ✅ Classifications to Remember

- ☐ Engineering Industry → By product, material, process, volume
- ☐ Manufacturing → Primary, Secondary (Material Removal), Joining, Surface Treatment
- ☐ Furnace → By heat source (Fuel-fired/Electric), Application, Atmosphere, Operation Mode

### ✅ Numerical Formulas (High-Yield for ESE)

- ☐ $T_{\text{hot,min}} = 0.6 \times T_m$ (Kelvin!)
- ☐ $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$
- ☐ $C_y = (W_{\text{casting}} / W_{\text{poured}}) \times 100$
- ☐ $\eta = (Q_{\text{useful}} / Q_{\text{fuel}}) \times 100$
- ☐ $P_{\text{forge}} = k_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \ln(A_0/A_f)$

### ✅ Critical Hazards (Foundry & Furnace)

- ☐ Burns (molten metal at 1500 °C)
- ☐ Silicosis (silica dust — Class 1A carcinogen)
- ☐ CO poisoning (fuel-fired furnaces)
- ☐ Arc flash (electric furnaces)

### ✅ Standards to Cite

topics.title: Health Hazard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT601 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Health Hazard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Health Hazard** is any chemical, physical, biological, ergonomic, or psychosocial agent, condition, or situation present in, arising from, or characteristic of the workplace that has the potential to cause illness, disease, impairment, or adverse health effects (acute or chronic) in exposed workers or the surrounding community. Under the KTU 2024 framework and the broader **Factories Act 1948 / OSHA / WHO Occupational Health** doctrine, a health hazard is identified by its inherent **toxicity (hazard)** combined with the **route, magnitude, frequency, and duration of exposure** (risk realization).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Core Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> *"A Health Hazard is any source, agent, or condition in the workplace capable of causing adverse systemic, local, reversible, or irreversible biological effects on human health through inhalation, ingestion, dermal absorption, or direct contact."*

The two governing variables are:

$$\text{Health Impact} \;=\; f(\text{Toxicity of Agent}, \text{Exposure Dose})$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Cup" Model

Imagine holding a cup of hot coffee in your office:

- **The coffee itself = the Hazard.** The substance inherently has the property of being hot (toxic). It doesn't care whether anyone is drinking it.
- **Your lips touching the cup = Exposure.** Until contact occurs, the hazard is dormant.
- **The burn you get = the Health Effect.** It happens only when hazard AND exposure converge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A substance in a sealed drum is a *potential* hazard. The same substance spilled on the floor with workers nearby is an *active* health hazard. **No exposure = No effect**, even for highly toxic materials like cyanide or asbestos. This distinction is the single most-tested concept in KTU valuation scripts.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot (High-Yield for Module 1)

| Category | Examples | Typical Industry |
|----------|----------|------------------|
| **Chemical** | Lead, Benzene, Asbestos, Silica, Mercury | Petrochemical, mining, painting |
| **Physical** | Noise, Vibration, Radiation, Heat, Cold | Heavy engineering, foundries, nuclear |
| **Biological** | Bacteria, Viruses, Fungi, Bioaerosols | Hospitals, food processing, sewage |
| **Ergonomic** | Repetitive motion, Awkward posture, Heavy lifting | Manufacturing, IT, construction |
| **Psychosocial** | Stress, Fatigue, Workplace violence | All sectors |

## 1.4 Standardized Reference Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **TLV-TWA (Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average):** **8 hours/day, 40 hours/week** weighted exposure.
- **PEL (Permissible Exposure Limit):** OSHA-enforceable legal limit.
- **REL (Recommended Exposure Limit):** NIOSH recommendation.
- **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health):** **30-minute** escape threshold.
- **LC₅₀ / LD₅₀:** Lethal concentration/dose for **50%** of test population.
- **Reference Unit for Noise:** **dB SPL** (Sound Pressure Level).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Health Hazard Realization

For a health hazard to materialize into disease, **ALL FOUR** conditions must be simultaneously satisfied. KTU examiners love testing whether students recognize missing pillars.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 4-Pillar Rule of Occupational Disease:**
> 1. **Source** — the hazardous agent must exist (e.g., solvent tank).
> 2. **Pathway** — a route of entry must exist (e.g., inhalation).
> 3. **Receptor** — a worker must be present at the point of exposure.
> 4. **Dose/Time** — exposure must exceed the toxicological threshold for a sufficient duration.

If **any one pillar collapses, no disease occurs.** This is the theoretical foundation of industrial hygiene control.

## 2.2 Routes of Entry — The "ABS-I" Mnemonic

| Mnemonic | Route | Industrial Examples | Defence Mechanism |
|----------|-------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **A** — Absorption | Skin/eye contact with chemicals | Pesticides, solvents, acids | PPE, gloves, aprons |
| **B** — Inhalation | Breathing contaminated air | Dusts, fumes, vapours, gases | Ventilation, respirators |
| **S** — Skin/Eye contact | Direct splash or immersion | Corrosives, caustics | Goggles, face shields |
| **I** — Ingestion | Hand-to-mouth transfer | Lead dust, contaminated food | Hygiene, washing facilities |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inhalation accounts for ≈ 90% of all occupational exposure** — this is the highest-yield statistic for KTU Module 1.

## 2.3 Acute vs. Chronic Health Effects (High-Frequency KTU Question)

- **Acute Effects:** Manifest within **seconds to days** of a single (or short-term) high-dose exposure. Examples: solvent narcosis, ammonia burns, CO poisoning.
- **Chronic Effects:** Manifest after **months to years** of repeated low-dose exposure. Examples: asbestosis, silicosis, lead neuropathy, benzene-induced leukemia.

$$\text{Latent Period: } T_{\text{latency}} = t_{\text{manifestation}} - t_{\text{exposure-onset}}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Dose Definition | $D = C \cdot t$ | ppm·hr or mg/m³·hr | Exposure quantification |
| 2 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | dB SPL | Noise hazard measurement |
| 3 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | dB SIL | Acoustic energy ratio |
| 4 | Noise Dose (OSHA) | $D\% = 100 \cdot \dfrac{C_1}{T_1} + 100 \cdot \dfrac{C_2}{T_2} + \cdots$ | % | Daily noise exposure |
| 5 | Allowable Exposure Time | $T = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}$ | hours | OSHA permissible duration |
| 6 | Risk Realization | $R = H \times E$ | dimensionless | Hazard × Exposure |
| 7 | Ventilation (Dilution) | $Q = \dfrac{G}{C_{\text{TLV}} - C_{\text{bg}}}$ | m³/s | Local exhaust design |
| 8 | Doubling Rule (Noise) | $\Delta L = +3\ \text{dB} \Rightarrow$ time halves | — | Hearing conservation |
| 9 | Heat Stress (WBGT) | $\text{WBGT} = 0.7\,T_{\text{nwb}} + 0.3\,T_{\text{g}}$ | °C | Outdoor heat exposure |
| 10 | Dose-Response (Haber's Law) | $C \cdot t = k$ (constant effect) | varies | Toxicological constant-effect |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Utility:** Formula 7 is the bread-and-butter of industrial ventilation design — it allows practitioners to size exhaust hoods for benzene, toluene, or welding fume booths. Formula 5 is tested annually in Part B (14-mark) questions across most KTU clusters.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical Process Plants:** Use PEL/TLV tables to design closed-loop reactors.
- **Construction:** Apply WBGT to schedule outdoor work shifts.
- **Foundries:** Calculate dilution ventilation Q to keep silica below **0.025 mg/m³** (NIOSH REL).
- **Hospitals:** Use biological hazard matrices for bio-safety cabinets (BSL-1 to BSL-4).
- **Manufacturing:** Apply ergonomic NIOSH Lifting Equation to prevent back injuries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sound Pressure Level (SPL) from Physical Pressure

The human ear responds to acoustic pressure logarithmically. We define SPL relative to the threshold of hearing $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}$.

**Step 1:** Take the ratio of measured pressure $p$ to reference pressure $p_0$.

$$\frac{p}{p_0}$$

**Step 2:** Apply logarithm base-10 (since decibels are base-10).

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by 20 (because pressure-squared gives intensity, hence factor 2 × 10 = 20).

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)\ \text{dB SPL}$$

**Step 4:** Worked example — A grinding wheel produces $p = 0.2\ \text{Pa}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_p &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right)\ \text{dB} \\
&= 20 \log_{10}(10{,}000) \\
&= 20 \times 4 \\
&= 80\ \text{dB SPL}
\end{aligned}$$

This falls **below** the OSHA PEL of **90 dB (8-hr TWA)** but exceeds the NIOSH REL of **85 dB**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — OSHA Allowable Exposure Time

OSHA's Table G-16a follows the **5 dB exchange rate** with a **90 dB criterion**. The reference dose is 8 hours at 90 dB.

**Step 1:** Express the dose equivalence requirement.

$$D = \frac{C}{T} \cdot 100\% \quad \text{(limit: } D \le 100\%\text{)}$$

**Step 2:** For constant level $L$ dB, the allowed time $T$ in hours is governed by:

$$T = \frac{8}{2^{(L - 90)/5}}$$

**Step 3:** Derivation logic — Each 5 dB increase corresponds to a doubling of acoustic energy, so permissible time is halved. Hence the exponent $\dfrac{L - 90}{5}$ controls the halving.

**Step 4:** Worked example — A worker's exposure is **100 dB**.

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{8}{2^{(100 - 90)/5}} \\
&= \frac{8}{2^{10/5}} \\
&= \frac{8}{2^2} \\
&= \frac{8}{4} \\
&= 2\ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}$$

The worker may legally remain at 100 dB for **only 2 hours** in an 8-hour shift. KTU examiners frequently award full marks only if the student writes the *unit* (hours) and *states the OSHA criterion (90 dB)* explicitly.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Noise Dose Percentage (Multi-Source)

A welder is exposed to three operations during an 8-hour shift.

| Operation | Level (dB) | Duration (hr) | OSHA Criterion Time $T_i$ |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|----------------------------|
| Arc welding | 95 | 2 | 4.0 |
| Grinding | 90 | 3 | 8.0 |
| Office break | 75 | 3 | (negligible) |

**Step 1:** Identify OSHA reference times from Table G-16a.

- At 95 dB → $T_1 = 4\ \text{hr}$
- At 90 dB → $T_2 = 8\ \text{hr}$

**Step 2:** Compute partial dose contributions.

$$\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= 100 \cdot \frac{C_1}{T_1} = 100 \cdot \frac{2}{4.0} = 50\% \\
D_2 &= 100 \cdot \frac{C_2}{T_2} = 100 \cdot \frac{3}{8.0} = 37.5\% \\
D_3 &\approx 0 \quad (\text{below 80 dB criterion})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Sum partial doses.

$$D_{\text{total}} = D_1 + D_2 + D_3 = 50\% + 37.5\% + 0\% = 87.5\%$$

**Step 4:** Interpret — Since $D_{\text{total}} < 100\%$, the exposure **complies with OSHA** but exceeds the NIOSH **85 dB / 8-hr** recommendation, requiring inclusion in the Hearing Conservation Program (HCP).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of PPE by Route

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk Level} \rightarrow \text{PPE Hierarchy:} \quad &\text{Hazard} \rightarrow \text{Engineering Control} \rightarrow \text{Admin Control} \rightarrow \text{PPE} \\
\end{aligned}$$

| Hazard Class | First-Line Engineering Control | Administrative Control | PPE (Last Resort) |
|--------------|--------------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Vapours (e.g., Toluene)** | Closed system, LEV | Job rotation, training | Half-face APR with organic cartridge |
| **Dusts (e.g., Silica)** | Wet suppression, enclosure | Housekeeping, medical surveillance | N95 / P100 respirator |
| **Noise > 90 dB** | Sound-dampening enclosures | Shift scheduling | Earplugs + earmuffs (dual protection) |
| **Heat Stress** | Air-conditioning, shade | Hydration, rest cycles | Cooling vests |
| **Biological (e.g., TB)** | Negative-pressure isolation | Vaccination, training | N95 respirator, gloves, gown |

## 3.5 Numerical Verification — Haber's Law Application

**Problem:** A worker is exposed to **50 ppm** of ammonia for **4 hours**. The TLV-TWA is **25 ppm (8 hr)**. Determine compliance.

**Step 1:** Compute equivalent dose for 8-hr TWA normalization.

$$E_{\text{TWA}} = \frac{C \cdot t}{8\ \text{hr}} = \frac{50 \times 4}{8} = 25\ \text{ppm}$$

**Step 2:** Compare with TLV.

$$E_{\text{TWA}} = 25\ \text{ppm} = \text{TLV-TWA} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Borderline compliance (Action Level reached)}$$

**Step 3:** Industrial-hygiene decision — Since $E_{\text{TWA}}$ equals TLV, OSHA's **Action Level (½ TLV = 12.5 ppm)** is exceeded. The employer must initiate **medical surveillance and exposure monitoring**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Health Hazards — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["HEALTH HAZARD ROOT"]:::root --> B["CHEMICAL"]
    A --> C["PHYSICAL"]
    A --> D["BIOLOGICAL"]
    A --> E["ERGONOMIC"]
    A --> F["PSYCHOSOCIAL"]

    B --> B1["Dusts and Particulates"]
    B --> B2["Vapours and Gases"]
    B --> B3["Fumes and Mists"]
    B --> B4["Liquids and Solvents"]

    C --> C1["Noise"]
    C --> C2["Vibration"]
    C --> C3["Ionising Radiation"]
    C --> C4["Non-Ionising Radiation"]
    C --> C5["Thermal Extremes"]

    D --> D1["Bacteria and Viruses"]
    D --> D2["Fungi and Moulds"]
    D --> D3["Bloodborne Pathogens"]
    D --> D4["Bioaerosols"]

    E --> E1["Repetitive Motion"]
    E --> E2["Postural Strain"]
    E --> E3["Manual Handling"]

    F --> F1["Workplace Stress"]
    F --> F2["Fatigue and Burnout"]
    F --> F3["Violence and Harassment"]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Routes of Exposure — Inhalation-Centric Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["HAZARD SOURCE"]:::src --> E["EMISSION POINT"]
    E --> P["PATHWAY"]
    P --> R["RECEPTOR WORKER"]
    R --> H["HEALTH EFFECT"]

    P --> P1["Inhalation 90 percent"]
    P --> P2["Skin Absorption 6 percent"]
    P --> P3["Ingestion 3 percent"]
    P --> P4["Injection 1 percent"]

    R --> R1["Acute Reversible"]
    R --> R2["Chronic Irreversible"]
    R --> R3["Carcinogenic"]
    R --> R4["Reproductive Toxicity"]

    H --> H1["Occupational Disease"]
    H --> H2["Permanent Disability"]
    H --> H3["Fatality"]

    classDef src fill:#8b0000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Controls Applied to Health Hazards

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0["ELIMINATION"]:::lvl1 --> L1["SUBSTITUTION"]:::lvl2
    L1 --> L2["ENGINEERING CONTROLS"]:::lvl3
    L2 --> L3["ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS"]:::lvl4
    L3 --> L4["PPE"]:::lvl5

    L0 -.- L0N["Remove the hazard entirely"]
    L1 -.- L1N["Replace with less toxic material"]
    L2 -.- L2N["Enclosures, LEV, automation"]
    L3 -.- L3N["Training, rotation, signage"]
    L4 -.- L4N["Respirators, gloves, ear plugs"]

    classDef lvl1 fill:#14532d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef lvl2 fill:#166534,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef lvl3 fill:#15803d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef lvl4 fill:#65a30d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef lvl5 fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy Order (Most Effective → Least Effective):** Elimination > Substitution > Engineering > Administrative > PPE. KTU Module 1 Part B answers that invert this order automatically lose **2 to 3 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define a *Health Hazard*. Differentiate between *Hazard* and *Risk* with one industrial example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Health Hazard** is any agent, condition, or situation in the workplace with the inherent potential to cause adverse health effects on exposed personnel.

| Term | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Hazard** | Inherent property to cause harm | Benzene is carcinogenic (property) |
| **Risk** | Probability × consequence given exposure | A worker inhaling 10 ppm benzene for 5 years |

Hazard is intrinsic; **Risk** depends on exposure scenario. *(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 differentiation + 1 example)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** List the **four routes of entry** of chemical health hazards and state the most common route in industries.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Inhalation** — most common (**≈ 90%**) — via lungs into bloodstream.
2. **Skin Absorption** — through intact or broken dermis.
3. **Ingestion** — hand-to-mouth transfer.
4. **Injection** — via sharp objects or high-pressure equipment.

Industrial statistic cited. *(3 marks)*

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Module 1 Internal Choice Standard:** KTU 2024 ESE provides a *choice between two full 14-mark questions* from Module 1. Both are answered below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Classify the different types of **Health Hazards** encountered in industrial workplaces with **two examples** for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A worker is exposed to **100 dB** noise for **3 hours** and **95 dB** for **2 hours** in an 8-hour shift. Using the OSHA 5-dB exchange rule and 90 dB criterion, determine **(i)** the partial dose for each operation, **(ii)** the total daily dose, and **(iii)** whether the exposure complies with the OSHA PEL of 90 dB (8-hr TWA). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

| # | Hazard Type | Description | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|-------------|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | **Chemical** | Toxic substances | Lead (Pb) | Benzene |
| 2 | **Physical** | Energy forms | Noise | Ionising radiation |
| 3 | **Biological** | Living organisms | Mycobacterium TB | Legionella |
| 4 | **Ergonomic** | Workstation design | Repetitive wrist motion | Heavy lifting |
| 5 | **Psychosocial** | Work culture | Chronic stress | Workplace violence |

*(7 marks: 1 per category identification + 0.5 per example + 0.5 for description)*

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Given:** Level 1 = 100 dB, $C_1 = 3$ hr; Level 2 = 95 dB, $C_2 = 2$ hr.

**Step 1 — Identify OSHA Reference Times from Table G-16a:**

- 100 dB → $T_1 = 2\ \text{hr}$
- 95 dB → $T_2 = 4\ \text{hr}$

**Step 2 — Compute Partial Doses:**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= 100 \cdot \frac{C_1}{T_1} = 100 \cdot \frac{3}{2} = 150\% \\
D_2 &= 100 \cdot \frac{C_2}{T_2} = 100 \cdot \frac{2}{4} = 50\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total Daily Dose:**

$$D_{\text{total}} = D_1 + D_2 = 150\% + 50\% = 200\%$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent 8-hr TWA (using OSHA formula):**

$$\begin{aligned}
TWA &= 16.61 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{D}{100}\right) + 90 \\
    &= 16.61 \cdot \log_{10}(2.0) + 90 \\
    &= 16.61 \cdot 0.3010 + 90 \\
    &= 5.0 + 90 = 95\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compliance Verdict:**

Since $TWA = 95\ \text{dB} > 90\ \text{dB (OSHA PEL)}$, the exposure **does NOT comply**. Mandatory engineering or administrative controls required. *(7 marks: 1 step-1, 2 step-2, 1 step-3, 1 step-4, 2 step-5)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of Threshold Limit Value (TLV)**. Discuss its three variants — **TLV-TWA, TLV-STEL, and TLV-C** — with reference to a chemical example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **ACGIH / OSHA / NIOSH classification of carcinogens** for occupational exposures. Cite **two examples** of confirmed human carcinogens and the protective measures prescribed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Threshold Limit Value (TLV)** = airborne concentration of a chemical below which **nearly all workers** may be repeatedly exposed **day after day** without adverse health effects. (Defined by **ACGIH**, not legally binding.)

| Variant | Definition | Time Basis | Example (Toluene) |
|---------|------------|------------|-------------------|
| **TLV-TWA** | Time-Weighted Average | 8 hr/day | 20 ppm |
| **TLV-STEL** | Short-Term Exposure Limit | 15-min, max 4×/day, ≥ 60 min apart | — |
| **TLV-C** | Ceiling Limit | Instantaneous, never to exceed | 75 ppm for some amines |

*(7 marks: 2 TLV definition + 5 variants with example)*

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Classification Hierarchy of Carcinogens:**

| Authority | Category A1 / Group 1 | Category A2 / Group 2 | Category A3 / Group 3 | Category A4 / Group 4 | Category A5 / Group 5 |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **ACGIH** | Confirmed Human | Suspected Human | Animal Only | Not Classifiable | Not Suspected |
| **IARC** | Group 1 | Group 2A | Group 2B | Group 3 | Group 4 |
| **OSHA** | Regulated | — | — | — | — |
| **NIOSH** | Ca | — | — | — | — |

**Two Confirmed Human Carcinogens (IARC Group 1):**

1. **Benzene** — causes **acute myeloid leukemia (AML)**. PEL = **1 ppm (8-hr TWA)**. Protective measures: closed-loop processes, LEV, air-supplied respirators, biological monitoring (S-phenylmercapturic acid in urine).
2. **Asbestos** — causes **mesothelioma, asbestosis, lung cancer**. PEL = **0.1 f/cc (8-hr TWA)**. Protective measures: wet cutting, sealed enclosures, full Tyvek suits with PAPRs, pre-employment medical surveillance.

**Protective Measures Hierarchy:**

$$\text{Elimination} \rightarrow \text{Substitution} \rightarrow \text{Engineering} \rightarrow \text{Admin} \rightarrow \text{PPE}$$

*(7 marks: 2 classification matrix + 2 examples + 1 measures hierarchy + 2 numerical PEL values)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on Health Hazard questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Hazard with Risk** — Examiners deduct **1 full mark** every time these terms are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Inverting the Hierarchy of Controls** — Writing PPE *before* engineering control costs **2 marks** outright.
> 3. **Forgetting the OSHA Criterion (90 dB) and Exchange Rate (5 dB)** in noise problems — these are *expected* to be stated, not implied.
> 4. **Quoting TLV values without units (ppm or mg/m³)** — KTU penalises unitless answers by **0.5 mark each**.
> 5. **Omitting routes-of-entry ranking** — stating the four routes is *insufficient*; the **90% inhalation statistic** is the syllabus-mandated emphasis.
> 6. **In chronic-vs-acute questions**, students often fail to mention the **latency period** — KTU 2024 specifically tests for it.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Hazard ≠ Risk.** Hazard = inherent property; Risk = probability of harm given exposure.
- ✅ The **4 pillars** of occupational disease: Source, Pathway, Receptor, Dose/Time.
- ✅ **Inhalation is the dominant route (≈ 90%)** of industrial exposure.
- ✅ **TLV-TWA = 8 hr/day, 40 hr/wk** reference; **TLV-STEL = 15 min**; **TLV-C = instantaneous**.
- ✅ **OSHA noise criterion:** 90 dB, 5 dB exchange rate, 8-hr PEL.
- ✅ **NIOSH noise criterion:** 85 dB, 3 dB exchange rate (more conservative).
- ✅ **Doubling rule:** +3 dB → acoustic energy doubles, allowable time halves.
- ✅ Hierarchy of Controls: **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE** (most to least effective).
- ✅ **IARC Group 1** = confirmed human carcinogen (e.g., Benzene, Asbestos, Formaldehyde).
- ✅ **Acute effects = short-term, high dose, reversible**; **Chronic = long-term, low dose, often irreversible**.
- ✅ **Heat stress formula:** WBGT = $0.7\,T_{\text{nwb}} + 0.3\,T_g$.
- ✅ **Risk equation:** $R = H \times E$ — control exposure $E$ when hazard $H$ cannot be eliminated.
- ✅ **Industrial hygiene mantra:** *"Control at source, not at the worker."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safe Methods of Operation - Forging Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT601 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Safe Methods of Operation - Forging Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Forging Operations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forging** is a metal-forming manufacturing process in which compressive forces are applied to a heated (or cold) metal workpiece (billet, ingot, or blank) using dies, hammers, or presses to plastically deform it into the desired geometric shape, while preserving the grain-flow continuity of the parent metal and producing superior mechanical properties compared to cast or machined components.

**Classification Basis (KTU Syllabus Taxonomy):**

| Forging Category | Operating Temperature | Force Application | Typical Products |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hot Forging** | Above recrystallization temperature (~0.6 × T<sub>melt</sub>) | Hammer / Hydraulic Press | Crankshafts, connecting rods |
| **Warm Forging** | Between 0.3 × T<sub>melt</sub> and recrystallization | Mechanical / Screw Press | Automotive stub axles |
| **Cold Forging** | Ambient temperature | High-speed mechanical press | Bolts, fasteners, splined shafts |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Clay on the Anvil" Intuition

Imagine you have a lump of **warm clay** sitting on a wooden block. You hit it with a flat wooden mallet. The clay **spreads out**, flattens, and takes the shape of the block underneath. If you press a shaped cookie-cutter against the clay, the clay squeezes into the cutter's cavity and emerges as a shaped piece.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forging is metallurgy's "clay-mallet" process**: heat softens the metal (making it pliable like clay), and the hammer/press does what the mallet does. **Safety is the "kitchen gloves"** — you need protection from heat, flying scale, loud noise, and crushing impact that the process inherently generates.

**Why forging is hazardous (layperson view):** Imagine standing next to a giant hammer slamming down 60–120 times per minute on glowing red-hot steel that throws sparks and small flakes of oxidized metal (called *scale*) into the air. Every second the worker is exposed to:
- **Intense heat** (radiation, hot dies, hot workpieces)
- **Flying hot scale particles** (like sparks)
- **Loud impact noise** (often > **100 dB(A)**)
- **Crushing force** (between hammer and anvil)
- **Burns** from contact with hot tooling
- **Vibration** transmitted through tools

## 1.3 Why Forging Demands Dedicated Safety Engineering

Forging plants historically have among the **highest injury rates** in the metalworking sector. Reported statistics from OSHA-style industrial audits typically show:
- **Burn injuries**: ~**30–35%** of all forging-related incidents
- **Lacerations / contusions**: ~**20–25%** (from flying scale, swinging tools)
- **Musculoskeletal disorders**: ~**15–20%** (from hammer vibration, repetitive loading)
- **Noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL)**: chronic, cumulative exposure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Forging is classified under **Schedule-I Hazardous Process** in the **Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC)** framework of India and carries **mandatory audit obligations** under the Factories Act, 1948.

## 1.4 Hazard Taxonomy in Forging (Foundation Concept)

A hazard is any source of potential damage, harm, or adverse health effects. In forging, hazards fall into **five universal categories**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Forging Hazard Classes (KTU Module 1 Anchor):**
> 1. **Mechanical Hazards** — moving dies, flying scale, trapping points
> 2. **Thermal Hazards** — heat stress, burns, ignition of lubricants
> 3. **Chemical Hazards** — fumes from heated lubricants, metal oxide dust
> 4. **Physical Hazards** — noise, vibration, radiation (IR/UV from hot stock)
> 5. **Ergonomic Hazards** — repetitive motion, manual handling, awkward postures

This five-class structure mirrors the **ISO 12100:2010** machinery safety standard and is the foundation for every safety control discussed in this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Forging Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

A typical forging sequence proceeds in **eight controlled stages**, each with its own hazard profile:

1. **Stock Cutting / Shearing** — Billets are cut to length on a shear or saw.
   *Hazard:* sharp edges, flying chips, pinch points on shear blade.
2. **Heating** — Stock is heated in a furnace (gas/oil/electric/induction) to forging temperature.
   *Hazard:* furnace explosion, CO/NO<sub>x</sub> fumes, fire from oil leaks, burns.
3. **Transfer to Die** — Hot stock is moved (manually with tongs or robotically) to the forging die.
   *Hazard:* drop of hot billet, burn from radiant heat, trip hazards.
4. **Deformation (Hammering / Pressing)** — Plastic deformation occurs under compressive load.
   *Hazard:* crushing, flying scale, die fragments, noise (> 110 dB(A)).
5. **Scale Removal / Descaling** — Oxide scale is blown off by steam/air or brushed.
   *Hazard:* flying hot particles, high-pressure air injection injuries.
6. **Trimming / Piercing** — Flash is trimmed and holes pierced.
   *Hazard:* punch fracture, trim press pinch points.
7. **Cooling** — Forged part cools (still air, controlled furnace, water spray).
   *Hazard:* thermal shock, steam burns, oil fires.
8. **Inspection & Finishing** — Dimensional checks, grinding, shot blasting.
   *Hazard:* grinding dust, explosion risk in dust collectors.

## 2.2 Forging Force and Energy — The Core Engineering Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KTU Board Examiner's mantra**: *Forging safety begins with understanding the magnitude of force your machine can unleash.* A worker who respects a 10 MN press never puts a hand where it shouldn't be.

**Generic Forging Force Equation (open-die upset forging baseline):**

$$F = K_f \cdot A \cdot \sigma_{flow}$$

Where:
- $F$ = forging force in **N (Newtons)**
- $K_f$ = forging shape / friction factor (dimensionless, $K_f \ge 1$)
- $A$ = projected contact area in **mm²**
- $\sigma_{flow}$ = material flow stress at forging temperature in **MPa**

**Forging Energy Requirement (single blow):**

$$E = V \cdot \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$$

Where:
- $E$ = deformation energy in **J (Joules)**
- $V$ = deformed volume in **mm³**
- $\bar{\sigma}_f$ = average flow stress in **MPa**
- $h_0$, $h_f$ = initial and final heights in **mm**

**Hammer Kinetic Energy (impact forging):**

$$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m_{ram} \cdot v_{ram}^{2}$$

Where $m_{ram}$ is the falling-ram mass (kg) and $v_{ram}$ is the impact velocity (m/s).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** A **1-tonne drop hammer** (m<sub>ram</sub> ≈ 1000 kg) falling from 2 m gives $E_{kinetic} \approx 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 2 \approx \mathbf{19,620 \text{ J}}$. That same hammer dropped on a human body would be instantly fatal — *this single calculation is why safety guards are non-negotiable*.

## 2.3 Noise Level Estimation (Hammer Forging)

Hammer-forge noise level can be empirically estimated by:

$$L_p \approx L_0 + 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{E_{kinetic}}{E_{0}}\right) + K_{d}$$

Where:
- $L_p$ = sound pressure level in **dB(A)**
- $L_0$ = reference noise level (**90 dB(A)** for typical hammer)
- $E_0$ = reference energy (**1000 J**)
- $K_d$ = distance attenuation factor in **dB**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forging force | $F = K_f \cdot A \cdot \sigma_{flow}$ | N | Press tonnage sizing |
| 2 | Hammer kinetic energy | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m_{ram} v_{ram}^2$ | J | Impact energy audit |
| 3 | Deformation work | $E = V \cdot \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot \ln(h_0/h_f)$ | J | Press energy sizing |
| 4 | Forging temperature | $T_f = 0.6 \cdot T_{melt}$ | °C / K | Heating control |
| 5 | Die stress | $\sigma_{die} = F / A_{die}$ | MPa | Die-life prediction |
| 6 | Hearing threshold exposure | $T_{safe} = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-85)/3}}$ | hours | OSHA-style noise dosing |
| 7 | Heat-stress index (WBGT) | $WBGT = 0.7 T_w + 0.3 T_g$ | °C | Worker exposure limit |
| 8 | PPE attenuation rating | $SNR = \text{Single Number Rating}$ | dB | Hearing protector sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember (KTU Board favorites):**
> - For steel: forging temperature ≈ **950–1250 °C** (above recrystallization ~**727 °C**).
> - Forge color for temperature reading: cherry-red ≈ **750 °C**, bright orange ≈ **1100 °C**, yellow-white ≈ **1300 °C**.
> - OSHA permissible noise exposure: **90 dB(A) for 8 hours**, halving allowable time for every **+5 dB** rise.

## 2.5 Hierarchy of Controls Applied to Forging (NIOSH / ISO 12100)

Engineering controls must follow the **Hierarchy of Controls** in strict order:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy Order (top = most effective):**
> 1. **Elimination** — Redesign to remove the forging step.
> 2. **Substitution** — Use cold forging instead of hot; use robotic handling instead of manual.
> 3. **Engineering Controls** — Guarding, ventilation, automation, noise enclosures.
> 4. **Administrative Controls** — Job rotation, training, signage, lockout/tagout.
> 5. **PPE** — Helmets, heat-resistant gloves, earplugs, face shields, aprons (last resort).

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that **PPE alone is never an acceptable primary control** in forging — it must be backed by engineering controls.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hammer Forging Energy to Noise Level Mapping

**Given:** A 1500 kg drop hammer falls through a stroke of 1.5 m. Find the impact energy and estimate the noise level at the operator station (3 m from the die).

**Step 1 — Compute the ram velocity at impact using kinematics.**

The ram starts from rest at the top of the stroke and free-falls under gravity:

$$v_{ram} = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot h}$$

Substituting $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ and $h = 1.5 \text{ m}$:

$$v_{ram} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1.5}$$

$$v_{ram} = \sqrt{29.43}$$

$$v_{ram} \approx 5.42 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the kinetic energy at impact.**

$$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m_{ram} \cdot v_{ram}^{2}$$

$$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1500 \cdot (5.42)^{2}$$

$$E_{kinetic} = 0.5 \cdot 1500 \cdot 29.3764$$

$$E_{kinetic} \approx 22{,}032 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the noise level using the empirical relation.**

$$L_p = L_0 + 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{E_{kinetic}}{E_0}\right)$$

Taking $L_0 = 90$ dB(A) and $E_0 = 1000$ J:

$$L_p = 90 + 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{22{,}032}{1000}\right)$$

$$L_p = 90 + 10 \cdot \log_{10}(22.032)$$

$$L_p = 90 + 10 \cdot 1.3431$$

$$L_p \approx 90 + 13.43$$

$$L_p \approx 103.4 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the OSHA-style hearing-conservation threshold.**

OSHA's permissible exposure limit (PEL) is **90 dB(A)** for 8 hours. Our reading of 103.4 dB(A) requires:

$$T_{safe} = \frac{8}{2^{(L - 90)/5}}$$

$$T_{safe} = \frac{8}{2^{(103.4 - 90)/5}}$$

$$T_{safe} = \frac{8}{2^{2.68}}$$

$$T_{safe} = \frac{8}{6.41}$$

$$T_{safe} \approx 1.25 \text{ hours per day}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation 1:** Without engineering controls (noise enclosure or vibration isolation), a worker can legally be exposed to this hammer for only **~75 minutes per day**. Hearing protection with **SNR ≥ 25 dB** is mandatory.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Press Tonnage Sizing for Upset Forging

**Given:** A cylindrical steel billet of diameter $d_0 = 80$ mm and height $h_0 = 120$ mm is upset (compressed axially) to a final height $h_f = 60$ mm in a hydraulic press at 1100 °C. Steel flow stress at this temperature $\sigma_{flow} \approx 75$ MPa. Friction-shape factor $K_f = 1.6$. Compute the press tonnage required.

**Step 1 — Volume conservation (incompressibility of plastic flow).**

$$V_0 = V_f \Rightarrow \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} h_0 = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} h_f$$

Solving for the final diameter:

$$d_f = d_0 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{h_0}{h_f}}$$

$$d_f = 80 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{120}{60}}$$

$$d_f = 80 \cdot \sqrt{2}$$

$$d_f \approx 80 \cdot 1.4142$$

$$d_f \approx 113.14 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the projected contact area at the end of deformation.**

$$A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4}$$

$$A_f = \frac{\pi \cdot (113.14)^2}{4}$$

$$A_f = \frac{\pi \cdot 12{,}800.6}{4}$$

$$A_f \approx 10{,}053 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the required forging force.**

$$F = K_f \cdot A_f \cdot \sigma_{flow}$$

$$F = 1.6 \cdot 10{,}053 \cdot 75$$

$$F \approx 1{,}206{,}360 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to tonnes (for press selection).**

$$\text{Tonnage} = \frac{F}{g \cdot 1000} \approx \frac{1{,}206{,}360}{9810}$$

$$\text{Tonnage} \approx 122.97 \text{ tonnes (metric)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation 2:** A minimum **~125-tonne hydraulic press** is required. KTU lesson — **press guarding must be rated for full tonnage**, and a worker standing between dies of this press would be subjected to crushing force equal to ~123 cars resting on their chest. **Hence the absolute prohibition on reaching into a press during its cycle.**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Hazard vs. Safe Method Matrix

| Forging Hazard | Engineering Control | Administrative Control | PPE Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hot scale ejection** | Enclosed die shields, exhaust hoods | Clean-floor policy, scale-collection trays | Face shield (IR-rated), heat gloves |
| **Die crushing** | Light curtains, two-hand trips, fixed perimeter guards | Lockout/tagout, die-set verification | Safety boots (steel toe) |
| **Burns from stock** | Robotic transfer, insulated tongs | Designated hot zones, color-coded floors | Aluminized heat apron, sleeve covers |
| **Noise > 100 dB(A)** | Acoustic enclosures, vibration mounts | Job rotation, audiometric testing | Earplugs + earmuffs (dual protection) |
| **Lubricant fire** | Auto-fire suppression (mist/dry chem.) | No-smoking zones, hot-work permits | Fire-resistant clothing |
| **CO / NO<sub>x</sub> fumes** | Local exhaust ventilation (LEV), canopy hoods | Furnace maintenance schedule | Respirator (if LEV insufficient) |
| **Repetitive strain** | Powered assist manipulators | Mandatory rest breaks | Vibration-damping gloves |
| **Pinch points on trim press** | Two-hand control, perimeter rails | Tool-check before each cycle | Cut-resistant gloves |

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Heat Stress at Forge Floor (WBGT Method)

**Given:** A forge shop has wet-bulb temperature $T_w = 32$ °C and globe temperature $T_g = 48$ °C (due to radiant heat from furnaces). Find the WBGT index and recommend work-rest cycle.

**Step 1 — Apply the indoor-with-solar-load WBGT formula.**

$$WBGT = 0.7 \cdot T_w + 0.3 \cdot T_g$$

$$WBGT = 0.7 \cdot 32 + 0.3 \cdot 48$$

$$WBGT = 22.4 + 14.4$$

$$WBGT = 36.8 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Reference threshold limits (ACGIH for acclimatized workers).**

| WBGT (°C) | Recommended Work-Rest Cycle |
|---|---|
| < 25.0 | Continuous work permitted |
| 25.0 – 27.5 | 75% work, 25% rest each hour |
| 27.5 – 30.0 | 50% work, 50% rest |
| > 30.0 | 25% work, 75% rest |

**Step 3 — Classify our case.**

$$WBGT = 36.8 \text{ °C} > 30.0 \text{ °C}$$

→ Mandatory work-rest cycle of **25% work / 75% rest**; continuous hydration; cool-down station mandatory within **15 m** of workstation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** A 1000 °C furnace radiates ~ **58 kW/m²** to a worker standing 1 m away without shielding. Without shielding, WBGT easily exceeds **45 °C** — *this is why furnace-side shields and reflective aprons are mandatory*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
