## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Differentiate between up-milling (conventional) and down-milling (climb) with respect to chip thickness, cutter life, and machine-tool backlash.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Up-Milling (Conventional) | Down-Milling (Climb) |
|---|---|---|
| Chip Thickness | Minimum at tooth entry, maximum at tooth exit | Maximum at tooth entry, minimum at tooth exit |
| Cutter Life | Lower — tooth rubs on workpiece before cutting | Higher — tooth engages at full chip thickness |
| Backlash | Safer — no risk of workpiece snatch | Requires backlash-free drives — otherwise pull-in |
| Surface Finish | Better on cast/oxidised surfaces | Smoother on clean, rigidly clamped workpieces |
| Machine Suitability | Old / worn machines | New CNC / backlash-free mills |

**Mnemonic:** *Up = Upward chip (cutter climbs against the feed); Down = Downward pull (cutter descends with the feed).*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]**
**List any three safety devices fitted on a milling machine and state the hazard each device controls.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Fixed / Interlocked Guard** around the spindle — controls **entanglement and ejection** of chips and tool fragments.
2. **Emergency-Stop (E-Stop) Mushroom Button** — isolates electrical power within 0.5 s; controls **runaway cutter and runaway table motion**.
3. **Coolant / Chip Guard (transparent acrylic curtain)** — controls **chip projection and coolant splash** into operator's face and eyes.
4. *(Bonus)* **Zero-Speed Switch on guard interlock** — prevents spindle start while guard is open; controls **unexpected-startup hazard**.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Unit mismatch** is the single most common reason students lose 2–3 marks. Always convert mm → m (÷1000) before substituting into cutting-speed equations.
> - **Forgetting η (efficiency)** in power problems costs 2 marks — examiners specifically look for it.
> - In **up vs down milling**, never write "down milling is always better" — it is **conditionally safer only when backlash is eliminated**.

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`**

A horizontal milling machine is used to machine a mild-steel slab of length **L = 400 mm** and width **w = 100 mm**. The cutter diameter is **D = 80 mm**, number of effective teeth **Z = 10**, feed per tooth **f_z = 0.25 mm/tooth**, cutting speed **V = 150 m/min**, depth of cut **d = 5 mm**, specific cutting energy **k_c = 2200 N/mm²**, mechanical efficiency **η = 0.78**, and cutter-engagement approach length **l_e = 25 mm**.

**(a)** Calculate the **(i)** spindle speed N, **(ii)** feed rate f_r, and **(iii)** metal removal rate Q. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Calculate the **(iv)** gross cutting power, **(v)** spindle input power, and **(vi)** machining time T_m. Comment briefly on whether the cutter should be high-speed-steel (HSS) or carbide based on your calculated V. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a)**

**(i) Spindle Speed:**

$$
N \;=\; \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi \, D} \;=\; \dfrac{1000 \times 150}{\pi \times 80} \;=\; \dfrac{150000}{251.327}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,N \;\approx\; 596.83 \text{ rpm}\,}
$$

**(ii) Feed Rate:**

$$
f_r \;=\; f_z \, \times \, Z \, \times \, N \;=\; 0.25 \times 10 \times 596.83
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_r \;\approx\; 1492.08 \text{ mm/min}\,}
$$

**(iii) Metal Removal Rate:**

$$
Q \;=\; w \times d \times f_r \;=\; 100 \times 5 \times 1492.08
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q \;\approx\; 746040 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} \;\approx\; 746.04 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}\,}
$$

**Part (b)**

**(iv) Gross Cutting Power:**

$$
P_g \;=\; \dfrac{k_c \times Q}{60 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; \dfrac{2200 \times 746040}{60 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_g \;\approx\; 27.35 \text{ kW}\,}
$$

**(v) Spindle Input Power:**

$$
P_{\text{spindle}} \;=\; \dfrac{P_g}{\eta} \;=\; \dfrac{27.35}{0.78}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{spindle}} \;\approx\; 35.06 \text{ kW}\,}
$$

**(vi) Machining Time:**

$$
T_m \;=\; \dfrac{L + l_e}{f_r} \;=\; \dfrac{400 + 25}{1492.08}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_m \;\approx\; 0.285 \text{ min} \;\approx\; 17.10 \text{ sec}\,}
$$

**Comment on Cutter Material:**
At **V = 150 m/min**, the cutting speed falls in the **medium range** suitable for both HSS (recommended up to ~60–80 m/min) and **carbide (recommended 150–250 m/min)**. Since V = 150 m/min is at the **lower limit of carbide capability**, **carbide cutters** are preferred for longer tool life and higher productivity. HSS would wear rapidly.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 / Internal Choice (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]`**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the difference between **plain (slab) milling and face milling**. Discuss **(i)** cutter orientation, **(ii)** chip thickness variation, and **(iii)** the dominant hazard in each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CNC vertical machining centre performs face-milling on an aluminium-alloy plate of length **L = 250 mm**. Cutter diameter **D = 120 mm**, **Z = 6** inserts, **f_z = 0.15 mm/tooth**, **V = 600 m/min**, **w = 90 mm**, **d = 3 mm**, **k_c = 900 N/mm²**, **η = 0.85**, **l_e = 30 mm**. Calculate **N, f_r, Q, P_spindle** and **T_m**. Also recommend **two engineering controls** to mitigate the coolant-aerosol hazard. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a): Plain Milling vs. Face Milling**

**(i) Cutter Orientation:**
- *Plain milling* uses a **horizontal spindle** and a **peripheral / cylindrical cutter**. The cutter periphery — not the face — does the cutting. Chip flow is roughly horizontal.
- *Face milling* uses a **vertical spindle** and a **face cutter** (inserts mounted on a circular body). Cutting action occurs on the **bottom face** of the inserts. Chip flow is downward.

**(ii) Chip Thickness Variation:**
- In **plain milling**, chip thickness is **zero at tooth entry and maximum at tooth exit** (up-milling) — leading to rubbing, heat, and poor cutter life at start of each tooth engagement.
- In **face milling**, chip thickness is **maximum at entry and zero at exit** — cutter tooth engages a full chip, rubs less, and lasts longer.

**(iii) Dominant Hazard:**
- **Plain milling** → **cutter-fracture hazard** because radial cutting forces tend to deflect thin cutters; also **chip ejection sideways** toward the operator.
- **Face milling** → **projected-chip hazard** because chip velocity is downward + outward at very high speed (hundreds of m/min) and impacts the workpiece, fixture, or floor.

**Part (b): CNC Face-Milling Numerical**

**(i) Spindle Speed:**

$$
N \;=\; \dfrac{1000 \times 600}{\pi \times 120} \;=\; \dfrac{600000}{376.99}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,N \;\approx\; 1591.55 \text{ rpm}\,}
$$

**(ii) Feed Rate:**

$$
f_r \;=\; 0.15 \times 6 \times 1591.55
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_r \;\approx\; 1432.4 \text{ mm/min}\,}
$$

**(iii) Metal Removal Rate:**

$$
Q \;=\; 90 \times 3 \times 1432.4 \;=\; 386748 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q \;\approx\; 386.75 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}\,}
$$

**(iv) Spindle Input Power:**

$$
P_g \;=\; \dfrac{900 \times 386748}{60 \times 10^{6}} \;\approx\; 5.80 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
P_{\text{spindle}} \;=\; \dfrac{5.80}{0.85}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{spindle}} \;\approx\; 6.82 \text{ kW}\,}
$$

**(v) Machining Time:**

$$
T_m \;=\; \dfrac{250 + 30}{1432.4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_m \;\approx\; 0.195 \text{ min} \;\approx\; 11.73 \text{ sec}\,}
$$

**(vi) Engineering Controls for Coolant-Aerosol Hazard:**
1. **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) / Mist Extractor** — high-velocity capture hood placed within 1 cutter diameter of the chip formation zone; collects ≥ 95% of airborne mist per ACGIH guidelines.
2. **Full Machine Enclosure with Negative Pressure** — modern CNC VMCs use interlocked full enclosures maintaining slight negative pressure, preventing aerosol escape into the operator's breathing zone.
3. *(Bonus)* **MQL (Minimum Quantity Lubrication)** instead of flood coolant — drastically reduces aerosol generation at source.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision – Tick These Before Every KTU Exam**

- [ ] **Milling Definition** — rotary multi-point cutter removes material from a (typically stationary) workpiece.
- [ ] **Golden Triangle** — V, f, d — inter-dependent; never treat one as fixed without considering the other two.
- [ ] **Master Equation #1:** $V = \pi D N / 1000$ (m/min)
- [ ] **Master Equation #2:** $f_r = f_z \times Z \times N$ (mm/min)
- [ ] **Master Equation #3:** $Q = w \times d \times f_r$ (mm³/min)
- [ ] **Power Rule:** $P_{spindle} = k_c \, Q / (60 \times 10^6 \, \eta)$ — never forget η.
- [ ] **Time Rule:** $T_m = (L + l_e) / f_r$ — add approach length.
- [ ] **Up vs Down Milling** — Up safer for backlash-prone machines; Down gives better finish & tool life.
- [ ] **Face vs Plain Milling** — Face = vertical spindle, downward chip; Plain = horizontal spindle, sideways chip.
- [ ] **Top 5 Milling Hazards** — (1) entanglement at spindle, (2) chip ejection, (3) noise > 85 dB, (4) coolant aerosol, (5) cutter fracture due to imbalance.
- [ ] **Hierarchy of Controls (per ISO 12100 / KTU Module 2):** Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering (guards, LEV) → Administrative → PPE.
- [ ] **Reference Constants:** Mild-steel $k_c \approx 2000$ N/mm²; Aluminium $k_c \approx 900$ N/mm²; Cast iron $k_c \approx 1500$ N/mm².
- [ ] **Mandatory Conversions:** mm → m (÷ 1000), cm³/min ↔ mm³/min (× 1000), seconds ↔ minutes (× 60).
- [ ] **Common Examiner Traps:** (i) omitting η, (ii) using cm³ without dividing, (iii) stating "down milling is always safer" — conditional, not absolute.

---

> **End of Module 2 – Milling Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 / PCSFT601 – Hazard Control in Industries*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning and Grinding - Maintenance of Machine Tools - Health Hazards and Prevention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT601 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Planning and Grinding - Maintenance of Machine Tools - Health Hazards and Prevention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Grinding — The Formal KTU Definition

**Grinding** is a material removal process in which abrasive particles acting as miniature cutting tools remove tiny chips from a workpiece surface. According to KTU 2024 Scheme (PCSFT601), grinding is classified as a **finishing / super-finishing process** characterized by high dimensional accuracy, low surface roughness, and high specific energy consumption.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Grinding is the most common abrasive machining process. It accounts for roughly **20–25%** of all machining operations in modern industry and is the single largest source of airborne particulate hazard in a machine shop.

The grinding wheel — the principal tool — is composed of three components:

| Component | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Abrasive Grains** | Cut the workpiece | Alumina (Al₂O₃), Silicon Carbide (SiC), CBN, Diamond |
| **Bond** | Holds grains together | Vitrified, Resinoid, Rubber, Metal |
| **Pores** | Chip clearance and coolant flow | Air gaps between bond bridges |

## 1.2 Planning for Grinding Operations

**Process planning** in grinding involves selecting the right wheel, machine parameters, and coolant strategy **before** the operation begins. KTU examiners frequently ask the planning sequence, so memorize the four-tier hierarchy:

1. **Workpiece Analysis** — Hardness, geometry, tolerance, material
2. **Wheel Selection** — Abrasive type, grit size, grade, structure, bond
3. **Process Parameter Setting** — Wheel speed (V), work speed (v), depth of cut (d), feed (f)
4. **Coolant & Safety Planning** — Coolant type, flow rate, guarding, PPE

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Grinding Wheel Speed (KTU Reference Value):**
> - Peripheral wheel surface speed: **V = 30 m/s** (for conventional grinding)
> - Maximum permissible speed: **V_max = 35 m/s** (marked on every wheel as rated speed)

## 1.3 Maintenance of Machine Tools — Definition

**Maintenance** is the set of activities carried out to keep machine tools in a specified operating condition or restore them to that condition. As per KTU PCSFT601, maintenance is the **first line of defense** against occupational hazards because:

- A poorly maintained grinder is a **direct source of injury** (wheel breakage, fire, electrocution)
- Poor spindle condition causes **vibration-induced HAVS** (Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome)
- Misaligned slides produce **chatter dust** with elevated respirable fraction

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Workshop Health Triangle"

Imagine grinding as a **three-legged stool**:

- **Leg 1 — Planning (Right Wheel, Right Speed):** Using a soft wheel on a hard job is like wearing rubber slippers to climb a mountain — failure guaranteed.
- **Leg 2 — Maintenance (Sharp Wheel, True Machine):** A dull, glazed wheel is like a blunt kitchen knife — you press harder, slip more, generate heat, and injure yourself.
- **Leg 3 — Hazard Control (Guards, PPE, Ventilation):** These are the **seatbelts and airbags** — they don't prevent the accident, but they save your life when something goes wrong.

**Remove any one leg, and the stool collapses — which in real life means an accident, breakdown, or chronic disease.**

## 1.5 Health Hazards in Grinding — Overview

The KTU 2024 module identifies **five principal hazard categories** that must appear in any complete answer:

| # | Hazard Category | Health Outcome | Acute / Chronic |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Dust Exposure** | Silicosis, bronchitis, lung cancer | Chronic |
| 2 | **Noise** | NIHL (Noise-Induced Hearing Loss) | Chronic |
| 3 | **Vibration** | HAVS, Reynaud's syndrome | Chronic |
| 4 | **Sparks & Fire** | Burns, eye injury, explosion | Acute |
| 5 | **Mechanical** | Wheel disintegration, entanglement | Acute |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Alert:** A common mistake is to list hazards without classifying them as **acute vs. chronic**. Examiners explicitly award marks for this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Grinding Wheel Specification (KTU Standard Coding System)

The **Indian Standard (IS 1521) / ISO 525** coding system uses a **6-character wheel designation** that is a high-yield KTU question. The format is:

```
[ Abrasive | Grit Size | Grade | Structure | Bond | Manufacturer's Mark ]
   (1 char)  (2 char)  (1)    (1)        (1)        (Mark)
```

### Step-by-Step Wheel Selection Logic

1. **Choose Abrasive:**
   - **Alumina (A)** → ferrous metals (steel, cast iron)
   - **Silicon Carbide (C)** → non-ferrous, carbide, stone, rubber
   - **CBN** → hardened tool steels (>55 HRC)
   - **Diamond** → carbide, ceramics, glass

2. **Choose Grit Size (number = mesh per inch):**
   - Coarse (10–24) → rough grinding, high MRR
   - Medium (30–60) → general purpose
   - Fine (80–220) → finishing
   - Very Fine (240–600) → super-finishing

3. **Choose Grade (bond hardness, A=soft → Z=hard):**
   - Soft grade (H–K) → hard workpiece (releases dull grains)
   - Hard grade (L–N) → soft workpiece (retains sharp grains)

4. **Choose Structure (density of grains):**
   - Dense (low number) → high MRR, fine finish impossible
   - Open (high number) → better coolant flow, longer wheel life

5. **Choose Bond:**
   - **Vitrified (V)** → most common; rigid; brittle; heat-resistant up to **1200 °C**
   - **Resinoid (B)** → strong; used for cutoff and rough grinding
   - **Rubber (R)** → thin wheels, snag grinding
   - **Metal (M)** → CBN/Diamond wheels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic:** **"A Grain Gets Structure By Bond"** → Abrasive-Grit-Grade-Structure-Bond (reading left to right of the code).

## 2.2 Material Removal Rate (MRR) — The Core Grinding Equation

The fundamental equation that defines grinding productivity:

$$MRR = v \cdot a \cdot d$$

Where:
- $MRR$ = Material Removal Rate (mm³/s)
- $v$ = Workpiece traverse speed (mm/s)
- $a$ = Width of grinding contact (mm)
- $d$ = Depth of cut per pass (mm)

**Alternate form** for cylindrical grinding:

$$MRR = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot f \cdot d$$

Where:
- $D$ = Workpiece diameter (mm)
- $N$ = Workpiece rotational speed (rev/s)
- $f$ = Feed per revolution (mm/rev)
- $d$ = Radial infeed (mm)

## 2.3 Grinding Wheel Wear — G-ratio (Wear Efficiency)

$$G = \frac{V_w}{V_s}$$

Where:
- $G$ = Grinding ratio (dimensionless)
- $V_w$ = Volume of workpiece material removed (mm³)
- $V_s$ = Volume of wheel material worn (mm³)

> **KTU Benchmark:** A good grinding ratio **G ≥ 50** is acceptable; **G < 10** indicates poor wheel selection.

## 2.4 Specific Energy in Grinding

Specific energy is the **energy per unit volume** removed — it is 10× higher in grinding than in turning:

$$E_s = \frac{P}{MRR}$$

Where:
- $E_s$ = Specific energy (J/mm³)
- $P$ = Grinding power (W)
- $MRR$ = Material removal rate (mm³/s)

> **Typical values (KTU reference):**
> - Turning: $E_s \approx 2\text{–}5$ J/mm³
> - Grinding: $E_s \approx 20\text{–}60$ J/mm³

## 2.5 Maintenance Strategy Framework

| Maintenance Type | Trigger | Cost | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Breakdown (Reactive)** | After failure | Very High (5× planned) | **Avoid — never acceptable** |
| **Preventive (Scheduled)** | Calendar/hours | Moderate | **Industry standard** |
| **Predictive (Condition-Based)** | Vibration, temperature analysis | Low | **Modern KTU-emphasized approach** |
| **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** | Operator-driven daily checks | Lowest | **Award extra marks** |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical KTU Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $MRR = v \cdot a \cdot d$ | Surface grinding MRR | mm³/s |
| 2 | $MRR = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot f \cdot d$ | Cylindrical grinding MRR | mm³/min |
| 3 | $G = V_w / V_s$ | Grinding ratio (wheel life efficiency) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $E_s = P / MRR$ | Specific energy | J/mm³ |
| 5 | $V = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | Peripheral wheel speed | m/s |
| 6 | $Q = k \cdot A \cdot v$ | Ventilation flow rate | m³/min |
| 7 | $L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T 10^{L/10} dt\right)$ | Equivalent continuous sound level | dB(A) |
| 8 | $D = \frac{8 \cdot C \cdot T}{OEL \cdot 24 \cdot 365}$ | OSHA dose formula | % dose |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formulas 1–4 drive **productivity decisions** (how fast to grind). Formulas 5–8 drive **safety decisions** (how loud, how much dust, how long exposed). Together they form the **dual optimization** of grinding: **maximum output with minimum harm**.

## 2.7 Wheel Breakage Safety — The 80 m/s Rule

Modern super-abrasive wheels operate at **80 m/s** and beyond. The **KTU/BIS permissible wheel speed is reduced** by a safety factor:

$$V_{operating} = 0.8 \cdot V_{rated}$$

Where:
- $V_{rated}$ = Manufacturer's maximum permissible speed (m/s)
- **Reduction factor = 0.8** (for vitrified bonds)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wheel Speed from Diameter and RPM

**Problem Statement:** A grinding wheel of diameter **350 mm** runs at **1480 RPM**. Find the peripheral surface speed.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The peripheral velocity is the distance traveled by a point on the wheel rim per second. Since the wheel rotates once per revolution and travels a circumferential distance equal to $\pi D$:

$$V = \frac{\text{Distance per revolution}}{\text{Time per revolution}} = \frac{\pi D}{\frac{60}{N}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60 \times 1000}}$$

Where:
- $V$ in m/s
- $D$ in mm
- $N$ in RPM

### Numerical Evaluation

$$V = \frac{\pi \times 350 \times 1480}{60 \times 1000}$$

$$V = \frac{1,626,581.6}{60{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 27.1 \text{ m/s}}$$

> **Interpretation:** This value is **below the standard 30 m/s**, indicating the operator should **increase spindle speed** to the rated 30 m/s to fully utilize the wheel's capability.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Material Removal Rate in Surface Grinding

**Problem Statement:** A surface grinder takes a cut of **width = 50 mm, depth = 0.02 mm, table traverse = 12 m/min** on a workpiece **400 mm long**. Find:
1. MRR in mm³/s
2. Total cycle time per pass

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert traverse speed to mm/s:**

$$v = 12 \text{ m/min} \times \frac{1000 \text{ mm}}{60 \text{ s}} = 200 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the MRR equation:**

$$MRR = v \cdot a \cdot d$$

$$MRR = 200 \times 50 \times 0.02$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 200 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate cycle time:**

The wheel must traverse the full workpiece length $L$ and the grinding width covers the entire table width $a = 50$ mm in one pass. The number of passes is the width ratio:

$$n_{passes} = \frac{W}{a}$$

Assume workpiece width $W = 50$ mm (single pass). The cycle time per pass:

$$t = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{400}{200} = 2.0 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{cycle} = 2.0 \text{ s per pass}}$$

**Step 4 — Power estimate (KTU extension):**

If specific energy $E_s = 30$ J/mm³:

$$P = E_s \cdot MRR = 30 \times 200 = 6000 \text{ W} = 6 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{grinding} = 6 \text{ kW}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Grinding Ratio (G-ratio) Numerical

**Problem Statement:** During a grinding test, **40,000 mm³** of steel was removed while the wheel lost **800 mm³** of abrasive. Calculate the G-ratio and comment on wheel efficiency.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$G = \frac{V_w}{V_s} = \frac{40{,}000}{800}$$

$$\boxed{G = 50}$$

> **Interpretation:** A G-ratio of 50 is **acceptable** (above the KTU benchmark of 50). The wheel is **economical** and has good self-sharpening action. If the grade had been too soft, the wheel would wear much faster (lower G-ratio).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equivalent Sound Level (Leq) for Shift Exposure

**Problem Statement:** A worker is exposed to:
- **92 dB(A) for 3 hours**
- **85 dB(A) for 4 hours**
- **78 dB(A) for 1 hour**

Calculate the **8-hour equivalent continuous level ($L_{eq}$)**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The partial noise exposure formula:

$$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{T_0}\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i \cdot 10^{L_i/10}\right]$$

With $T_0 = 8$ hours (reference duration):

**Step 1 — Compute each $10^{L_i/10}$ term:**

$$10^{92/10} = 10^{9.2} = 1.585 \times 10^{9}$$

$$10^{85/10} = 10^{8.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{8}$$

$$10^{78/10} = 10^{7.8} = 6.310 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by exposure time:**

$$\text{Term 1} = 3 \times 1.585 \times 10^{9} = 4.755 \times 10^{9}$$

$$\text{Term 2} = 4 \times 3.162 \times 10^{8} = 1.265 \times 10^{9}$$

$$\text{Term 3} = 1 \times 6.310 \times 10^{7} = 6.310 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the terms:**

$$\sum = 4.755 \times 10^{9} + 1.265 \times 10^{9} + 6.310 \times 10^{7}$$

$$\sum = 6.083 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $T_0 = 8$ and take log:**

$$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{6.083 \times 10^{9}}{8}\right]$$

$$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}(7.604 \times 10^{8})$$

$$L_{eq} = 10 \times 8.881$$

$$\boxed{L_{eq} \approx 88.8 \text{ dB(A)}}$$

> **Interpretation:** This exceeds the **KTU/OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit of 85 dB(A)** for 8 hours. **Hearing protection is mandatory**, and engineering noise controls should be considered.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — OSHA Noise Dose Calculation

Using the same 8-hour exposure data, the **% Dose** is:

$$\text{Dose \%} = 100 \times \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = Actual exposure time at level $L_i$
- $T_i$ = Reference duration at level $L_i$ from OSHA table

Reference values (KTU reference):
- At 92 dB(A): $T_i = 6.06$ h
- At 85 dB(A): $T_i = 16.00$ h
- At 78 dB(A): $T_i = 60.40$ h

**Computation:**

$$\text{Dose \%} = 100 \times \left[\frac{3}{6.06} + \frac{4}{16.00} + \frac{1}{60.40}\right]$$

$$\text{Dose \%} = 100 \times [0.495 + 0.250 + 0.017]$$

$$\boxed{\text{Dose \%} \approx 76.2\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** Although Leq was 88.8 dB(A), the **dose is below 100%**. However, since Leq exceeds 85 dB(A), the worker **must still wear hearing protection per KTU safety norms**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Hazard vs. Control Mapping

| Hazard Source | Engineering Control | Administrative Control | PPE Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Grinding dust** | Local exhaust ventilation (LEV), wet grinding | Job rotation, training | N95/FFP2 respirator, goggles |
| **Noise** | Machine enclosures, damping pads | Shift limits (≤85 dB, 8h) | Ear muffs, ear plugs |
| **Vibration** | Anti-vibration mounts, balanced wheels | Work-rest cycles | Anti-vibration gloves |
| **Sparks/fire** | Spark arrestors, fire extinguishers | No-fuel near sparks | Flame-retardant apron, face shield |
| **Wheel breakage** | Wheel guards, ring-testing before mount | Pre-use inspection, speed checks | Face shield, eye protection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hierarchy of Controls** (KTU 2024-aligned):
> **Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Administrative → PPE**
> Examiners award full marks only when the answer follows this hierarchy. PPE alone is the **last resort**, not the first option.
