# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Grinding Ratio (G-ratio). State its significance in grinding economics.**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Grinding ratio, denoted as $G$, is defined as the **ratio of volume of workpiece material removed to the volume of grinding wheel material worn** during a grinding operation.

$$G = \frac{V_w}{V_s}$$

**Significance:**
1. **Wheel life indicator** — Higher G means longer wheel life and lower per-piece grinding cost.
2. **Wheel selection validation** — A target G-ratio confirms whether the chosen abrasive, grit, and grade are appropriate for the workpiece.
3. **Process optimization parameter** — Used to compare coolant strategies, dressing schedules, and machine stiffness.

> **KTU Marking Note:** Award **1 mark** for definition, **2 marks** for the three significance points.

---

### **Question 2: List any four health hazards associated with grinding operations.**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Respiratory hazards** — Inhalation of fine metallic and abrasive dust leading to silicosis, siderosis, or chronic bronchitis.
2. **Noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL)** — Prolonged exposure above **85 dB(A)** causes permanent cochlear damage.
3. **Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS)** — Extended exposure to handle-transmitted vibration leads to vascular and neurological disorders (white finger disease).
4. **Eye injury from flying particles and sparks** — Grinding sparks travel at **30 m/s** and can cause corneal abrasions or burns.

> **KTU Marking Note:** **1 mark per hazard with a brief technical detail.** Listing without elaboration receives only **0.5 marks per item**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — Explain the systematic procedure for planning a grinding operation. Discuss the role of wheel specification (Abrasive, Grit, Grade, Structure, Bond).** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Workpiece Analysis (2 marks):**
The planner must first identify:
- **Material:** Ferrous (steel, CI) → Alumina; Non-ferrous (Al, Cu, Brass) → Silicon Carbide
- **Hardness:** Above 55 HRC → CBN or Diamond
- **Required surface finish:** Ra < 0.4 μm → Fine grit (120–220)
- **Tolerance:** ±0.005 mm → Cylindrical or surface grinding with fine feed

**Step 2 — Wheel Specification as per IS 1521 (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Function | Selection Logic |
|---|---|---|
| **Abrasive** | Cutting agent | Alumina for steel; SiC for carbide, stone; CBN for tool steel; Diamond for ceramic |
| **Grit Size** | Determines finish & MRR | Coarse (24–36) for roughing; Fine (60–120) for finishing |
| **Grade** | Bond hardness (A soft → Z hard) | Hard workpiece → soft grade wheel (auto-dress); soft workpiece → hard grade |
| **Structure** | Grain spacing | Open structure for ductile material and high MRR; dense structure for finish |
| **Bond** | Holds grains | Vitrified (most common, rigid, brittle, heat-resistant to 1200 °C) |

**Step 3 — Parameter Setting:** Wheel speed = 30 m/s; depth of cut = 0.01–0.05 mm; coolant flow = 10–20 L/min.

#### **Part (b) — Describe the types of maintenance applicable to machine tools. Why is predictive maintenance preferred over breakdown maintenance?** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Types of Maintenance (4 marks):**

1. **Breakdown (Reactive) Maintenance:** Repair after failure. Cost = 5× preventive. Causes secondary damage. **Not acceptable** in modern KTU-aligned industry.
2. **Preventive (Scheduled) Maintenance:** Time-based tasks (lubrication, alignment, dressing). Cost = 1× baseline.
3. **Predictive (Condition-Based) Maintenance:** Uses vibration analysis, thermography, oil analysis, and acoustic emission to schedule repairs **just before** failure. Cost = 0.6× preventive.
4. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM):** Operator-driven daily checks (cleaning, lubrication, inspection). Highest OEE.

**Why Predictive is preferred over Breakdown (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Breakdown Maintenance | Predictive Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| Cost of failure | Very high (5× planned) | Minimal |
| Downtime | Unplanned, often long | Planned, short |
| Worker safety | High accident probability | Lower risk |
| Spare parts availability | Emergency procurement | Scheduled procurement |
| Equipment life | Reduced (10–20%) | Extended (20–30%) |
| OEE | 40–60% | 85%+ |

> **Concluding statement (1 mark):** Predictive maintenance aligns with **KTU 2024 Industry 4.0 standards** and is the modern benchmark for occupational safety.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — With a neat sketch, explain the surface grinding process. List the health hazards and their prevention methods.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Surface Grinding Process (3 marks):**
Surface grinding produces flat surfaces using a **reciprocating table** and a **rotating abrasive wheel**. The workpiece is clamped to a magnetic chuck, the table reciprocates beneath the wheel, and a vertical infeed removes material. Coolant is sprayed at the contact zone to reduce thermal damage and dust.

**Process Flow:**
- Wheel rotation: $\sim$1500 RPM → V ≈ 30 m/s
- Table traverse: 12–20 m/min
- Cross-feed: 5–10 mm/pass
- Infeed (down-feed): 0.01–0.05 mm/pass

**Health Hazards (2 marks) and Prevention (2 marks):**

| Hazard | Health Effect | Prevention |
|---|---|---|
| **Dust (silica, metal)** | Silicosis, bronchitis | Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) + N95 mask |
| **Noise (90–100 dB)** | NIHL, tinnitus | Machine enclosure + ear muffs |
| **Sparks (1100 °C)** | Burns, eye injury | Wheel guard + face shield |
| **Coolant mist** | Dermatitis, asthma | Mist collector + barrier cream |
| **Wheel breakage** | Laceration, fatality | Ring test + guard + 0.8 speed factor |

---

#### **Part (b) — Calculate the G-ratio and specific energy for a grinding operation with the following data:**

- Volume of workpiece removed: **75,000 mm³**
- Volume of wheel worn: **1500 mm³**
- Spindle power: **5 kW**
- MRR: **250 mm³/s**

**Determine if the wheel selection is economical. Comment on the specific energy compared to turning.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute G-ratio (2 marks):**

$$G = \frac{V_w}{V_s} = \frac{75{,}000}{1500} = 50$$

**Step 2 — Compare with benchmark (1 mark):**

Since $G = 50 \geq 50$ (KTU benchmark), the wheel selection is **economical**.

**Step 3 — Compute specific energy (2 marks):**

$$E_s = \frac{P}{MRR} = \frac{5000 \text{ W}}{250 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}} = 20 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Compare with turning (2 marks):**

| Process | Specific Energy |
|---|---|
| Turning | 2–5 J/mm³ |
| **This grinding operation** | **20 J/mm³** |
| Grinding (typical range) | 20–60 J/mm³ |

**Conclusion:** The specific energy of **20 J/mm³** is at the **low end** of grinding values, which is favourable. However, it is still **4–10× higher** than turning, confirming that grinding is energy-intensive and is reserved for **finishing operations** where high accuracy is paramount.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in MRR:** Writing MRR without mm³/s loses 0.5–1 mark per question.
> 2. **Confusing G-ratio with specific energy:** G is dimensionless; Es has units of J/mm³. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for the swap.
> 3. **Listing hazards without classifying** acute vs. chronic — loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Not mentioning the 0.8 safety factor** on wheel speed — a mandatory KTU point.
> 5. **Suggesting PPE as the FIRST control** instead of the last in the hierarchy — major conceptual error.
> 6. **Skipping the ring test** when discussing wheel safety — examiners expect it explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Planning, Grinding, Maintenance & Hazards**

**A. Grinding Fundamentals**
- Grinding is an **abrasive** process, not a single-point cutting process.
- A grinding wheel = **Abrasive + Bond + Pores** (3 components).
- Standard wheel surface speed = **30 m/s** (conventional), **80 m/s+** (CBN/Diamond).
- Operating speed rule: **V_operating ≤ 0.8 × V_rated** (safety factor).

**B. Wheel Coding (IS 1521 / ISO 525)**
- Order = Abrasive → Grit → Grade → Structure → Bond → Manufacturer Mark.
- **Mnemonic:** *A Grain Gets Structure By Bond*.

**C. Key Formulas (Must Memorize)**
- $MRR = v \cdot a \cdot d$ (surface grinding)
- $MRR = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot f \cdot d$ (cylindrical grinding)
- $G = V_w / V_s$ (target: $G \geq 50$)
- $E_s = P / MRR$ (typical grinding: 20–60 J/mm³)
- $V = \pi D N / 60{,}000$ (m/s, with D in mm)
- $L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{T_0}\sum t_i \cdot 10^{L_i/10}\right]$

**D. Maintenance Hierarchy**
- Best to worst: **Predictive > Preventive > TPM > Breakdown**.
- Breakdown maintenance = **5× cost** of preventive — avoid in industry.

**E. Five Health Hazards**
- Dust → Silicosis (use LEV + N95)
- Noise → NIHL (limit 85 dB, 8h; use muffs)
- Vibration → HAVS (use anti-vibration gloves/mounts)
- Sparks → Burns (use guards + face shield)
- Mechanical → Wheel breakage (use ring test + speed check)

**F. Hierarchy of Controls**
- **Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Administrative → PPE**
- PPE is the **last** layer, not the first.

**G. KTU Buzzwords That Score Marks**
- "Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)"
- "Condition-Based Monitoring"
- "Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL)"
- "Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)"
- "Ring Test before mounting"
- "Hierarchy of Controls"

**H. Numerical Conversion Mastery**
- 1 m/s = 197 RPM at D = 100 mm (quick check)
- 1 mm³/s = 60 mm³/min
- 1 dB increase = 26% more acoustic energy (logarithmic!)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding and Cutting - Safety Precautions of Gas Welding and Arc Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT601 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Welding and Cutting - Safety Precautions of Gas Welding and Arc Welding # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Welding and Cutting – A Foundational Definition **Welding** is a permanent joining process in which two or more pieces of metal (or thermoplastic) are fused together by the application of heat, pressure, or both, forming a strong metallurgical bond across the joint interface. In industrial hazard control, welding is classified as a **hot-work process** because it inherently generates high temperatures (up to **6000 °C** in the arc core), molten metal spatter, intense electromagnetic radiation, toxic fumes, and combustible gases. **Cutting**, in this context, refers to **thermal cutting processes** such as **oxy-fuel cutting (OFC)**, **arc cutting**, and **plasma cutting**, which use controlled thermal energy to sever metal by localized melting or oxidation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Under the broader umbrella of *Industrial Hazards & Safety*, this topic demands rigorous coverage of **(i) Gas Welding (Oxy-Acetylene) Safety** and **(ii) Arc Welding (SMAW/GTAW/GMAW) Safety**, with explicit linkage to the hierarchy of controls (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE). ### 1.2 Classification of Welding Hazards The principal hazard families in welding operations are tabulated below for clarity: | Hazard Family | Typical Source | Primary Effect | |---|---|---| | **Fire & Explosion** | Sparks, spatter, hot slag, leaking fuel gas | Burns, structural fire, detonation | | **Electrical** | Arc welding power source, secondary circuit | Electrocution, secondary shock | | **Radiant Energy** | UV, Visible, IR from arc / flame | Arc eye (photokeratitis), skin burns | | **Chemical / Toxic** | Fumes, ozone (O₃), nitrogen oxides (NOₓ), phosgene | Respiratory illness, metal-fume fever | | **Mechanical** | Falling cylinders, cable trip hazards | Crush injuries, lacerations | | **Ergonomic** | Awkward postures, repetitive motion | MSDs, welder's neck | ## 1.3 Intuitive Overview – The "Kitchen Stove vs. Lightning Bolt" Analogy To a first-time learner, welding safety can be visualized through two everyday analogies: > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 1 – Gas Welding ≈ A High-Pressure Kitchen Stove on Steroids** > Imagine a domestic LPG stove, but with **two extremely dangerous bottles**: one containing **oxygen (O₂)** at ~**150 bar** (a fierce oxidizer that violently accelerates combustion), and another containing **acetylene (C₂H₂)** at ~**17 bar** (an unstable fuel that detonates if mishandled). Now imagine the flame burning at ~**3,160 °C** instead of a stove's 300 °C. Every safety rule in gas welding is essentially "Don't let the high-pressure oxidizer and the explosive fuel meet uncontrolled." > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 2 – Arc Welding ≈ A Controlled Lightning Bolt** > The electric arc welding process is conceptually similar to a **miniature lightning strike** sustained between the electrode and the workpiece. Temperatures inside the arc column reach approximately **6,000–20,000 °C**, hotter than the surface of the sun. The arc also emits intense **UV/IR radiation** (which is why you cannot stare at it without a filter shade of at least **Shade No. 10–13**). Safety here is essentially "Do not become the path of least resistance for the lightning, and do not look at it." ### 1.4 Why Safety Precautions are Non-Negotiable in KTU Context Globally, welding-related incidents account for a significant proportion of industrial injuries. The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** and the **Factories Act, 1948 (India)** along with **Indian Electricity Rules** mandate strict controls. From a KTU examiner's viewpoint, a student is expected to: 1. **Identify** the hazard, 2. **Evaluate** its risk level, 3. **Recommend** the appropriate engineering control or PPE, and 4. **Justify** the choice using regulatory or scientific reasoning. This four-step analytical flow is the **examination scoring matrix** for this topic. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Gas Welding (Oxy-Acetylene) – Hazards and Safety Precautions Gas welding uses the combustion of **acetylene (C₂H₂)** with **oxygen (O₂)** to produce a flame. The neutral flame temperature is approximately **3,160 °C**. ### 2.1.1 The Combustion Chemistry $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CO} + \text{H}_2 + \text{Heat} \quad (\text{Primary flame — Inner cone})$$ $$\text{2CO} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 \quad ; \quad \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (\text{Secondary flame — Outer envelope})$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **neutral flame** stoichiometric ratio is **O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1 : 1 by volume**. Any deviation produces an **oxidizing** (excess O₂) or **carburizing** (excess C₂H₂) flame, each carrying its own weld-defect and safety implications. ### 2.1.2 Cylinder Safety (High-Yield Area) **Oxygen Cylinder Color Code (India – IS 4379):** $$\text{Color: Black body, white shoulder} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Purity: 99.5\% min O}_2, \text{ Pressure: } \sim 150 \text{ bar at 15 °C}$$ **Acetylene Cylinder Color Code:** $$\text{Color: Maroon body, white shoulder} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Purity: 98\% min C}_2\text{H}_2, \text{ Pressure: } \sim 17 \text{ bar at 15 °C}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Trap:** Many students write "Acetylene cylinder pressure = 17 bar" as if it is harmless. In reality, acetylene is **unstable above 2 bar gauge in free gaseous form**. Cylinders are filled with a **porous monolithic mass (calcium silicate)** soaked in **acetone**, which dissolves the acetylene safely. **Never** use acetylene above **1.03 bar** pressure in free pipelines – this is the **critical safety ceiling**. ### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Hazard Control Logic for Gas Welding The following structured list represents the engineering and administrative controls mandated by KTU-equivalent industrial safety standards: **A. Cylinder Handling & Storage** - Store cylinders **upright**, chained/secured to a wall or trolley to prevent tipping. - Maintain **minimum 6 m** separation between fuel-gas and oxygen cylinders, OR a **fire-resistive wall (≥ 2 hour rating)** of height ≥ 1.5 m between them. - Cap valves when cylinders are not in use. - Never lift cylinders by the valve cap or hoist with magnets. - Do not expose cylinders to temperatures above **45 °C** (acetone vapor pressure rises dangerously). **B. Regulator and Hose Safety** - Use only **oxygen-specific regulators** (green, right-hand thread) on O₂ cylinders and **fuel-gas regulators** (red, left-hand thread) on C₂H₂ cylinders. **Cross-thread reversal** is the first line of defense against accidental fuel-oxidizer mixing. - Inspect hoses daily; replace if cracked, burnt, or oil-contaminated. - Use **hose check valves (flashback arrestors)** at both torch and regulator ends. The **flashback arrestor** contains a **flame-quenching sintered metal element** and a **non-return valve**. - Use **hose colour code**: **Black** for acetylene, **Blue** for oxygen (per IS 4472 / OSHA 1926.350). **C. Backfire, Flashback, and Burn-Back** | Phenomenon | Definition | Cause | Control | |---|---|---|---| | **Backfire** | Flame retreats into nozzle, momentary pop | Tip touching workpiece, loose tip, low O₂ | Re-light after cooling; check tip seating | | **Flashback** | Flame propagates back through hose into regulator/cylinder | Damaged tip, reverse gas flow, cracked hose | **Flashback arrestor** at torch and regulator | | **Burn-back** | Tip melts due to sustained overheating | Insufficient cooling, wrong tip size | Stop work, replace tip | **D. Fire Prevention and Ventilation** - Maintain a **fire watch** for at least **30 minutes** after hot-work completion. - Keep a **Class B (CO₂ or Dry Powder) fire extinguisher** within **3 m** of the work area. - Ensure mechanical ventilation with **≥ 15 air changes/hour** in confined spaces. - No oil/grease on oxygen fittings → **O₂ + hydrocarbon = spontaneous combustion** (auto-ignition temperature for oil in pure O₂ ≈ **250 °C**, vs. **400 °C** in air). ### 2.2 Arc Welding (Shielded Metal Arc Welding – SMAW) – Hazards and Safety Arc welding uses an **electric arc** between a consumable electrode and the workpiece. Typical open-circuit voltage (OCV) is **50–80 V** for SMAW; working voltage is **20–30 V** at currents of **60–400 A**. ### 2.2.1 Electrical Hazard Hierarchy $$\text{Threshold of perception} \approx 1\text{ mA} \quad ; \quad \text{Let-go threshold} \approx 10\text{–}20\text{ mA} \quad ; \quad \text{Ventricular fibrillation} \approx 100\text{ mA for 1 s}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > While the welding secondary is **"low voltage"** (under 100 V), the **primary side** operates at **220 V / 415 V, 50 Hz**. **Primary-side shock is frequently fatal**. Secondary-side "**wet-welding**" conditions (sweaty gloves, standing in water) reduce body resistance and can cause severe secondary shock. ### 2.2.2 Radiant Energy Hazard – Optical Radiation The arc emits a continuous spectrum dominated by UV-B and UV-C: $$\text{Effective irradiance at 1 m from arc} \approx 100\text{ W/m}^2 \text{ (UV-C)} \quad ; \quad \text{Threshold for photokeratitis} \approx 50\text{ J/m}^2$$ **Filter Shade Selection (IS 1179 / ANSI Z49.1):** $$\text{Shade No.} = f(\text{Process}, \text{Current}) \quad ; \quad \text{For SMAW ≤ 200 A → Shade 10–12} \quad ; \quad \text{200–400 A → Shade 12–14}$$ ### 2.2.3 Step-by-Step Hazard Control Logic for Arc Welding **A. Electrical Safety Controls** - Use **double-insulated** welding power sources conforming to **IS 1851**. - Ground the workpiece using a separate **dedicated grounding cable** (cross-section ≥ **6 mm² copper**). - Inspect electrode holder insulation daily; replace if damaged. - Never change electrodes with bare, wet hands; use dry, hole-free **leather gauntlets**. - Install a **residual current device (RCD / ELCB)** of rating **≤ 30 mA** on the primary supply for confined-space welding. **B. Eye & Face Protection** - Use an **arc welding helmet** with **auto-darkening filter (ADF)** having a **shade rating** appropriate for the process. - Provide **flash goggles (Shade 5–6)** for helpers and bystanders within **10 m** of the arc. - Install **transparent welding curtains** (UV-absorbing polycarbonate, shade ≥ **4–5**) around the work cell. **C. Fume & Ventilation Control** $$\text{Welding fume generation rate} \approx 0.1\text{–}2.0 \text{ g/min} \quad (\text{depends on process, electrode, current})$$ - **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** with capture velocity of **0.5–1.0 m/s** at the source. - Threshold Limit Values (TLV) – ACGIH: - **Mn** (Manganese) = **0.02 mg/m³** (respirable fraction) – **neurological hazard** - **Cr** (Chromium VI) = **0.005 mg/m³** - **Total welding fume** = **5 mg/m³** **D. PPE Complete Kit for Arc Welding** - **Helmet** with correct filter shade - **Flame-retardant coveralls** (FRC) – cotton treated with Proban/Pyrovatex - **Leather gauntlets** (gauntlet length ≥ 200 mm) - **Safety boots** with steel toe-cap and metatarsal guard - **Earplugs/earmuffs** if noise exceeds **85 dBA** ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Standard Value | |---|---|---| | Acetylene max safe free-line pressure | $P_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} \le 1.03$ | bar (gauge) | | Oxygen auto-ignition of oil | $T_{\text{auto}}$ in O₂ ≈ **250** | °C | | Neutral flame O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio | $\text{O}_2 / \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 = 1:1$ | by volume | | Arc core temperature | $T_{\text{arc}} \approx 6000\text{–}20000$ | °C | | Neutral flame temperature | $T_{\text{flame}} \approx 3160$ | °C | | SMAW open-circuit voltage | $V_{\text{OCV}} \approx 50\text{–}80$ | V | | SMAW working voltage | $V_{\text{w}} \approx 20\text{–}30$ | V | | Ventricular fibrillation threshold (let-go) | $I_{\text{threshold}} \approx 100$ | mA (1 s exposure) | | LEV capture velocity (welding) | $v_{\text{capture}} \approx 0.5\text{–}1.0$ | m/s | | Air changes (confined space gas welding) | $\text{ACH} \ge 15$ | per hour | | Fire extinguisher reach | $d \le 3$ | metres from work area | | Cylinder separation distance | $d_{\text{O}_2-\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} \ge 6$ | metres (or 2-h fire wall) | | PPE shade selection (SMAW ≤ 200 A) | $\text{Shade} \approx 10\text{–}12$ | IS 1179 / ANSI Z49.1 | | Grounding cable cross-section | $A_{\text{copper}} \ge 6$ | mm² | | RCD rating (welding primary) | $I_{\Delta n} \le 30$ | mA | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why Acetylene is Restricted to 1.03 bar Free-Line Pressure We derive the thermodynamic ceiling for safe handling of gaseous acetylene using the **fundamental decomposition reaction** of acetylene: $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 \;(g) \longrightarrow 2\text{C}\,(s) + \text{H}_2\;(g) \quad \Delta H = -226 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Step 1:** At pressures above **1.03 bar (gauge)** in the free gaseous state, acetylene becomes **thermodynamically metastable**. Any localised energy input — a spark, adiabatic compression, or even a brass fitting impact — provides the **activation energy (Eₐ ≈ 80–100 kJ/mol)** required to initiate exothermic decomposition. **Step 2:** The decomposition is **exothermic** with $\Delta H = -226 \text{ kJ/mol}$. Once initiated, the reaction is **self-sustaining** because the heat released exceeds that required to dissociate adjacent molecules, producing a **detonation wave** rather than deflagration. $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{C} + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -226 \text{ kJ/mol} \quad (\text{Self-propagating at } P > 1.03 \text{ bar gauge})$$ **Step 3:** Therefore, to **eliminate** the detonation hazard, acetylene is dissolved in acetone inside a porous monolith, raising the effective "decomposition energy barrier." The pipeline downstream of the regulator is kept at ≤ 1.03 bar gauge, ensuring even if decomposition initiates, the wave cannot sustain itself due to the **pressure-volume energy deficit**. $$\boxed{\,P_{\text{free-line, C}_2\text{H}_2} \le 1.03 \text{ bar gauge (mandatory safety ceiling)}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This is why acetylene cylinders are NEVER filled with gas alone — the **acetone matrix** is not a packing material; it is a **safety functional component**. Loss of acetone (e.g., leaving the cylinder horizontal for long durations, allowing acetone to migrate out) is treated as a serious safety violation. ## 3.2 Derivation: Filter Shade Number for Arc Welding Eye Protection The optical density (D) of a filter required to attenuate the arc radiance to a safe level is: $$D = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{L_{\text{arc}}}{L_{\text{safe}}}\right)$$ where $L_{\text{arc}}$ is the arc radiance (cd/m²) and $L_{\text{safe}}$ is the maximum safe corneal radiance ≈ $10 \text{ cd/m}^2$ for prolonged exposure. **Step 1:** For a 200 A SMAW arc, the radiance is approximately: $$L_{\text{arc}} \approx 10^{6} \text{ cd/m}^2$$ **Step 2:** Substituting: $$D = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^{6}}{10}\right) = \log_{10}(10^{5}) = 5$$ **Step 3:** The empirical correlation between optical density and **shade number** (per IS 1179) is: $$\text{Shade No.} \approx 1 + \frac{7}{3}D$$ **Step 4:** Substituting $D = 5$: $$\text{Shade No.} = 1 + \frac{7}{3}(5) = 1 + 11.67 \approx 12$$ $$\boxed{\,\text{Required shade for 200 A SMAW} \approx 12\,}$$ This matches the **KTU-recommended** value of Shade 10–12 for SMAW ≤ 200 A. ## 3.3 Derivation: Ventilation Requirement Calculation Given a confined-space welding booth of volume $V = 6 \text{ m} \times 4 \text{ m} \times 3 \text{ m} = 72 \text{ m}^3$, and a fume generation rate $G = 0.5 \text{ g/min}$ for a GMAW process, determine the exhaust flow rate $Q$ required to keep fume concentration below the TLV of $5 \text{ mg/m}^3$. **Step 1:** Mass-balance steady-state equation: $$Q \cdot C_{\text{TLV}} = G$$ **Step 2:** Express $C_{\text{TLV}} = 5 \text{ mg/m}^3 = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/m}^3$, $G = 0.5 \text{ g/min} = 0.5 / 60 \text{ g/s}$. $$Q = \frac{G}{C_{\text{TLV}}} = \frac{0.5 / 60}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{8.33 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 3:** Air changes per hour: $$\text{ACH} = \frac{Q \times 3600}{V} = \frac{1.67 \times 3600}{72} = \frac{6012}{72} \approx 83.5 \text{ ACH}$$ $$\boxed{\,\text{Exhaust flow required} \approx 1.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \;(\approx 100 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}) \Rightarrow 83.5 \text{ ACH}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Since the calculated ACH of 83.5 >> the minimum 15 ACH mandated for gas welding, this confirms that **LEV at the source (fume extractor)** is more efficient than general dilution ventilation, and is therefore the **preferred hierarchy-of-controls choice** in KTU answers. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Comparative Hazard Severity Scoring This matrix is a **KTU-style analytical tool** to rank hazards quantitatively for exam-style risk-evaluation questions. | Hazard | Severity (S, 1–5) | Probability (P, 1–5) | Risk Score $R = S \times P$ | Priority | |---|---|---|---|---| | Acetylene cylinder detonation | 5 | 2 | **10** | Critical | | Arc-eye (photokeratitis) | 3 | 4 | **12** | Critical | | Fume inhalation (Mn) – chronic | 4 | 4 | **16** | Critical | | Skin burn (UV-B) | 3 | 4 | **12** | Critical | | Primary-side electrocution | 5 | 2 | **10** | Critical | | Secondary shock (wet welding) | 4 | 3 | **12** | Critical | | Fire from sparks | 4 | 3 | **12** | Critical | | Trip hazards (cables) | 2 | 4 | **8** | High | | Ergonomic – welder's neck | 2 | 5 | **10** | Critical | **Interpretation:** Risks with $R \ge 10$ are categorized as **Critical** and require immediate engineering controls AND PPE; $5 \le R < 10$ requires administrative controls; $R < 5$ is acceptable with routine monitoring. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Oxy-Acetylene Welding Safety Chain The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional safety architecture** of an oxy-acetylene welding station, illustrating how each safety element interacts in series — failure of any single element compromises the entire chain. ```mermaid flowchart LR CYLO["OXYGEN CYLINDER
Black body 150 bar
Green regulator RHT"] CYLA["ACETYLENE CYLINDER
Maroon body 17 bar
Red regulator LHT"] REG1["O2 REGULATOR
Drops to 2-3 bar"] REG2["C2H2 REGULATOR
Max 1.03 bar gauge"] FBA1["FLASHBACK ARRESTOR 1
Regulator end"] FBA2["FLASHBACK ARRESTOR 2
Torch end"] HOSEO["O2 HOSE Blue"] HOSEA["C2H2 HOSE Black"] TORCH["WELDING TORCH
Nozzle tip"] VENT["VENTILATION
15 ACH min"] FIRE["FIRE EXTINGUISHER
Class B within 3 m"] PPE["PPE KIT
Goggles gloves apron boots"] OP["WELDER
Trained certified"] CYLO --> REG1 --> FBA1 --> HOSEO --> TORCH CYLA --> REG2 --> FBA2 --> HOSEA --> TORCH TORCH --> OP VENT -.-> OP FIRE -.-> OP PPE -.-> OP ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows represent the **flow of gases and energy**; dashed arrows represent **supporting safety controls** that act upon the welder independently of the gas flow. This dual-layer architecture — *process safety + personal safety* — is the hallmark of the **Swiss Cheese Model (James Reason)** applied to welding hazards. ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Arc Welding (SMAW) Safety Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB POW["WELDING POWER SOURCE
220 V 415 V primary
50-80 V OCV secondary"] PRI["PRIMARY PROTECTION
ELCB RCD 30 mA
MCB 16 A"] SEC["SECONDARY CIRCUIT
Electrode holder
Grounding clamp 6 mm2"] ARC["ELECTRIC ARC
6000 to 20000 C
UV IR visible"] FUME["WELDING FUME
Mn Cr Fe oxides
0.1 to 2 g per min"] LEV["LOCAL EXHAUST VENT
0.5 to 1 m per sec capture"] HELM["ARC HELMET
Shade 10 to 14 ADF"] GLOV["LEATHER GAUNTLETS
Flame retardant"] SUIT["FRC COVERALL
Cotton Proban"] BOOT["SAFETY BOOTS
Steel toe metatarsal"] CURT["WELDING CURTAIN
Shade 4 to 5 polycarbonate"] FIREW["FIRE WATCH
30 min post-work"] OP2["WELDER
Certified trained"] POW --> PRI --> SEC --> ARC ARC --> FUME --> LEV ARC --> HELM ARC -.-> GLOV ARC -.-> SUIT ARC -.-> BOOT ARC -.-> CURT FIREW -.-> OP2 HELM -.-> OP2 GLOV -.-> OP2 SUIT -.-> OP2 BOOT -.-> OP2 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Hazard Hierarchy of Controls (Applied to Welding) ```mermaid flowchart TD ELIM["ELIMINATION
Replace hot work with mechanical fastening"] SUB["SUBSTITUTION
Use low-fume electrodes E7018 instead of E6010"] ENG["ENGINEERING CONTROLS
LEV flashback arrestors RCD ventilation"] ADM["ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS
Permit-to-work SOP training fire watch"] PPE["PPE
Helmet gloves boots coveralls curtain"] WORKER["WELDER
Last line of defense"] ELIM --> SUB --> ENG --> ADM --> PPE --> WORKER ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Emphasis:** When asked *"Suggest control measures,"* always present answers in the order **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE**. Students who jump straight to PPE lose marks for demonstrating shallow understanding of the hierarchy. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **"Why is acetylene never used at free-line pressures above 1.03 bar gauge? Explain in three points."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Thermodynamic instability:** Above 1.03 bar (gauge), gaseous acetylene becomes metastable and can undergo **exothermic decomposition** ($C_2H_2 \rightarrow 2C + H_2$, $\Delta H = -226 \text{ kJ/mol}$) without external oxygen. 2. **Self-sustaining detonation:** The decomposition is **self-propagating** — a single spark can initiate a detonation wave through the entire pipe network. 3. **Mitigation by dissolution:** Acetylene is therefore dissolved in acetone inside a porous monolith in cylinders; this raises the activation energy and effectively eliminates detonation risk in free pipelines. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **"List any six personal protective equipment (PPE) required for arc welding."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Sl. No. | PPE Item | Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Arc welding helmet with shade 10–14 ADF** | Protects eyes/face from UV, IR, visible radiation | | 2 | **Leather gauntlets** (≥ 200 mm length) | Protects hands/forearms from heat, spatter, electric shock | | 3 | **Flame-retardant coveralls (FRC)** | Protects body from sparks, spatter, UV | | 4 | **Steel-toe safety boots** with metatarsal guard | Protects feet from falling objects and sparks | | 5 | **Welding goggles (Shade 5–6)** for helpers | Protects bystanders from arc flash | | 6 | **Earplugs / earmuffs** | Protects hearing (noise > 85 dBA) | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark Losers):** > - Writing generic answers like *"wear gloves and goggles"* without specifying **shade number, material, or standard** → loses 1–2 marks per sub-part. > - Failing to mention **regulatory standard references** (IS 1179, IS 1851, ANSI Z49.1, OSHA 1926) → loses up to 2 marks in 14-mark questions. > - Ignoring **hierarchy of controls** — going straight to PPE without discussing elimination/substitution/engineering → loses 2–3 marks. > - Confusing **RHT/LHT thread convention** (oxygen = right-hand, fuel = left-hand) → loses 1 mark. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]** **(a)** Explain the **safety precautions to be observed during oxy-acetylene welding** with respect to: (i) cylinder storage and handling, (ii) regulator and hose connections, (iii) prevention of backfire and flashback. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **fire prevention measures** that must be implemented at a gas welding site, including extinguisher selection and fire-watch protocol. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) (i) Cylinder storage and handling – 2.5 marks:** - Cylinders stored **upright**, chained to wall or trolley. - Minimum **6 m** separation between O₂ and C₂H₂ cylinders OR a **2-hour fire-rated wall** of height ≥ 1.5 m between them. - Valve protection caps fitted when not in use. - Storage area well-ventilated, away from heat sources; ambient temperature **< 45 °C**. - "No smoking" and "No naked flame" signs prominently displayed. - Cylinders moved using **trolleys** — never rolled or dragged. **(a) (ii) Regulator and hose connections – 2.5 marks:** - Use **dedicated regulators**: oxygen regulator (green, **right-hand thread**) on O₂ cylinder; fuel regulator (red, **left-hand thread**) on C₂H₂ cylinder. This **thread reversal** prevents accidental cross-connection. - Open cylinder valve **slowly** and stand to the side (not in front of regulator). - Use **flashback arrestors** at both regulator end and torch end of each hose. - Hose colour code: **Black** = acetylene, **Blue** = oxygen (IS 4472). - Keep hoses off the floor where they can be damaged; use **cable/hose covers** in walkways. - **Purge hoses individually** before lighting the torch — open fuel valve first to clear air, then oxygen. **(a) (iii) Prevention of backfire and flashback – 2 marks:** - Use correct **tip size** for the metal thickness. - Keep torch tip **clean**; use tip cleaners (never files) to remove carbon. - Maintain adequate **gas flow** — never starve the flame of oxygen or fuel. - Install **flashback arrestors** with flame-quenching sintered element + non-return valve. - If flashback occurs, **immediately close the oxygen valve first**, then the acetylene valve. This starves the flame and prevents re-ignition inside the hose. **(b) Fire prevention measures – 7 marks:** **Fire Triangle Elimination (3 marks):** - **Fuel source control:** Remove combustible materials within **11 m** of the work area; cover immovable combustibles with **fire-resistant blankets**. - **Ignition source control:** Maintain spark/spatter shields; clear area of rags, paper, sawdust; close floor/wall openings within **11 m** radius. - **Oxidizer source control:** Check all O₂ fittings for oil contamination; ensure acetylene lines have no leaks. **Fire Extinguisher Selection (2 marks):** - **Class B (flammable liquid/gas)** extinguishers are mandatory: **CO₂** (preferred — leaves no residue) or **Dry Chemical Powder (DCP)**. - Extinguisher must be within **3 m** of the work area and operators must be trained in its use (PASS technique: Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep). - **Never use water** on a gas-cylinder fire or on burning acetylene — water can spread the fire and may cause acetylene line rupture. **Fire Watch Protocol (2 marks):** - A designated **trained fire watch** must be present during hot work and for **at least 30 minutes after** work completion (some standards mandate **60 minutes** for high-risk sites). - The fire watch must patrol the work area and adjacent spaces, watching for **smouldering fires** behind walls, in ducts, or on floors below. - Fire watch must have **no other duties** during this period — it is a dedicated, single-task assignment. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]** **(a)** Describe the **electrical hazards** in arc welding and the **safety precautions** to mitigate them. Include in your answer the role of ELCB/RCD, grounding cable sizing, and the open-circuit voltage concept. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **radiant energy hazards** (UV, IR, visible) generated by the welding arc. Discuss the choice of **filter shade**, the phenomenon of **arc eye (photokeratitis)**, and the engineering controls to protect bystanders. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Electrical hazards and safety precautions – 7 marks:** **Hazard identification (2 marks):** - **Primary side shock (220/415 V)** — potentially fatal; can cause ventricular fibrillation at currents > 100 mA. - **Secondary side shock (50–80 V OCV)** — usually not lethal in dry conditions but dangerous under "wet" conditions (sweat, water, rain) where body resistance drops from ~100 kΩ to ~1 kΩ. - **Open-circuit voltage (OCV)** — present even when not welding; contact with electrode or holder gives shock. - **Improper grounding** — current may seek alternative paths through the operator. **Safety controls (5 marks):** - Install **RCD / ELCB of rating ≤ 30 mA** on the primary supply — disconnects within 30 ms on earth fault. - Use **double-insulated welding power source** conforming to IS 1851. - **Dedicated grounding cable** of cross-section ≥ **6 mm² copper**, connected directly between workpiece and power source ground terminal. - Inspect **electrode holder** insulation daily; never use a holder with damaged insulation. - Do not weld in wet clothing or standing in water; use **rubber mats** in damp locations. - Use **dry leather gauntlets** — never change electrodes with bare hands. - Disconnect power source before maintenance or when leaving equipment unattended. - Ensure proper **cable sizing** to avoid voltage drop and overheating. **(b) Radiant energy hazards – 7 marks:** **Spectrum emitted by arc (2 marks):** - **UV-C (200–280 nm):** Causes photokeratitis (arc eye) and erythema. - **UV-B (280–315 nm):** Skin burns, snow blindness. - **UV-A (315–400 nm):** Cataracts (chronic exposure). - **Visible (400–700 nm):** Retinal damage, "blue-light hazard." - **IR (700 nm–1 mm):** Cataracts, retinal burns, heat stress. **Arc eye / photokeratitis (2 marks):** - Inflammation of the cornea caused by UV-B exposure. - Symptoms appear **2–24 hours** after exposure: pain, gritty sensation, lacrimation, photophobia. - Usually reversible within 24–48 hours, but **highly disabling**. - The "**welder's flash**" phenomenon. **Filter shade selection (2 marks):** - For SMAW ≤ 200 A → **Shade 10–12** (IS 1179 / ANSI Z49.1). - For 200–400 A → **Shade 12–14**. - For gas welding (light-duty) → **Shade 4–6**. - Calculation: $D = \log_{10}(L_{\text{arc}}/L_{\text{safe}})$ and Shade No. $= 1 + \frac{7}{3}D$. **Bystander protection (1 mark):** - **Welding curtains** of UV-absorbing polycarbonate (Shade 4–5) around the work cell. - **Bystander goggles (Shade 5–6)** for helpers within **10 m**. - **Restricted-access zone** demarcated with warning signs. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read Before Every Exam** **A. Gas Welding Safety — Top 12 Must-Knows** 1. Acetylene free-line pressure ≤ **1.03 bar gauge** (detonation prevention). 2. Acetylene dissolved in acetone in porous monolith — loss of acetone = serious hazard. 3. Cylinder colour code (India): **O₂ = black body / white shoulder**; **C₂H₂ = maroon body / white shoulder**. 4. **6 m** separation between O₂ and C₂H₂ cylinders OR 2-h fire wall ≥ 1.5 m. 5. **O₂ regulator = green, right-hand thread (RHT)**; **Fuel regulator = red, left-hand thread (LHT)**. 6. Hose colour code: **Black = C₂H₂**, **Blue = O₂**. 7. Always install **flashback arrestors** at both torch-end and regulator-end of each hose. 8. To extinguish a flashback: **close O₂ valve first**, then C₂H₂. 9. **Never use oil/grease** on oxygen fittings — auto-ignition in pure O₂ at ~250 °C. 10. Minimum **15 air changes per hour** in confined spaces. 11. **Class B fire extinguisher (CO₂ or DCP)** within **3 m**; never use water on gas-cylinder fires. 12. Fire watch for **30 minutes** after hot-work completion. **B. Arc Welding Safety — Top 12 Must-Knows** 1. Primary side **220/415 V** = potentially **fatal**; secondary side **50–80 V OCV** = dangerous under wet conditions. 2. Install **RCD/ELCB ≤ 30 mA** on primary side. 3. **Grounding cable ≥ 6 mm² copper**, dedicated from workpiece to power source. 4. Use **double-insulated** welding power source per **IS 1851**. 5. **SMAW ≤ 200 A → Shade 10–12**; **200–400 A → Shade 12–14** (IS 1179 / ANSI Z49.1). 6. Arc temperature **6000–20,000 °C**; never view without filter. 7. **Arc eye / photokeratitis** appears 2–24 h post-exposure; reversible but disabling. 8. **Mn (Manganese) TLV = 0.02 mg/m³** — neurological hazard. 9. **LEV capture velocity** for fume: **0.5–1.0 m/s** at source. 10. Use **flame-retardant coveralls (FRC)** — never synthetic clothing (melts onto skin). 11. **Leather gauntlets ≥ 200 mm**; dry condition; inspect insulation daily. 12. Welding curtains (Shade 4–5 polycarbonate) for bystander protection; **10 m** restricted zone. **C. Universal Safety Principles** - **Hierarchy of controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → **PPE**. - Always reference **Indian Standards (IS)** and **Factories Act 1948** in answers. - Maintain **fire extinguisher within 3 m** + **fire watch for 30 min post-work** (universal for both processes). - **Permit-to-work** system mandatory for all hot-work operations. - **No welding on containers that held flammable substances** without gas-freeing certificate. **D. Acronyms Decoded** - **OCV:** Open-Circuit Voltage - **RCD/ELCB:** Residual Current Device / Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker - **LEV:** Local Exhaust Ventilation - **FRC:** Flame-Retardant Coverall - **ADF:** Auto-Darkening Filter - **DCP:** Dry Chemical Powder (extinguisher) - **TLV:** Threshold Limit Value - **PPE:** Personal Protective Equipment - **SOP:** Standard Operating Procedure - **MSD:** Musculoskeletal Disorder **E. Common Numerical Values to Memorize** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Acetylene max free-line pressure | 1.03 bar | | Cylinder separation (O₂/fuel) | 6 m | | Air changes per hour (confined space) | 15 | | Fire extinguisher reach | 3 m | | Fire watch duration | 30 min | | Arc temperature | 6,000–20,000 °C | | Neutral flame temperature | 3,160 °C | | O₂ auto-ignition of oil | 250 °C | | SMAW OCV | 50–80 V | | SMAW working voltage | 20–30 V | | RCD rating | ≤ 30 mA | | Grounding cable | ≥ 6 mm² Cu | | LEV capture velocity | 0.5–1.0 m/s | | Shade (SMAW ≤ 200 A) | 10–12 | | Shade (SMAW 200–400 A) | 12–14 | | Shade (gas welding) | 4–6 | | Bystander restricted zone | 10 m | | Mn TLV | 0.02 mg/m³ | | Noise threshold for ear PPE | 85 dBA |

topics.title: Cutting and Finishing - Gas Cylinders and Equipments - Heat Treatment - Furnaces and Salt Baths - Operations and Maintenance - Safety in Handling and Storage of Salts - Disposal of Effluents - Health Precautions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT601 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cutting and Finishing - Gas Cylinders and Equipments - Heat Treatment - Furnaces and Salt Baths - Operations and Maintenance - Safety in Handling and Storage of Salts - Disposal of Effluents - Health Precautions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cutting and Finishing Operations in Industrial Hazard Context

**Cutting** in manufacturing refers to mechanical, thermal, or chemical separation processes used to remove excess material from a workpiece, producing the desired shape and size. **Finishing** operations are subsequent processes that improve surface roughness, dimensional accuracy, and aesthetic appearance of the cut component.

In the context of **Hazard Control in Industries**, cutting and finishing operations are critical because:
- They generate fine particulate matter (dust, fumes, swarf)
- They use high-energy sources (lasers, plasma, chemicals)
- They expose workers to sharp edges, noise, vibration, and toxic byproducts
- They frequently involve hazardous consumables (cutting fluids, solvents, acids)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Cutting and finishing are manufacturing sub-processes that introduce occupational hazards including mechanical injury, inhalation exposure, fire/explosion risks from lubricants, and chemical burns from finishing agents.

### Conceptual Analogy

Think of cutting and finishing like a **chef preparing a meal**. The chef first "cuts" the vegetables (rough work) and then "finishes" by garnishing and plating (fine work). Both stages require different tools and introduce different risks — a dull knife causes accidents, and improper cleaning chemicals make the chef sick. Similarly, in industry, the rough cutting tools and fine finishing chemicals each bring unique hazards that must be controlled.

---

## 1.2 Gas Cylinders and Equipment

**Compressed Gas Cylinders** are high-pressure vessels (typically designed for working pressures of **150 bar to 250 bar**) used to store gases permanently in gaseous or liquefied form for industrial applications.

### Classification of Industrial Gases by Hazard

| Class | Examples | Primary Hazard |
|---|---|---|
| **Flammable** | Acetylene, Hydrogen, LPG, Methane | Fire / Explosion |
| **Oxidizing** | Oxygen, Nitrous Oxide, Chlorine | Fire intensification |
| **Toxic** | Ammonia, Chlorine, Phosgene | Poisoning / Asphyxiation |
| **Inert (Asphyxiant)** | Nitrogen, Argon, CO₂ | Oxygen displacement |
| **Corrosive** | Hydrogen Chloride, Sulfur Dioxide | Chemical burns |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Acetylene cylinders are uniquely dangerous because acetylene is **unstable above 2 bar gauge pressure** in pure form. They are therefore dissolved in acetone (C₃H₆O) absorbed in a porous mass — this is why acetylene cylinders must NEVER be stored lying down (acetone could leak out and cause explosion risk).

---

## 1.3 Heat Treatment — Definition

**Heat Treatment** is a controlled process involving heating and cooling of metals/alloys in the solid state to obtain desired microstructural, mechanical, and physical properties without changing the product shape.

### Primary Heat Treatment Processes

1. **Annealing** — softening, stress relief
2. **Normalizing** — refines grain structure
3. **Hardening** — increases hardness via quenching
4. **Tempering** — reduces brittleness after hardening
5. **Case Hardening (Carburizing, Nitriding, Cyaniding)** — surface hardening
6. **Austempering / Martempering** — specialized quenching

---

## 1.4 Furnaces and Salt Baths

A **Furnace** is an enclosed chamber where temperature is regulated to perform heat treatment, melting, or other thermal processes.

A **Salt Bath Furnace** uses molten salts (typically chloride, nitrate, or cyanide-based mixtures) as the heating medium, where the workpiece is immersed for uniform heat transfer.

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine heating a metal rod over an open gas flame versus dipping it in a **hot oil bath** for frying. The bath gives much more **uniform and rapid heat transfer** without direct flame contact, and protects the metal from oxidation. Salt baths work exactly like that frying oil — except at temperatures of **550°C to 1300°C** with chemically specialized salts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Salt baths are classified as HIGH-HAZARD equipment** because of (a) extreme temperatures, (b) highly toxic salt constituents (cyanides), (c) splash/burn risk, and (d) toxic fume generation. KTU examiners frequently ask about cyanide salt bath hazards.

---

## 1.5 Operations, Maintenance, Safety & Health Precautions

**Operations and Maintenance (O&M)** encompasses the daily procedures, inspection routines, and preventive actions required to keep heat treatment equipment functional and safe.

**Health Precautions** refer to the personal and collective protective measures taken to safeguard workers from:
- Thermal burns
- Toxic fume inhalation
- Cyanide poisoning
- Salt splash to eyes/skin
- Fire and explosion
- Long-term diseases (cyanide-induced, respiratory issues)

### Effluents from Heat Treatment

Heat treatment operations generate two main waste streams:

1. **Liquid Effluents** — spent quench oils, rinse waters containing cyanides, nitrites, heavy metals (Cr, Ni, Cd), acids, alkalis
2. **Solid Effluents** — spent salt cakes, scale, sludge from salt bath pots, used refractories

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The course explicitly focuses on the **disposal of effluents** because cyanide, chromate, and nitrite salts are **priority pollutants** regulated under the Indian Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC Rules, 1989) and the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gas Cylinders — Detailed Operating Principles

### 2.1.1 Pressure-Temperature Relationship

Compressed gas cylinders are governed by the **Ideal Gas Law** (with compressibility factor corrections at high pressure):

$$
PV = ZnRT
$$

Where:
- $P$ = absolute pressure (Pa)
- $V$ = volume of gas (m³)
- $Z$ = compressibility factor (typically 0.8 to 1.1 at cylinder conditions)
- $n$ = number of moles
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **critical temperature** ($T_c$) and **critical pressure** ($P_c$) determine whether a gas can be liquefied inside a cylinder. Above $T_c$, the gas cannot be liquefied regardless of pressure.

### 2.1.2 Acetylene Cylinder — Special Design

Acetylene (C₂H₂) cylinder working pressure is limited to approximately **1.5 MPa (≈ 15 bar)** because pure acetylene is thermally unstable. The cylinder is filled with:
- A porous monolithic mass (calcium silicate / charcoal)
- Acetone solvent (≈ 400 g per litre of cylinder volume)
- Acetylene dissolved in acetone (≈ 28% saturation)

$$
\text{Volume of acetylene in cylinder} = V_{\text{acetone}} \times 0.28 \times \rho_{\text{acetone}}
$$

### 2.1.3 Color Coding of Cylinders (IS 4379 / ISO 32)

| Gas | Body Color | Shoulder Color |
|---|---|---|
| Oxygen | Black | White |
| Acetylene | Maroon | White |
| Hydrogen | Red | White |
| Nitrogen | Black | Black |
| Argon | Peacock Blue | Black |
| CO₂ | Aluminum | Black |
| Ammonia | Red | Yellow |
| Chlorine | Yellow | Yellow |

---

## 2.2 Heat Treatment Furnace Theory

### 2.2.1 Heat Transfer Modes in Furnaces

Heat flows from the heat source to the workpiece through three modes:

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{conduction}} + Q_{\text{convection}} + Q_{\text{radiation}}
$$

#### Conduction (Through Furnace Walls)
$$
Q_{\text{cond}} = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{d}
$$

Where $k$ = thermal conductivity of refractory (W/m·K), $A$ = area (m²), $d$ = wall thickness (m).

#### Convection (Hot Gases to Workpiece)
$$
Q_{\text{conv}} = hA(T_{\text{gas}} - T_{\text{workpiece}})
$$

Where $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (typically **10 to 200 W/m²·K** in furnace atmospheres).

#### Radiation (Dominant in High-Temperature Furnaces)
$$
Q_{\text{rad}} = \sigma A \varepsilon (T_h^4 - T_c^4)
$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = **Stefan-Boltzmann constant = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity of surface (0 to 1)
- $T_h$ = hot surface temperature (K)
- $T_c$ = cold surface temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** For temperatures above **600°C**, radiation is the dominant heat transfer mode. This is why furnace design focuses on element placement, reflector geometry, and refractory selection.

### 2.2.2 Furnace Efficiency

$$
\eta_{\text{furnace}} = \frac{Q_{\text{absorbed by charge}}}{Q_{\text{input (fuel/electricity)}}} \times 100\%
$$

Typical efficiencies:
- **Electric resistance furnace:** 60–80%
- **Gas-fired furnace:** 30–55%
- **Salt bath furnace:** 70–85% (excellent heat transfer)
- **Induction furnace:** 75–90%

---

## 2.3 Salt Bath Heat Treatment — Theory

### 2.3.1 Salt Bath Compositions by Temperature

| Temperature Range | Salt Type | Composition | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **150–550°C** | Nitrate-Nitrite | NaNO₃ + KNO₃ + NaNO₂ | Tempering, Austempering |
| **550–900°C** | Chloride | BaCl₂, NaCl, KCl | Hardening, Annealing |
| **900–1300°C** | Barium Chloride + others | BaCl₂, NaCl, KCl, CaCl₂ | High-speed steel hardening |
| **600–950°C** | Cyanide (Cyaniding) | NaCN, KCN, NaCl, BaCl₂ | Case hardening |
| **500–600°C** | Neutral salt | CaCl₂, NaCl | Neutral hardening |

### 2.3.2 Carburizing Reaction (in Cyanide Bath)

$$
2\text{NaCN} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NaCNO}
$$

$$
4\text{NaCNO} \rightarrow 2\text{NaCN} + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CO} + 2\text{N}
$$

The nascent nitrogen and CO diffuse into the steel surface, forming a hard case.

### 2.3.3 Heat Transfer in Salt Bath

Salt baths provide superior heat transfer because:

$$
h_{\text{salt bath}} \approx 1500 \text{ to } 5000 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}
$$

Compared to air or protective gas furnaces:

$$
h_{\text{air furnace}} \approx 10 \text{ to } 200 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Because of the high $h$ value, salt baths achieve **uniform heating in a fraction of the time** required by conventional furnaces. The trade-off is severe hazard from molten salt splash, fume toxicity (especially HCN from cyanide baths), and salt disposal issues.

### 2.3.4 Fick's Laws of Diffusion (Case Depth)

The depth of case hardening follows Fick's Second Law:

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}
$$

For semi-infinite solid with constant surface concentration, the solution gives:

$$
\frac{C_s - C_x}{C_s - C_0} = \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)
$$

Where:
- $C_s$ = surface concentration
- $C_x$ = concentration at depth $x$
- $C_0$ = initial bulk concentration
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient (m²/s)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $x$ = depth (m)

---

## 2.4 Effluent Treatment — Key Parameters

### 2.4.1 Cyanide Destruction Chemistry

**Alkaline Chlorination Method** (most common for industrial effluent):

$$
\text{NaCN} + \text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{NaCNO} + 2\text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
2\text{NaCNO} + 3\text{Cl}_2 + 4\text{NaOH} \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + \text{N}_2 + 6\text{NaCl} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **NEVER acidify cyanide-containing effluents.** Acid + Cyanide → **HCN (Hydrogen Cyanide) gas**, which is lethal at concentrations as low as **100–300 ppm**.

### 2.4.2 Standard Effluent Parameters

| Parameter | Unit | Permissible Limit (Inland Surface Water, India) |
|---|---|---|
| pH | — | 5.5 – 9.0 |
| Total Cyanide (CN⁻) | mg/L | 0.2 |
| Hexavalent Chromium (Cr⁶⁺) | mg/L | 0.1 |
| Total Chromium | mg/L | 2.0 |
| Nickel (Ni) | mg/L | 3.0 |
| Lead (Pb) | mg/L | 0.1 |
| Oil & Grease | mg/L | 10 |
| BOD (3 days, 27°C) | mg/L | 30 |
| COD | mg/L | 250 |
| TSS | mg/L | 100 |

> [!NOTE]
> These limits are taken from the **Environmental (Protection) Rules, 1986 (Schedule VI)** — frequently cited in KTU board exams.

---

## 2.5 Health Hazard Metrics in Heat Treatment

### 2.5.1 Threshold Limit Values (TLV — ACGIH)

| Substance | TLV-TWA (ppm) | TLV-STEL (ppm) | Health Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| HCN | 4.7 | 10 | Rapid asphyxiation |
| NaCN (as CN) | — | 5 mg/m³ ceiling | Thyroid/cardiac |
| NaOH | — | 2 mg/m³ ceiling | Severe respiratory |
| CO | 25 | — | Hypoxia |
| NO₂ | 0.5 | 1 | Pulmonary edema |
| CrO₃ (Cr⁶⁺) | 0.05 mg/m³ | — | Lung carcinogen |
| Mineral oil mist | 5 mg/m³ | 10 mg/m³ | Pneumonia, skin cancer |

### 2.5.2 Cyanide Poisoning — Dose Response

$$
\text{Lethal oral dose (NaCN)} \approx 200 \text{ to } 300 \text{ mg (adult human)}
$$

$$
\text{Lethal air concentration (HCN)} \approx 100 \text{ to } 300 \text{ ppm for 30–60 min}
$$

$$
\text{IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life and Health)} = 50 \text{ ppm (HCN)}
$$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Ideal Gas Law | $PV = ZnRT$ | Cylinder pressure/temperature |
| Acetylene storage | $V = V_{\text{acetone}} \times 0.28$ | Acetylene cylinder capacity |
| Conduction heat transfer | $Q = kA(T_1 - T_2)/d$ | Furnace wall losses |
| Convection heat transfer | $Q = hA(T_g - T_w)$ | Gas-to-workpiece heating |
| Stefan-Boltzmann radiation | $Q = \sigma A \varepsilon (T_h^4 - T_c^4)$ | High-temperature furnace |
| Furnace efficiency | $\eta = Q_{\text{charge}}/Q_{\text{input}}$ | Energy audit |
| Case depth (Fick's law) | $\frac{C_s - C_x}{C_s - C_0} = \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$ | Carburizing/nitriding depth |
| Cyanide chlorination | $\text{CN}^- + \text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{CNO}^- + 2\text{Cl}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Effluent treatment |
| BOD | $\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2)}{P}$ | Effluent organic load |
| Lethal dose (NaCN) | $LD_{50} \approx 6.4 \text{ mg/kg (oral, rat)}$ | Toxicity assessment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** When using $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) or any vertical bar notation in a written answer, render it as $\lvert x \vert$ to ensure the equation compiles correctly in LaTeX. The pipe symbol in plain text breaks markdown tables.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles above directly drive decisions in:

1. **Industrial Furnace Design** — selecting refractory, fuel, and insulation
2. **Heat Treatment Shops (e.g., forging industries, automotive)** — process selection, safety audits
3. **Effluent Treatment Plants (ETP)** — sizing chlorination tanks, settling ponds
4. **Compressed Gas Distribution** — pipeline sizing, pressure regulation
5. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) Specification** — respirator cartridge selection
6. **OSHA / Factory Act Compliance Audits** — permissible exposure limits
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heating Time in a Salt Bath Furnace

### Problem Statement
A steel component of thickness $L$ = **0.05 m** is to be hardened by immersion in a salt bath at $T_b$ = **850°C (1123 K)**. The initial temperature of the steel is $T_0$ = **25°C (298 K)**. The salt bath convective heat transfer coefficient is $h$ = **2000 W/m²·K**. The thermal conductivity of steel is $k_{steel}$ = **45 W/m·K**, density $\rho$ = **7850 kg/m³**, specific heat $c_p$ = **486 J/(kg·K)**. Calculate the time required for the centre of the slab to reach **750°C (1023 K)**. Use lumped capacitance assumption.

### Step 1: Apply Energy Balance on the Slab

The rate of heat input to the slab = Rate of heat storage in the slab:

$$
hA(T_b - T(t)) = \rho V c_p \frac{dT}{dt}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{dT}{T_b - T} = \frac{hA}{\rho V c_p} dt
$$

### Step 2: Define Biot Number to Check Lumped Capacitance Validity

$$
\text{Bi} = \frac{h L_c}{k_{steel}}
$$

For a slab of thickness $L$ with surface on both sides, characteristic length:

$$
L_c = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{0.05}{2} = 0.025 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\text{Bi} = \frac{2000 \times 0.025}{45} = \frac{50}{45} \approx 1.11
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since Bi $\approx$ 1.11 > 0.1, the lumped capacitance method is **marginally invalid** (lumped capacitance requires Bi < 0.1). For a more accurate solution, the Heisler chart or one-term Fourier series must be used. However, KTU 2024 examinations frequently allow the lumped capacitance approach as a **first-order approximation** if explicitly stated.

### Step 3: Lumped Capacitance Time Equation

Integrating both sides with limits $T_0$ to $T(t)$:

$$
\int_{T_0}^{T(t)} \frac{dT}{T_b - T} = \frac{hA}{\rho V c_p}\int_0^t dt
$$

$$
-\ln(T_b - T) \Big|_{T_0}^{T(t)} = \frac{hA}{\rho V c_p} t
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{T_b - T_0}{T_b - T(t)}\right) = \frac{hA}{\rho V c_p} t
$$

Solving for $t$:

$$
t = \frac{\rho V c_p}{hA} \ln\left(\frac{T_b - T_0}{T_b - T(t)}\right)
$$

Since $V/A = L_c$:

$$
t = \frac{\rho c_p L_c}{h} \ln\left(\frac{T_b - T_0}{T_b - T(t)}\right)
$$

### Step 4: Substitute Numerical Values

$$
\rho c_p = 7850 \times 486 = 3.815 \times 10^6 \text{ J/(m}^3\text{·K)}
$$

$$
\frac{\rho c_p L_c}{h} = \frac{3.815 \times 10^6 \times 0.025}{2000} = \frac{95{,}375}{2000} = 47.69 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_b - T_0 = 1123 - 298 = 825 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_b - T(t) = 1123 - 1023 = 100 \text{ K}
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{825}{100}\right) = \ln(8.25) = 2.110
$$

### Step 5: Final Time Calculation

$$
t = 47.69 \times 2.110 = 100.6 \text{ seconds} \approx \mathbf{1.68 \text{ minutes}}
$$

### Step 6: Engineering Validation

- A 50 mm thick steel slab requires only ~1.7 minutes in a salt bath to reach near-bath temperature.
- This is **5–10 times faster** than a gas-fired furnace.
- Practical hardening time includes a **soaking period** of 5–15 minutes for metallurgical transformation, so total time is more like 7–17 minutes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "soaking time"?** The temperature must be held at austenitizing temperature long enough for carbon/nitrogen to dissolve uniformly in the austenite phase. This is typically $\frac{1}{2}$ hour per inch of thickness (rule of thumb).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Case Depth in Carburizing (Fick's Law Application)

### Problem Statement
A low-carbon steel (0.2% C) is carburized in a salt bath at **920°C**. The surface carbon concentration becomes 1.0% C. Calculate the time required to obtain a carbon concentration of **0.6% C** at a depth of **0.5 mm** below the surface.

Given data: $D$ (diffusion coefficient of C in γ-Fe at 920°C) = **1.5 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s**

### Step 1: Identify Parameters

- $C_s = 1.0\%$
- $C_0 = 0.2\%$
- $C_x = 0.6\%$
- $x = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- $D = 1.5 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

### Step 2: Apply Fick's Second Law Solution

$$
\frac{C_s - C_x}{C_s - C_0} = \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{1.0 - 0.6}{1.0 - 0.2} = \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{0.4}{0.8} = 0.5 = \text{erf}(\eta)
$$

Where $\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$.

### Step 3: Find Argument of Error Function

From standard error function tables:

$$
\text{erf}(0.477) \approx 0.5
$$

So $\eta = 0.477$.

### Step 4: Solve for Time

$$
\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} \Rightarrow \eta \cdot 2\sqrt{Dt} = x
$$

$$
\sqrt{Dt} = \frac{x}{2\eta} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 0.477} = 5.24 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}
$$

$$
Dt = (5.24 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 2.746 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
t = \frac{2.746 \times 10^{-7}}{1.5 \times 10^{-11}} = 1.83 \times 10^{4} \text{ s}
$$

### Step 5: Convert to Hours

$$
t = \frac{1.83 \times 10^4}{3600} = \mathbf{5.08 \text{ hours}}
$$

### Step 6: Cross-Validation

This is consistent with industrial practice where **gas carburizing** at 920°C takes **4–8 hours** for a 0.5 mm case depth.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Acetylene Cylinder Pressure vs. Temperature

### Problem Statement
An acetylene cylinder is filled at **15°C (288 K)** with a pressure of **1.5 MPa (gauge)**. If the cylinder is exposed to a fire and reaches **60°C (333 K)**, what is the new pressure? (Assume acetylene behaves as an ideal gas dissolved in acetone with $Z \approx 1$.)

### Step 1: Apply Ideal Gas Relationship at Constant Volume

$$
\frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2}
$$

### Step 2: Convert Gauge to Absolute Pressure

$$
P_{1,\text{abs}} = 1.5 \text{ MPa} + 0.101 \text{ MPa (atmospheric)} = 1.601 \text{ MPa}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute

$$
\frac{1.601}{288} = \frac{P_2}{333}
$$

$$
P_2 = 1.601 \times \frac{333}{288} = 1.601 \times 1.156 = 1.852 \text{ MPa (abs)}
$$

$$
P_{2,\text{gauge}} = 1.852 - 0.101 = 1.751 \text{ MPa} \approx \mathbf{17.5 \text{ bar}}
$$

### Step 4: Safety Implication

> [!WARNING]
> A **17°C rise** (from 15°C to 60°C) increased pressure by **16.7%**. If exposed to fire, the cylinder could rupture catastrophically. This is why acetylene cylinders must be **kept away from heat sources**, and the **safety fuse plug** melts at **~100°C** to release acetylene safely.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hazard-Control Decision Tree

| Hazard Scenario | Primary Control | Secondary Control | Tertiary Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acetylene cylinder overheating | Pressure relief device | Cool water spray | Distance ≥ 10 m |
| Salt bath splash | Operator PPE (face shield, apron) | Splash guards | Emergency shower |
| HCN fume generation | Local exhaust ventilation | Cyanide-free salt substitution | HCN gas detector |
| Furnace refractory failure | Temperature monitoring | Regular inspection | Emergency shutdown |
| Quench oil fire | Fire-resistant oil (Class IIIB) | Automatic sprinklers | Foam extinguisher |
| Cyanide effluent leak | pH control (alkaline) | Alkaline chlorination | ETP barrier |
| Hexavalent chrome in effluent | Reduce Cr⁶⁺ to Cr³⁺ | Precipitation as Cr(OH)₃ | Sludge landfill |
| Electric shock (furnace) | ELCB / RCD | Double insulation | PPE (insulated gloves) |

---

## 3.5 Conversion & Sanity Check Tools

### 3.5.1 Temperature Conversion

$$
T(\text{K}) = T(\text{°C}) + 273.15
$$

$$
T(\text{°C}) = \frac{5}{9}\left[T(\text{°F}) - 32\right]
$$

### 3.5.2 Pressure Conversion

$$
1 \text{ atm} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 14.696 \text{ psi}
$$

### 3.5.3 Mass Concentration Conversion (Cyanide)

$$
1 \text{ ppm} = 1 \text{ mg/L (for water, approx.)}
$$

### 3.5.4 Quick Heuristic for Case Hardening

$$
t_{\text{carburizing}} \approx \frac{x^2}{D}
$$

Where $x$ is case depth and $D$ is the diffusion coefficient. This gives a quick sanity check within an order of magnitude.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
List any **three color codes** used for compressed gas cylinder identification as per IS 4379, and state the significance of proper color coding in industrial safety.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Oxygen cylinder** — Black body with white shoulder — signifies non-flammable but strong oxidizer; prevents confusion with flammable gases during welding/heat treatment.
2. **Acetylene cylinder** — Maroon body with white shoulder — quick visual identification of a highly flammable gas that requires special storage and flashback arrestors.
3. **Ammonia cylinder** — Red body with yellow shoulder — indicates toxic and corrosive nature; mandates use of SCBA and chemical-resistant PPE.

**Significance (1 mark):** Prevents accidental mixing of flammable and oxidizing gases (which would cause explosion), supports emergency response, ensures correct regulator/fittings are used, and complies with IS 4379 / Factory Act 1948.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What are the **two main classes of heat treatment furnaces**? Give one example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Heat treatment furnaces are classified as:

1. **Batch-type furnaces** — Charge is loaded, processed, and unloaded in batches.
   - Example: Box furnace, Pit furnace, Bell furnace, Salt bath furnace.

2. **Continuous-type furnaces** — Workpieces move continuously through different temperature zones.
   - Example: Rotary hearth furnace, Walking beam furnace, Pusher furnace, Roller hearth furnace.

**Additional 1 mark for:** Naming that salt bath furnaces are batch-type and are preferred for case hardening due to high heat transfer coefficient.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Internal Choice Q1]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **safety precautions to be observed in the storage and handling of industrial gas cylinders** used in heat treatment and cutting operations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **principle, construction, and operation of a salt bath furnace**. List the hazards associated with molten salt bath operation and the corresponding control measures. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Storage Precautions (3.5 marks):**

1. **Location and Segregation**
   - Store in a well-ventilated, fire-resistant, dry shed.
   - Maintain minimum **6 m** separation between flammable gas cylinders (e.g., acetylene, hydrogen) and oxidizing gas cylinders (oxygen, nitrous oxide), or use a **fire wall of at least 1.5 m height** with 30-minute fire rating.
   - Keep cylinders away from heat sources, electrical circuits, and corrosives.

2. **Cylinder Positioning**
   - Always store cylinders **upright** and **chain them** to prevent tipping.
   - Keep the **valve protection cap** in place when not in use.
   - **Acetylene cylinders must NEVER be stored horizontally** — acetone loss would destabilize the gas and create an explosion risk.

3. **Identification**
   - Color coding as per IS 4379.
   - Mark "MT" (Empty) on empty cylinders in chalk.
   - Maintain a stock register.

4. **Ventilation and Inspection**
   - Continuous mechanical ventilation at 6 air changes per hour.
   - Weekly inspection for leaks using soap solution (never use oil/grease on oxygen fittings).
   - Gas detectors for H₂, CH₄, CO.

**Handling Precautions (3.5 marks):**

1. **Transportation** — Use a proper cylinder trolley with chain. Never roll cylinders or use them as rollers. Never lift by the valve cap.

2. **Regulator and Hose Connection**
   - Use only the **correct regulator** for the gas.
   - Install **flashback arrestor** on the regulator outlet for oxy-fuel gases.
   - Use hose of standard color (black for acetylene, blue for oxygen).
   - Use **hose check valves** (non-return valves).

3. **Leak Testing**
   - Open valve slowly with regulator not connected.
   - Test connections with soap solution.
   - Never use a flame to detect leaks.

4. **Operational Discipline**
   - Purge regulators before use.
   - Close cylinder valve when not in use, even if regulator is closed.
   - Never use oil/grease on oxygen equipment — fire risk.
   - Maintain a **Permit-to-Work** system for hot work.

---

#### Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Principle (1 mark):**
A salt bath furnace uses **molten salts** as the heating medium. The workpiece is immersed in the molten salt, and heat is transferred primarily by **convection** with a very high heat transfer coefficient (1500–5000 W/m²·K), resulting in **rapid and uniform heating**.

**Construction (2 marks):**
- **Pot** — Fabricated from stainless steel (for nitrate baths up to 600°C) or ceramic/ceramic-lined (for chloride/cyanide baths up to 1300°C).
- **Heating system** — External gas burners, immersed electrodes (for low-voltage AC), or resistance elements around the pot.
- **Refractory lining** — Insulation between the pot and outer shell.
- **Thermocouples** — For control (TC1) and over-temperature protection (TC2).
- **Exhaust hood** — Captures fumes and vents to a wet scrubber.
- **Workpiece handling fixture** — Crane, hoist, or manual tongs.

**Operation (1.5 marks):**
1. Preheat the bath to operating temperature (e.g., 850°C for hardening).
2. Skim surface oxides/carbonates.
3. Preheat the workpiece to avoid thermal shock.
4. Immerse the workpiece in the molten salt for the calculated soaking time.
5. Remove and quench in oil/water/polymer.
6. Temper in a separate furnace.

**Hazards and Controls (2.5 marks):**

| Hazard | Control Measure |
|---|---|
| HCN fume from cyanide bath | Wet scrubber + HCN monitor + exhaust hood at 100 ft/min |
| Molten salt splash | Splash guards + full PPE (face shield, aluminized suit) |
| Thermal burn from bath | Emergency shower + first aid; never add wet workpieces |
| Explosion (water + molten salt) | Pre-dry workpieces; preheat before immersion |
| Refractory failure | Routine inspection; replace at scheduled intervals |
| Electric shock (electrode type) | ELCB + double insulation + lock-out-tag-out |
| Salt bath decomposition | Maintain correct composition; cover with charcoal; avoid contamination |

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Internal Choice Q2]**

**(a)** Describe the **health hazards associated with salt bath heat treatment operations**. Discuss the **medical surveillance and first aid measures** for cyanide exposure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **disposal methods for effluents generated from heat treatment industries**, with specific reference to cyanide, chromium, and oil-bearing wastewater. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Health Hazards (3.5 marks):**

1. **Acute Cyanide Poisoning** — Inhalation of HCN from cyanide salt baths (especially when moisture is present or bath is overheated above 850°C). Symptoms: headache, giddiness, vomiting, convulsions, respiratory failure. Lethal concentration 100–300 ppm for 30–60 min.

2. **Skin and Eye Burns** — Contact with molten salt (temperatures up to 1300°C) causes severe thermal and chemical burns.

3. **Respiratory Hazards** — Inhalation of salt dust, metallic oxides (CrO₃, NiO, Fe₂O₃ fumes), and nitrogen oxides from nitrate baths.

4. **Dermatological Effects** — Chromium salts cause **chrome ulcers** and **contact dermatitis**. Nickel salts cause **nickel itch**.

5. **Long-term Effects** — Chronic exposure to Cr⁶⁺ increases risk of **lung and nasal cancers**. Mineral oil mist exposure causes **pneumonia and skin cancer**.

6. **Heat Stress** — Operators working near 850°C+ baths for prolonged periods suffer dehydration, heat exhaustion, heat stroke.

7. **Cumulative Toxicity** — Cyanides disrupt cytochrome oxidase in the electron transport chain, leading to cellular hypoxia.

**Medical Surveillance (2 marks):**

1. Pre-employment medical examination including:
   - Complete blood count (CBC)
   - Liver and kidney function tests
   - Chest X-ray and pulmonary function tests (PFT)
   - Urine thiocyanate (SCN⁻) baseline (for cyanide-exposed workers)
2. Periodic health check-up **every 6 months** for high-exposure workers.
3. Biological monitoring — urine thiocyanate should be **< 50 mg/g creatinine** (ACGIH BEI).
4. Maintain occupational health records.

**First Aid for Cyanide Exposure (1.5 marks):**

1. **Move victim to fresh air immediately** — do not enter contaminated area without SCBA.
2. **Remove contaminated clothing** — prevent dermal absorption.
3. **Administer 100% oxygen**.
4. **Amyl nitrite ampoule** — crush and inhale for 15–30 seconds per minute (generates methemoglobin which binds CN⁻).
5. **Inject Sodium thiosulfate (25% solution, 50 mL IV)** — converts CN⁻ to thiocyanate (SCN⁻).
6. **Inject Sodium nitrite (3% solution, 10 mL IV)** — induces methemoglobinemia.
7. **Hydroxocobalamin (Cyanokit, 5g IV)** — modern preferred antidote, binds CN⁻ to form cyanocobalamin (Vitamin B12).
8. **Hospitalization** — even after apparent recovery, observation for 24–48 hours.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Marks Commonly Lost:**
> 1. Students often confuse **amyl nitrite (inhalation)** with **sodium nitrite (IV)** — examiners deduct marks.
> 2. Forgetting to mention that **antidotes must be administered by a trained medical professional**, not a fellow worker.
> 3. Failing to mention that **mouth-to-mouth resuscitation is dangerous** to the rescuer if HCN is the cause.
> 4. Not stating that **methemoglobin level should not exceed 20%** to avoid secondary toxicity.

---

#### Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Cyanide-Containing Effluents (2.5 marks):**

The **alkaline chlorination method** is most widely used:

**Stage 1 — Conversion of CN⁻ to CNO⁻:**
$$
\text{NaCN} + \text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{NaCNO} + 2\text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Stage 2 — Complete oxidation to CO₂ and N₂:**
$$
2\text{NaCNO} + 3\text{Cl}_2 + 4\text{NaOH} \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + \text{N}_2 + 6\text{NaCl} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Process conditions:**
- pH must be maintained at **10–11** (alkaline) to prevent HCN gas release.
- Use **sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl)** instead of Cl₂ gas for safer handling.
- Residual free chlorine must be **< 0.5 mg/L** before discharge.
- Final CN⁻ concentration: **< 0.2 mg/L** as per Indian standards.

**Alternative methods (½ mark):**
- Ozonation: O₃ + CN⁻ → CNO⁻ → CO₂ + N₂
- Electrochemical oxidation
- Wet air oxidation

**2. Hexavalent Chromium Effluents (2 marks):**

Two-step process:

**Step 1 — Reduction of Cr⁶⁺ to Cr³⁺** (at pH 2.0–2.5):
$$
\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 6\text{Fe}^{2+} + 14\text{H}^+ \rightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 6\text{Fe}^{3+} + 7\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$
(Or use SO₂ / NaHSO₃ as reducing agent)

**Step 2 — Precipitation as Cr(OH)₃** (at pH 8.0–9.0):
$$
\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{Cr(OH)}_3 \downarrow
$$

The green gelatinous sludge is filtered in a **filter press**, dried, and sent to a **Treatment, Storage, and Disposal Facility (TSDF)** for secure landfill.

**3. Oil-Bearing Effluents (1.5 marks):**

1. **Oil-water separation** — Gravity separator (API separator) removes free oil.
2. **Emulsion breaking** — Add alum or polyelectrolyte to break oil-in-water emulsion.
3. **Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** — Air bubbles attach to oil droplets and float them.
4. **Activated carbon adsorption** — Polishing step for trace oils.
5. **Final disposal** — Recovered oil is either re-used (if clean) or incinerated.

**General ETP Flow (1 mark):**
Equalization → Oil removal → Cyanide oxidation (Cl) → Cr⁶⁺ reduction → pH neutralization → Sludge settling → Filtration → Carbon polishing → Discharge.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Marks Commonly Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting pH** — students often write the cyanide equation but omit the critical pH = 10–11 condition. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Cr⁶⁺ equation error** — students often mistakenly write Cr⁶⁺ precipitation directly, skipping the reduction step.
> 3. **Mixing waste streams** — cyanide and chromium streams must NEVER be mixed in the same tank. Acidic chromium effluent will liberate HCN from cyanide effluent.
> 4. **Not mentioning TSDF** — sludge disposal destination is mandatory.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

### A. Cutting and Finishing
- ☐ Cutting = separation; Finishing = surface quality improvement
- ☐ Hazards: dust, fumes, sharp edges, chemicals, noise, vibration
- ☐ Controls: machine guarding, LEV, PPE, dust collection

### B. Gas Cylinders
- ☐ Color coding: Oxygen (Black/White), Acetylene (Maroon/White), Hydrogen (Red/White)
