- ☐ Acetylene dissolved in acetone — never lay cylinder horizontal
- ☐ Always use **flashback arrestor** and **hose check valve**
- ☐ Acetylene max working pressure ≈ 1.5 MPa gauge
- ☐ Storage: 6 m between flammable and oxidizing, or fire wall
- ☐ Cylinder must be chained upright with valve cap on
- ☐ Never use oil/grease on oxygen fittings

### C. Heat Treatment
- ☐ Annealing, Normalizing, Hardening, Tempering, Case Hardening
- ☐ Salt bath gives **5–10× faster** heating than air furnace
- ☐ Cyanide baths release **HCN** — highly toxic

### D. Furnaces
- ☐ Batch vs Continuous
- ☐ Above 600°C, **radiation** dominates
- ☐ Stefan-Boltzmann: $Q = \sigma A \varepsilon T^4$
- ☐ Salt bath $h$ ≈ 1500–5000 W/m²·K (vs. air 10–200)

### E. Salt Bath Hazards
- ☐ HCN fume (cyanide bath)
- ☐ Molten salt splash
- ☐ Water + molten salt = **violent explosion**
- ☐ Always preheat and dry workpieces

### F. Effluent Treatment
- ☐ **Cyanide**: alkaline chlorination (pH 10–11), NaOCl or Cl₂
- ☐ **Cr⁶⁺**: reduce to Cr³⁺, then precipitate at pH 8.5
- ☐ **Oil**: API separator → DAF → carbon
- ☐ Final CN⁻ < 0.2 mg/L, Cr⁶⁺ < 0.1 mg/L
- ☐ Sludge → TSDF landfill

### G. Health Precautions
- ☐ HCN antidote: **Amyl nitrite (inhale) + Sodium thiosulphate (IV)**
- ☐ Modern antidote: **Hydroxocobalamin (Cyanokit)**
- ☐ Urine thiocyanate BEI < 50 mg/g creatinine
- ☐ Acutely lethal HCN: 100–300 ppm for 30 min
- ☐ PPE: face shield, aluminized suit, heat-resistant gloves, SCBA near bath

### H. Critical Equations to Memorize
- ☐ $PV = ZnRT$ — gas cylinders
- ☐ $Q = \sigma A \varepsilon (T_h^4 - T_c^4)$ — furnace radiation
- ☐ $\text{erf}(\eta) = \frac{C_s - C_x}{C_s - C_0}$ — case depth
- ☐ $\text{CN}^- + \text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{CNO}^- + 2\text{Cl}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ — effluent treatment

### I. Indian Standards to Remember
- ☐ **IS 4379** — Cylinder color coding
- ☐ **IS 8198** — Salt bath furnaces safety
- ☐ **IS 1446** — Classification of dangerous goods
- ☐ **Factories Act 1948, Section 14** — Safety measures
- ☐ **MSIHC Rules 1989** — Manufacture, Storage, Import of Hazardous Chemicals
- ☐ **EPA 1986, Schedule VI** — Effluent discharge standards

### J. Common Examiner Traps
- ☐ **NEVER acidify cyanide effluent** → HCN release
- ☐ **Acetylene is unstable above 2 bar pure** → must dissolve in acetone
- ☐ **Chrome ulcers** are from Cr⁶⁺, not Cr³⁺
- ☐ **Martempering ≠ Austempering** — do not confuse in case-hardening questions

---

## 5.5 Suggested 1-Page Mnemonic for Exam Hall

> **C**olor-code cylinders
> **Y**ellow for chlorine, Maroon for acetylene
> **A**lkaline chlorination kills cyanide
> **N**ever add water to molten salt
> **N**ever mix cyanide with chromium
> **E**xhaust hood above every salt bath
> **L**ethal HCN at 100 ppm in 30 min
> **E**mergency shower + amyl nitrite
> **D**ouble-check pH before effluent discharge
> **P**PE — last line of defense, not first

**Mnemonic: "CYANNE LED-P"** — recite before entering the exam hall!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exposure to Hazardous Fumes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT601 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Exposure to Hazardous Fumes # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Hazardous Fumes In the context of industrial hygiene and occupational safety engineering, **hazardous fumes** are defined as finely divided solid particles (typically less than **1 micrometre (µm)** in aerodynamic diameter) formed by the condensation of volatilized material after the vaporization of molten metals, welding operations, smelting, or combustion of organic substances. These airborne particulates represent a distinct category of **airborne contaminants** (also called *airborne hazards* or *respiratory hazards*) and are scientifically classified under the umbrella of **aerosols**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Box:** > *Hazardous fumes are airborne solid particulates generated by industrial processes such as welding, smelting, soldering, brazing, metal cutting, and combustion. They are characterized by sub-micron particle size, high surface-area-to-mass ratio, and potential to cause acute and chronic respiratory, systemic, and neurological health effects upon occupational exposure.* The **Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA)** under 29 CFR 1910.1000 and the **American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH)** formally recognize hazardous fumes as a sub-class of airborne particulates requiring strict airborne exposure monitoring. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Soup" Inside a Workshop Imagine walking into a busy welding workshop. You cannot see individual particles, but the air feels "heavy" and you notice a metallic taste on your tongue. That invisible cloud is essentially a **soup of suspended microscopic metallic oxides** (e.g., iron oxide, manganese oxide, chromium VI, zinc oxide) that have been boiled off the weld pool and re-condensed into ultra-fine solids. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Campfire Smoke" Comparison:** > Think of a campfire. The wood burns (combustion), volatile gases rise, condense in the cooler air above, and form the visible blue-grey smoke. Now shrink the campfire down to a weld pool of molten steel at 3000°C. The same physics occurs, but the particles are so small (sub-micron) that they remain suspended in air for hours, easily penetrate deep into the alveoli of the lungs, and may even cross into the bloodstream. The crucial point: **fumes are NOT gases and NOT vapours**. They are solid particulates in air — and this distinction is the single most common exam trap in KTU questions. ## 1.3 The Four-Family Classification of Airborne Contaminants | Term | Physical State | Particle Size | Example | |------|----------------|---------------|---------| | **Gas** | Molecular dispersion | < 0.001 µm | Carbon monoxide (CO), Chlorine (Cl₂) | | **Vapour** | Liquid evaporated into gas | Molecular scale | Benzene vapour, Mercury vapour | | **Fume** | Solid condensed from vapour | 0.001 – 1 µm | Welding fume, Zinc oxide fume | | **Dust** | Solid mechanically produced | 1 – 100 µm | Coal dust, Grain dust, Silica dust | | **Mist** | Liquid droplet | 1 – 100 µm | Acid mist, Paint spray mist | | **Fibre** | Solid elongated particle | Length > 5 µm, Aspect ratio > 3:1 | Asbestos, Ceramic fibre | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Pearl:** Fumes and dusts are both *particulates*, but **fumes originate from a vapour → solid phase change** (combustion/condensation), while **dusts originate from mechanical disintegration** (crushing, grinding, blasting). Examiners frequently test this distinction. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Reference Constants - **Threshold Limit Value – Time-Weighted Average (TLV-TWA):** **8-hour time-weighted average exposure** that should not be exceeded. Unit: **ppm** (parts per million) for gases/vapours, **mg/m³** for particulates. - **Threshold Limit Value – Short-Term Exposure Limit (TLV-STEL):** **15-minute time-weighted average** that should not be exceeded even if the 8-hour TWA is within limits. - **Threshold Limit Value – Ceiling (TLV-C):** Absolute upper limit that must never be exceeded. - **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL):** OSHA-enforceable legal limit. - **Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health (IDLH):** Concentration above which an unprotected worker faces immediate or delayed irreversible health effects. - **Lethal Concentration 50 (LC₅₀):** Concentration at which **50% of test animals die** within a specified exposure period (typically 4 hours for inhalation toxicity). Unit: **ppm or mg/m³**. - **Lethal Dose 50 (LD₅₀):** Single dose at which **50% of test animals die**. Unit: **mg/kg body weight**. **Universal Constants for Air:** - Standard temperature: **25 °C (298 K)** - Standard pressure: **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** - Molar volume of ideal gas at STP: **24.45 L/mol** (at 25 °C, 1 atm) - Conversion factor: **1 ppm (v/v) = (M/24.45) mg/m³** where M is molecular weight in g/mol # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Pathway of Fume Exposure The complete exposure pathway can be broken into **six sequential steps**, each of which is a KTU favourite for short-answer questions: 1. **Generation / Source:** Fumes are generated at the source — e.g., the electric arc of a Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) process vaporizes the electrode coating and base metal at temperatures exceeding **3000 °C**. 2. **Dispersion / Migration:** Air currents in the workplace transport the fumes. The rate of dispersion is governed by the **General Ventilation Equation** and the **Aerosol Dynamics Equation**. 3. **Contact / Inhalation:** The worker inhales contaminated air through the respiratory tract (nose, mouth, pharynx, larynx, trachea, bronchi, bronchioles, alveoli). 4. **Deposition / Absorption:** Particles deposit in the respiratory system by **impaction, sedimentation, diffusion, and electrostatic precipitation**. Fumes (< 1 µm) predominantly reach the **alveolar region** via diffusion (Brownian motion). 5. **Systemic Distribution:** Soluble fume components dissolve in lung fluid, cross the **alveolar-capillary membrane**, and enter the bloodstream, distributing to target organs (brain, liver, kidney, bone). 6. **Biological Effect:** The toxicant interacts with cellular targets, producing **acute, chronic, local, or systemic effects**. ## 2.2 Toxicological Dose-Response Framework The fundamental axiom of toxicology is: > **"The Dose Makes the Poison"** — Paracelsus (1538) The **Haber's Law** formalizes this for inhalation toxicology: $$C \times t = k$$ where: - $C$ = concentration of toxicant (ppm or mg/m³) - $t$ = duration of exposure (minutes or hours) - $k$ = constant for a given toxicological endpoint (e.g., LC₅₀) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** Haber's Law is used by industrial hygienists to predict whether a brief excursion to a high concentration (e.g., 500 ppm for 5 min) is equivalent to a chronic low exposure (e.g., 25 ppm for 100 min). For many irritant gases Haber's Law holds; for systemic toxicants (e.g., benzene) it is often modified because detoxification mechanisms are saturable. ## 2.3 Routes of Occupational Exposure | Route | Description | Example | Engineering Control | |-------|-------------|---------|--------------------| | **Inhalation** | Entry via respiratory tract | Welding fume inhalation | Local exhaust ventilation (LEV) | | **Skin (Dermal) Absorption** | Penetration through intact skin | Aniline, Phenol, Organophosphates | Protective gloves, Aprons | | **Ingestion** | Hand-to-mouth contact | Lead dust on fingers | Hand washing, No eating policy | | **Ocular** | Splash or airborne dust | Acid mist in eye | Safety goggles, Face shield | | **Parenteral** | Through cuts/wounds | Needle stick, Sharp injury | First aid, Puncture-resistant gloves | > [!IMPORTANT] > In fume exposure, **inhalation is the dominant route (>90% of occupational poisonings)**. Therefore, the rest of this module focuses on respiratory exposure. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | Application | |---|---------|-------------|-------|-------------| | 1 | $C_{ppm} = \frac{V_{vapour}}{V_{air}} \times 10^6$ | ppm definition | dimensionless | Gas/vapour conc. | | 2 | $C_{mg/m^3} = \frac{C_{ppm} \times M}{24.45}$ | ppm ↔ mg/m³ | mg/m³ | Unit conversion at 25°C, 1 atm | | 3 | $C_{ppm} = \frac{24.45 \times C_{mg/m^3}}{M}$ | mg/m³ ↔ ppm | ppm | Inverse conversion | | 4 | $TWA = \frac{\sum C_i \times t_i}{\sum t_i}$ | Time-weighted average | ppm or mg/m³ | Compliance check | | 5 | $C \times t = k$ | Haber's Law | varies | Toxic equivalency | | 6 | $Q = \frac{G}{C_{TLV} - C_{background}}$ | Ventilation rate | m³/s | LEV design | | 7 | $V_{captured} = Q \times A$ | Capture volume | m³/s | Hood design | | 8 | $D = C \times IR \times t$ | Exposure dose | mg | Dose calculation | | 9 | $IR = f_r \times V_t$ | Inhalation rate | m³/min | Respiratory minute volume | | 10 | $R = \frac{C_{measured}}{TLV} \times 100\%$ | Risk index / Hazard ratio | % | Compliance margin | | 11 | $D_{50} = \rho_p \times d_p^2 \times \frac{C_c}{18\mu}$ | Stokes settling velocity | m/s | Particle deposition | | 12 | $n = 3.22 \times 10^{25}$ | Loschmidt's number | molecules/m³ | Ideal gas at STP | | 13 | $V_m = 22.414$ L/mol | Molar volume at STP (0°C) | L/mol | Gas stoichiometry | | 14 | $V_m = 24.45$ L/mol | Molar volume at 25°C, 1 atm | L/mol | Hygiene calculations | | 15 | $C_o = C_i \times e^{-(Q/V) \times t}$ | Ventilation decay | varies | Time to reach threshold | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formula Notation Glossary:** > - $C$ = concentration; $M$ = molecular weight (g/mol); $TWA$ = time-weighted average > - $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s); $G$ = mass generation rate (mg/s); $A$ = capture area (m²) > - $IR$ = inhalation rate; $f_r$ = breathing frequency (breaths/min); $V_t$ = tidal volume (L/breath) > - $\rho_p$ = particle density; $d_p$ = particle diameter; $C_c$ = Cunningham slip correction factor > - $\mu$ = air dynamic viscosity ($\approx 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s at 25°C) > - $C_o$ = outlet concentration; $C_i$ = initial concentration; $V$ = room volume ## 2.5 Engineering Significance In real industrial settings, the engineer must answer four practical questions: 1. **How much fume is being generated?** (Source characterization by air sampling) 2. **What concentration reaches the worker?** (Air monitoring + modeling) 3. **Is the exposure within the TLV?** (Compliance assessment) 4. **What engineering controls reduce the exposure?** (LEV, substitution, isolation, PPE) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Application Domains:** > - Welding fume compliance in shipyards and structural fabrication > - Soldering fume (rosin, lead) in electronics manufacturing > - Smelting fume (SO₂, metal oxides) in metal refineries > - Combustion fume (CO, NOₓ, PAH) in fire and explosion investigations > - Pharmaceutical dust/fume in API manufacturing # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between ppm and mg/m³ The conversion of gas/vapour concentration from **ppm (molar basis)** to **mg/m³ (mass basis)** is a cornerstone calculation in industrial hygiene. The complete derivation is presented below. ### 3.1.1 Starting Point: Definition of ppm Concentration in parts per million is defined as the volume of contaminant per million volumes of air: $$C_{ppm} = \frac{V_{contaminant}}{V_{air}} \times 10^6$$ ### 3.1.2 Applying the Ideal Gas Law For an ideal gas, the volume occupied by $n$ moles at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$ is: $$V = \frac{nRT}{P}$$ where $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) is the universal gas constant. ### 3.1.3 Expressing Mass in Terms of Moles The mass of contaminant is related to moles by: $$m = n \times M$$ where $M$ is the molecular weight (g/mol or kg/mol). ### 3.1.4 Deriving the Conversion Formula The mass concentration in mg/m³ is: $$C_{mg/m^3} = \frac{m_{contaminant}}{V_{air}} = \frac{n \times M}{V_{air}}$$ From the ppm definition, the mole fraction is: $$\frac{n}{V_{air}} = \frac{C_{ppm}}{V_{molar} \times 10^6}$$ where $V_{molar}$ is the molar volume (L/mol or m³/mol). Substituting: $$C_{mg/m^3} = \frac{C_{ppm} \times M}{V_{molar} \times 10^6}$$ At standard conditions for industrial hygiene (**25 °C, 1 atm**), $V_{molar}$ = **24.45 L/mol** = **0.02445 m³/mol**. Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} C_{mg/m^3} &= \frac{C_{ppm} \times M}{(0.02445) \times 10^6} \\ C_{mg/m^3} &= \frac{C_{ppm} \times M}{24.45} \end{aligned} $$ > This is the **industry-standard conversion factor**. The 24.45 comes from converting m³ to L and dividing by $10^6$. **Inverse formula:** $$C_{ppm} = \frac{24.45 \times C_{mg/m^3}}{M}$$ ### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A workplace contains **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** at a measured concentration of **50 ppm**. Molecular weight of CO = **28.01 g/mol**. Convert to mg/m³. **Solution Step-by-Step:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{mg/m^3} &= \frac{C_{ppm} \times M}{24.45} \\ C_{mg/m^3} &= \frac{50 \times 28.01}{24.45} \\ C_{mg/m^3} &= \frac{1400.5}{24.45} \\ C_{mg/m^3} &\approx 57.28 \text{ mg/m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ The TLV-TWA for CO per ACGIH is **25 ppm** (= **29 mg/m³**). Therefore, the workplace exposure **exceeds the TLV by 100%**, mandating immediate engineering control intervention. ## 3.2 Derivation: Time-Weighted Average (TWA) Calculation The OSHA/ACGIH method for an 8-hour TWA is: $$TWA_{8h} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \times t_i}{8 \text{ hours}}$$ ### 3.2.1 Worked Example: Welder's Daily Exposure A welder performs the following tasks in an 8-hour shift: | Task | Duration $t_i$ (hr) | Concentration $C_i$ (mg/m³) | |------|---------------------|------------------------------| | SMAW welding | 3.0 | 12.0 | | Grinding | 1.5 | 4.0 | | Rest break | 0.5 | 0.5 | | Inspection | 2.0 | 1.5 | | Cleaning | 1.0 | 0.0 | Total accounted time = 3.0 + 1.5 + 0.5 + 2.0 + 1.0 = **8.0 hours ✓** **Calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Numerator} &= (12.0 \times 3.0) + (4.0 \times 1.5) + (0.5 \times 0.5) + (1.5 \times 2.0) + (0.0 \times 1.0) \\ &= 36.0 + 6.0 + 0.25 + 3.0 + 0.0 \\ &= 45.25 \text{ mg·hr/m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ $$ TWA_{8h} = \frac{45.25}{8.0} = 5.66 \text{ mg/m}^3 $$ If the TLV-TWA for total welding fume is **5.0 mg/m³** (ACGIH), the exposure is **non-compliant** (5.66 > 5.0), requiring controls. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Common Student Error:** Many students forget to divide by 8 hours when the cumulative time is already 8 hours. The denominator is always 8 hours (the standard shift), not the sum of exposure durations. If the cumulative exposure duration is less than 8 hours (e.g., 6 hours of varied tasks), the formula still divides by 8 hours to project to a full-shift equivalent. ## 3.3 Derivation: Required Ventilation Flow Rate (Q) For a contaminant generated at a constant rate $G$ (mg/s) in a well-mixed room, the steady-state mass balance gives: $$G = Q \times (C_{room} - C_{supply})$$ Rearranging for the required ventilation flow rate to keep the room concentration at the TLV: $$Q = \frac{G}{C_{TLV} - C_{background}}$$ ### 3.3.1 Worked Example: Soldering Fume LEV Design A soldering station generates rosin fume at $G$ = **15 mg/s**. The TLV-C for rosin-core solder pyrolysis products (as formaldehyde) is **0.1 mg/m³** (ceiling). The supply air is clean ($C_{background} = 0$). Determine the required LEV flow rate. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= \frac{G}{C_{TLV} - C_{background}} \\ Q &= \frac{15 \text{ mg/s}}{0.1 \text{ mg/m}^3 - 0 \text{ mg/m}^3} \\ Q &= 150 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\ Q &= 9{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **minimum exhaust flow rate**. In practice, a safety factor of **1.5 to 2.0** is applied, giving an LEV design flow of **13,500 – 18,000 m³/min**. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Note:** Such high flow rates are achieved by **slot hoods** or **push-pull ventilation** placed within 1–2 duct diameters of the source. The capture velocity must exceed the contaminant velocity, typically **0.5 – 1.0 m/s** at the hood face. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Toxicological Profile of Common Industrial Fumes | Fume | Source | TLV-TWA (ACGIH) | IDLH (OSHA) | Critical Health Effect | Target Organ | |------|--------|------------------|--------------|------------------------|--------------| | Iron Oxide (Fe₂O₃) | Steel welding, grinding | **5 mg/m³** | 2,500 mg/m³ | Siderosis (benign pneumoconiosis) | Lung | | Manganese (Mn) | Steel welding (Mn-steel) | **0.02 mg/m³ (R)** | 500 mg/m³ | Manganism (Parkinson-like) | CNS, Lung | | Chromium VI [Cr(VI)] | Stainless steel welding | **0.0002 mg/m³** | 15 mg/m³ | Lung cancer | Lung, Skin | | Zinc Oxide (ZnO) | Galvanized welding (brass) | **2 mg/m³ (R)** | 500 mg/m³ | Metal fume fever (flu-like) | Lung (acute) | | Cadmium Oxide (CdO) | Cd-alloy welding | **0.002 mg/m³ (R)** | 9 mg/m³ | Pulmonary oedema, emphysema | Lung, Kidney | | Lead (Pb) | Soldering, smelting | **0.05 mg/m³** | 100 mg/m³ | Neuropathy, anaemia | CNS, Kidney, Blood | | Carbon Monoxide (CO) | Combustion, ICE | **25 ppm** | 1,200 ppm | Chemical asphyxia | Blood (Hb) | | Ozone (O₃) | MIG/TIG welding (UV) | **0.05 ppm** | 5 ppm | Pulmonary oedema | Lung | | Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂) | Welding, diesel exhaust | **0.2 ppm** | 20 ppm | Bronchiolitis obliterans | Lung | | Phosgene (COCl₂) | Chlorinated solvent pyrolysis | **0.1 ppm** | 2 ppm | Delayed pulmonary oedema | Lung | > *(R) = Respirable fraction; (I) = Inhalable fraction; ppm conversion factors are based on molecular weight M of each compound.* # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Fume Generation–Exposure–Effect Pathway (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD src1[SOURCE: Welding Arc]:::src src2[SOURCE: Soldering Iron]:::src src3[SOURCE: Smelting Furnace]:::src src4[SOURCE: Combustion Engine]:::src src1 --> phys[Physical Process:
Vaporization at 3000 C]:::process src2 --> phys src3 --> phys src4 --> phys phys --> oxid[Metal Oxide Formation:
Fe2O3, ZnO, CrO3, MnO2]:::chem oxid --> disp[Atmospheric Dispersion:
Aerosol Dynamics]:::env disp --> zone1[Breathing Zone
Concentration C]:::zone disp --> zone2[Background Zone
Concentration Cbg]:::zone zone1 --> resp[Inhalation Route:
Nasal to Alveolar]:::route zone2 --> resp resp --> dep[Particle Deposition:
Impaction, Sedimentation, Diffusion]:::bio dep --> alv[Alveolar Region:
PM less than 2.5 micrometre]:::bio alv --> abs[Absorption into Blood:
Alveolar Capillary Membrane]:::bio abs --> org1[Target Organ:
Central Nervous System]:::effect abs --> org2[Target Organ:
Lung Parenchyma]:::effect abs --> org3[Target Organ:
Kidney]:::effect abs --> org4[Target Organ:
Liver]:::effect org1 --> eff1[Acute: Headache, Confusion]:::effect org1 --> eff2[Chronic: Neuropathy, Parkinsonism]:::effect org2 --> eff3[Acute: Metal Fume Fever]:::effect org2 --> eff4[Chronic: Pneumoconiosis, Cancer]:::effect org3 --> eff5[Chronic: Renal Failure]:::effect org4 --> eff6[Chronic: Hepatotoxicity]:::effect classDef src fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000 classDef process fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B4500,color:#000000 classDef chem fill:#FFFFB0,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000000 classDef env fill:#B0E0FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 classDef zone fill:#DDA0FF,stroke:#4B0082,color:#FFFFFF classDef route fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000000 classDef bio fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000 classDef effect fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Hierarchy of Fume Exposure Controls (Engineering Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD L0[Elimination
Remove the process entirely]:::lvl1 L1[Substitution
Replace with less hazardous material]:::lvl1 L2[Engineering Controls
LEV, Enclosure, Ventilation, Automation]:::lvl2 L3[Administrative Controls
Rotation, Training, Reduced shift length]:::lvl3 L4[PPE
Respirators, Eye protection, Gloves]:::lvl4 L0 --> L1 L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L2 --> eng1[Local Exhaust Ventilation
Slot hood at source]:::detail L2 --> eng2[General Ventilation
Dilution airflow]:::detail L2 --> eng3[Enclosure
Total isolation booth]:::detail L2 --> eng4[Wet Suppression
Water curtain]:::detail L4 --> ppe1[Air Purifying Respirator
HEPA or Chemical cartridge]:::detail L4 --> ppe2[Supplied Air Respirator
SCBA or SAR]:::detail L4 --> ppe3[Powered Air Purifying
PAPR with TH3 hood]:::detail classDef lvl1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000 classDef lvl2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000000 classDef lvl3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4500,color:#000000 classDef lvl4 fill:#FF7F7F,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF classDef detail fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333333,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 Air Sampling and Monitoring System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Workplace Atmosphere]:::env --> B[Sample Inlet
Positioned in Breathing Zone]:::sample B --> C[Filter Cassette
5 micrometre PVC membrane]:::filter B --> D[Impinger / Sorbent Tube
For vapours]:::filter C --> E[Volumetric Pump
Flow rate 1 to 4 L per min]:::pump D --> E E --> F[Calibrated Flow Meter
Bubble meter or rotameter]:::meter C --> G[Laboratory Analysis
ICP-MS, AAS, XRF]:::lab D --> G G --> H[Concentration Result
mg per cubic metre]:::result F --> I[Total Volume Sampled
Time x Flow]:::result H --> J[Compliance Check
versus TLV TWA and STEL]:::eval I --> J J --> K{Compliant?}:::eval K -- YES --> L[Continue Monitoring
Periodic Schedule]:::ok K -- NO --> M[Implement Controls
LEV, Substitution, PPE]:::alert classDef env fill:#B0E0FF,stroke:#003366 classDef sample fill:#DDA0FF,stroke:#4B0082 classDef filter fill:#FFFFB0,stroke:#8B8000 classDef pump fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B4500 classDef meter fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400 classDef lab fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000 classDef result fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333333 classDef eval fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000 classDef ok fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 classDef alert fill:#FF7F7F,stroke:#8B0000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **"Distinguish between 'fumes' and 'dusts' as airborne contaminants in an industrial workplace, giving two examples of each."** **Model Answer (3 marks — distributed as 1.5 + 1.5):** - **Fumes** are solid airborne particulates formed by the **condensation of volatilized material** that has been vaporized at high temperatures (typically above the melting point of the metal or during combustion). Fume particles are usually **less than 1 micrometre in diameter** and originate from processes such as **welding, smelting, soldering, and metal refining**. Examples: **Welding fume (iron oxide, manganese oxide)** and **Zinc oxide fume from galvanizing**. - **Dusts** are solid airborne particulates produced by the **mechanical disintegration or crushing of solid material** at ordinary temperatures. Dust particles are typically **larger (1 to 100 micrometre)** and originate from processes such as **grinding, drilling, blasting, crushing, and handling of bulk solids**. Examples: **Coal dust in mining** and **Silica dust in stone crushing**. **Key Distinction:** Fumes = vapour-to-solid phase change (high temperature); Dusts = mechanical breakdown of solid (ordinary temperature). *(1 mark for the key distinction.)* ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **"What is TLV-STEL? How does it differ from TLV-TWA? Give one example."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **TLV-STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit):** It is the **15-minute time-weighted average concentration** of an airborne contaminant that should not be exceeded at any time during a workday, even if the 8-hour TWA is within acceptable limits. It is intended to protect against **acute effects** of short-duration exposures such as irritation, narcosis, or CNS depression. *(1.5 marks)* **Difference from TWA:** | Parameter | TLV-TWA | TLV-STEL | |-----------|---------|----------| | Averaging period | **8 hours** | **15 minutes** | | Protects against | Chronic / long-term effects | Acute / short-term effects | | Number per shift allowed | Continuous | **Maximum 4 exposures, separated by ≥ 60 min** | | Should not exceed | – | 5 ppm × TWA value as a guide | *(1 mark)* **Example:** For **Phosgene (COCl₂)**, TLV-TWA = **0.1 ppm**; TLV-STEL is **not separately established** because phosgene is governed by its TLV-C (ceiling) of 0.1 ppm. A better example: **Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S)** has TLV-TWA = 1 ppm and TLV-STEL = 5 ppm. *(0.5 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]` **"A welding workshop has the following exposure profile for a welder during an 8-hour shift. Calculate the 8-hour TWA and compare with the TLV-TWA for total welding fume. Comment on the compliance status. (a) [7 marks] Discuss the various types of Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) hoods used for controlling welding fume, with sketches/diagrams. (b) [7 marks]"** **Exposure Profile:** | Task | Duration (hr) | Welding Fume Conc. (mg/m³) | |------|----------------|------------------------------| | SMAW welding | 2.5 | 11.0 | | FCAW welding | 1.5 | 14.0 | | TIG welding | 1.0 | 3.0 | | Rest break | 0.5 | 0.5 | | Material handling | 1.5 | 1.0 | | Inspection | 1.0 | 0.8 | TLV-TWA for total welding fume (ACGIH) = **5.0 mg/m³** (respirable fraction). #### Part (a) Model Solution — TWA Calculation [7 marks] **Step 1: Verify total time.** $$ \sum t_i = 2.5 + 1.5 + 1.0 + 0.5 + 1.5 + 1.0 = 8.0 \text{ hours} \checkmark $$ **Step 2: Calculate the numerator (concentration × time products).** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Product}_1 &= 11.0 \times 2.5 = 27.5 \\ \text{Product}_2 &= 14.0 \times 1.5 = 21.0 \\ \text{Product}_3 &= 3.0 \times 1.0 = 3.0 \\ \text{Product}_4 &= 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25 \\ \text{Product}_5 &= 1.0 \times 1.5 = 1.5 \\ \text{Product}_6 &= 0.8 \times 1.0 = 0.8 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Sum the products.** $$ \sum C_i t_i = 27.5 + 21.0 + 3.0 + 0.25 + 1.5 + 0.8 = 54.05 \text{ mg·hr/m}^3 $$ **Step 4: Apply the TWA formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} TWA_{8h} &= \frac{\sum C_i \times t_i}{8 \text{ hours}} \\ TWA_{8h} &= \frac{54.05}{8.0} \\ TWA_{8h} &= 6.756 \text{ mg/m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Compliance assessment.** $$ \text{Hazard Ratio} = \frac{TWA_{8h}}{TLV-TWA} = \frac{6.756}{5.0} = 1.351 = 135.1\% $$ **Conclusion:** The exposure is **non-compliant** (exceeds the TLV-TWA by **35.1%**). The welder is over-exposed, and engineering controls (LEV), administrative controls, or PPE must be implemented immediately. *(Marks distribution: 1 mark for verifying time; 2 marks for products; 1 mark for sum; 1 mark for TWA value; 1 mark for hazard ratio; 1 mark for compliance conclusion and recommendation.)* #### Part (b) Model Solution — LEV Hood Types [7 marks] **Four principal LEV hood types used in welding fume control:** **1. Slot Hood (Fixed or Movable) — [1.5 marks]** A rectangular hood with a long narrow slot (slot width 25–50 mm) facing the source. The slot velocity is high (10–15 m/s) to provide a directional capture stream. Slot hoods are commonly mounted on a **welding fume extractor arm** (also called a *snorkel* or *flex-duct hood*). They are ideal for **stationary welding fixtures** and can be positioned 0.5–1.0 × diameter of the source away from the arc. **2. Captor Hood (Funnel / Bellmouth) — [1.5 marks]** A flared or bellmouth-shaped hood placed near the source. The capture velocity is lower (0.5–2.5 m/s) but the effective capture area is larger. Suitable for **low-concentration, large-area sources** like grinding benches. Requires precise positioning because the capture stream is more diffuse. **3. On-Tool Extraction Hood — [1.5 marks]** An integrated extraction nozzle built into the **welding torch** or **grinding tool** itself. The extraction occurs at the point of fume generation, providing the highest capture efficiency (often >95%). Used in **robotic welding cells** and **extraction torches** for manual MIG/TIG welding. **4. Push-Pull Ventilation System — [1.5 marks]** A two-duct system where a **supply (push) duct** directs clean air across the work zone to push fumes toward an **exhaust (pull) duct** that captures them. Used for **large, open welding bays** (e.g., ship hull welding) where a single hood cannot effectively reach the source. Capture efficiency is 80–90% with careful design. **Selection Criteria (to mention for full marks):** - Capture velocity must exceed the contaminant emission velocity - Hood must be within the "zone of influence" (typically 1.5 × hood diameter) - Transport velocity in duct must be 15–20 m/s to prevent particle settling - Use high-efficiency filters (HEPA, H13/H14) downstream of fan **Conclusion:** For the welder above, the recommended control is a **combination of on-tool extraction (for high-fume SMAW/FCAW) and an LEV arm with a slot hood for positioning flexibility**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]` **"(a) [7 marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the air-sampling procedure for the assessment of hazardous fume exposure in an industry. List the standard analytical methods used. (b) [7 marks] A spray painting operation generates toluene vapour (C₇H₈, M = 92.14 g/mol) at a rate of 250 mg/s. The TLV-TWA for toluene is 50 ppm. Calculate the minimum ventilation flow rate required to maintain the concentration at the TLV. If the measured breathing-zone concentration is 75 ppm, what is the hazard ratio and what is the recommended action?"** #### Part (a) Model Solution — Air Sampling Procedure [7 marks] **Block Diagram: Air Sampling Procedure (text representation):** ``` [Workplace Atmosphere] ↓ [Sampling Site Selection — Breathing Zone / Source / Background] ↓ [Collection Medium — Filter cassette (particulates) OR Sorbent tube (vapours)] ↓ [Calibrated Sampling Pump — Flow rate 1–4 L/min] ↓ [Volume Measurement — Time × Flow rate → Total volume in m³] ↓ [Sample Preservation — Refrigeration, sealed containers] ↓ [Laboratory Analysis — see methods below] ↓ [Concentration Calculation — Mass collected / Volume sampled = mg/m³] ↓ [Comparison with TLV-TWA / STEL / PEL] ↓ [Documentation & Reporting] ``` **Detailed Steps [3 marks]:** 1. **Calibrate the sampling pump** using a primary flow standard (bubble flow meter or electronic calibrator). Acceptable accuracy: ±5%. 2. **Position the sampler in the worker's breathing zone** (within 30 cm of nose and mouth) for personal sampling, OR at fixed locations for area sampling. 3. **Select collection medium:** - For **welding fume**: 5 µm PVC or MCE filter in a 37-mm cassette. - For **organic vapours (toluene, benzene)**: activated charcoal or Tenax sorbent tube. 4. **Collect sample** for a representative duration (typically 4–8 hours for TWA; 15 min for STEL). 5. **Record flow rate, time, temperature, pressure** for volume correction. 6. **Analyse in laboratory** and calculate concentration. 7. **Compare with TLV** and assess compliance. **Standard Analytical Methods [2 marks]:** | Contaminant Type | Analytical Method | Detection Limit | |------------------|-------------------|----------------| | Metal fumes (Fe, Mn, Cr, Ni) | **ICP-MS** (Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometry) | µg/L level | | Metal fumes (alternative) | **AAS** (Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy) | mg/L level | | Welding fume mass | **Gravimetry** (weighing filter pre/post) | 0.1 mg | | Organic vapours (toluene) | **GC-FID** (Gas Chromatography – Flame Ionization Detector) | ppb level | | Organic vapours (alternative) | **GC-MS** (Gas Chromatography – Mass Spectrometry) | ppt level | | Gases (CO, NO₂, SO₂) | **NDIR** (Non-Dispersive Infra-Red) or **Electrochemical sensor** | ppm level | | Hexavalent Chromium | **UV-Vis Spectrophotometry** with diphenylcarbazide | µg/L level | **Quality Assurance [1 mark]:** - Field blanks (10% of samples) - Laboratory spikes and duplicates - Standard reference materials (SRMs) from NIST - Chain-of-custody documentation - Documentation of any deviations from NIOSH/OSHA method **Reference:** NIOSH Manual of Analytical Methods (NMAM) and OSHA Analytical Methods Manual provide standardized protocols for each contaminant. #### Part (b) Model Solution — Ventilation & Hazard Ratio [7 marks] **Given:** - Toluene generation rate: $G$ = 250 mg/s - TLV-TWA (toluene): 50 ppm - Molecular weight: $M$ = 92.14 g/mol - Measured breathing-zone concentration: 75 ppm **Step 1: Convert TLV from ppm to mg/m³ [2 marks]** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{TLV, mg/m^3} &= \frac{C_{ppm} \times M}{24.45} \\ C_{TLV, mg/m^3} &= \frac{50 \times 92.14}{24.45} \\ C_{TLV, mg/m^3} &= \frac{4607}{24.45} \\ C_{TLV, mg/m^3} &\approx 188.42 \text{ mg/m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Calculate minimum ventilation flow rate [2 marks]** Assuming clean supply air ($C_{background} = 0$): $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= \frac{G}{C_{TLV} - C_{background}} \\ Q &= \frac{250 \text{ mg/s}}{188.42 \text{ mg/m}^3} \\ Q &= 1.327 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\ Q &= 1.327 \times 60 \\ Q &= 79.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \end{aligned} $$ **With safety factor of 1.5 (industry practice):** $$ Q_{design} = 1.5 \times 79.6 = 119.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} $$ **Step 3: Convert measured concentration to mg/m³ [1 mark]** $$ C_{measured, mg/m^3} = \frac{75 \times 92.14}{24.45} = 282.6 \text{ mg/m}^3 $$ **Step 4: Calculate Hazard Ratio [1 mark]** $$ HR = \frac{C_{measured}}{TLV} = \frac{75 \text{ ppm}}{50 \text{ ppm}} = 1.50 = 150\% $$ Or equivalently: $$ HR = \frac{282.6}{188.42} = 1.50 = 150\% $$ **Step 5: Recommended action [1 mark]** Since the hazard ratio is **1.50 (50% over TLV)**, the situation is **non-compliant**. Hierarchy of controls: 1. **Immediate:** Provide **air-supplied respiratory protection (SAR or PAPR with organic vapour cartridge)** until engineering controls are implemented. 2. **Short-term:** Increase the **local exhaust ventilation** to the design flow of 119.4 m³/min. Verify with re-sampling. 3. **Long-term:** Substitute toluene with a **less hazardous solvent** (e.g., water-based or acetone-based formulations) and enclose the spray booth with a down-draft ventilation system. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Unit Mix-ups (–2 marks):** Students often confuse ppm with mg/m³ and fail to convert units. Always check which unit the TLV is given in for the specific compound. Use $C_{mg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times M / 24.45$ as a mandatory first step. > > 2. **TWA Denominator Error (–1 mark):** The denominator of the TWA is **always 8 hours** (the standard shift), not the sum of the exposure durations. Many students use the sum of exposure hours, which is wrong unless the cumulative duration equals 8 hours. > > 3. **Confusing Fume with Vapour (–1 mark):** Fumes = solid condensed from vapour; Vapours = gaseous phase of a substance normally liquid at room temperature. Examiners frequently test this distinction. > > 4. **Forgetting Safety Factor in LEV Design (–1 mark):** A calculated Q must be multiplied by a safety factor of 1.5–2.0 in real design. Students who quote the bare calculated value lose a mark. > > 5. **PEL vs TLV Confusion (–1 mark):** PEL = OSHA legally enforceable limit (US regulation). TLV = ACGIH recommended guideline (advisory). Both may differ numerically; state clearly which is being used. > > 6. **Skipping the Conclusion (–1 mark):** Every numerical problem must end with an explicit **compliance statement** ("compliant" / "non-compliant") and a **recommendation**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Summary — "Must-Know" Items:** > > ✅ **Fume** = solid sub-micron particle formed by **condensation from a vapour phase** after high-temperature processes (welding, smelting, soldering). > > ✅ **Dust** = solid particle formed by **mechanical disintegration** at ordinary temperatures (grinding, crushing). > > ✅ **Vapour** = gaseous phase of a substance that is **liquid at room temperature** (benzene, toluene, mercury). > > ✅ **Aerosol** = the dispersion of solid or liquid particles in a gaseous medium. > > ✅ **TLV-TWA** = 8-hour time-weighted average; protects against chronic effects. > > ✅ **TLV-STEL** = 15-minute average; protects against acute effects; max **4 exposures per shift, ≥ 60 min apart**. > > ✅ **TLV-C** = absolute ceiling; must never be exceeded. > > ✅ **IDLH** = Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health; the level at which a worker can escape within 30 min without irreversible health effects. > > ✅ **Conversion:** $C_{mg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times M / 24.45$ (at 25 °C, 1 atm). > > ✅ **TWA Formula:** $TWA = \sum (C_i \times t_i) / 8 \text{ hr}$. > > ✅ **Haber's Law:** $C \times t = k$ for many irritant and asphyxiant gases. > > ✅ **Ventilation Design:** $Q = G / (C_{TLV} - C_{background})$; apply safety factor of **1.5–2.0**. > > ✅ **Particle Deposition Mechanisms:** **Impaction** (large particles, upper airways), **Sedimentation** (medium particles, bronchial tree), **Diffusion** (sub-micron particles, alveoli), **Electrostatic precipitation** (charged particles). > > ✅ **Hazard Ratio / Risk Index:** $HR = C_{measured} / TLV \times 100\%$. HR > 100% = non-compliant. > > ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls (in order):** **Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering (LEV) → Administrative → PPE**. > > ✅ **Critical Welding Fumes:** Iron oxide (TLV 5 mg/m³), Manganese (0.02 mg/m³ — most stringent), Cr(VI) (0.0002 mg/m³ — carcinogen), ZnO (2 mg/m³ — metal fume fever). > > ✅ **Critical Gases:** CO (25 ppm, asphyxiant), NO₂ (0.2 ppm, delayed pulmonary oedema), O₃ (0.05 ppm, generated by UV in MIG/TIG welding). > > ✅ **Analytical Methods:** **ICP-MS** for metals, **GC-FID/GC-MS** for organics, **AAS** for metals (older), **NDIR** for gases, **Gravimetry** for total particulate mass. > > ✅ **Air Sampling Standards:** NIOSH Manual of Analytical Methods (NMAM), OSHA Analytical Methods, ASTM, IS (Indian Standards) — for sampling pump calibration and method validation. > > ✅ **8-hour Reference Period:** Industrial hygiene "day" is always 8 hours, not the worker's actual shift length, for TWA compliance. > > ✅ **Levy of Fume:** Welding fume TLV is expressed as the **respirable fraction** (PM₄ or PM₂.₅), not total dust.

topics.title: Source of Fumes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT601 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Source of Fumes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Fumes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fumes** are finely divided solid airborne particulates, typically **sub-micron in size (< 1 μm)**, formed by the **condensation of vapors** — usually of metallic origin — that have been generated by heating molten materials, welding, smelting, or chemical reactions, and then rapidly cooled in the surrounding air.

According to industrial hygiene nomenclature (ACGIH / OSHA framework adopted by KTU 2024 syllabus), airborne contaminants in workplaces are classified into four broad categories:

| Contaminant Type | Physical State | Formation Mechanism | Typical Particle Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dust** | Solid (mechanical) | Crushing, grinding, drilling | 1 – 100 μm |
| **Fume** | Solid (condensed vapor) | Welding, smelting, vaporization-condensation | **< 1 μm** |
| **Mist** | Liquid droplet | Spraying, condensation of vapor | 1 – 10 μm |
| **Vapor / Gas** | Gaseous | Evaporation, chemical reaction | Molecular |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of fumes like "invisible metallic smoke."** When a blacksmith heats a piece of iron until it glows red-orange, you might see a faint bluish haze rising from it. That haze is **not smoke (combustion product)** and **not dust (chipped-off solid)** — it is **vaporized metal that has condensed into ultra-fine particles** in the cool air. These particles are so tiny (smaller than what a human hair can trap) that they float for hours and can be inhaled deep into the lungs.

Imagine pouring a cup of hot tea into a cold room — the **steam (vapor)** quickly disappears as it **condenses** into tiny water droplets (mist/fine aerosol). Fumes behave identically, except the condensing substance is a **metal or its oxide** rather than water.

## 1.3 Physical & Chemical Characteristics of Fumes

- **Particle Diameter:** 0.001 μm to 1 μm (typically 0.1 – 0.5 μm)
- **Shape:** Often spherical primary particles, **agglomerated into chain-like or flocculent clusters**
- **Composition:** Usually **metal oxides** (e.g., Fe₂O₃, MnO₂, ZnO, CrO₃, PbO, NiO) rather than the parent metal itself
- **Behavior in Air:** Settle very slowly due to low mass; remain airborne for **minutes to hours**
- **Respirability:** Highly **respirable** — can penetrate beyond ciliated airways into the **alveolar (gas-exchange) region** of the lungs
- **Color:** Often gives the visible "welding plume" its characteristic bluish, brownish, or yellow tint

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** Fumes are **not combustion products of organic matter** (that is smoke/soot). They are **condensation aerosols** of (typically) metallic origin. A welding arc, for instance, vaporizes the electrode and base metal; the vapor immediately reacts with atmospheric oxygen and re-condenses as fine metal-oxide fume.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

The **Source of Fumes** topic directly underpins **Course Outcome CO3** of PCSFT601: *"Identify and evaluate hazardous emissions from industrial processes and recommend appropriate control strategies."* Examiners routinely test students' ability to (a) classify airborne contaminants, (b) identify the specific process generating a given fume, and (c) name the **toxic constituents** that follow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Fume Formation (The "How")

Fume formation is a **two-stage physical-chemical process** that distinguishes it from dust generation:

**Stage 1 — Vaporization (Endothermic):**
The source material is heated above its **vaporization temperature**. Energy input sources include:
- Electric arc (welding, arc furnaces)
- Oxy-fuel flame (oxy-acetylene cutting, brazing)
- Induction heating (induction furnaces)
- Exothermic chemical reaction (thermite, refining)
- Resistance heating (electric resistance furnaces)

**Stage 2 — Oxidation + Condensation (Exothermic):**
The vaporized atoms encounter atmospheric oxygen and **oxidize rapidly** (in many cases this is the dominant reaction), then **condense homogeneously** onto themselves, forming primary particles of ~0.01 – 0.1 μm. These primary particles **agglomerate** into chain-like clusters of 0.1 – 1 μm.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **welding fume is largely metal oxide, not pure metal** — oxidation is virtually instantaneous in the high-temperature plume.

## 2.2 Major Industrial Sources of Fumes

The KTU 2024 syllabus organizes sources into **process families**. The following hierarchy covers > 95% of industrial fume emissions:

### A. Welding & Allied Joining Processes
- **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW / Stick)** — flux-coated electrode vaporization; emits **Fe, Mn, Cr, Ni, F** compounds
- **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW / MIG/MAG)** — wire and shielding gas; high fume rate; **Fe, Mn, Cu, Zn, Pb**
- **Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW)** — tubular electrode with flux core; **highest fume generation rate** of all arc processes
- **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG)** — low fume rate, but emits **W, Th (if thoriated), Zr, Mo, Cr (from stainless)**
- **Plasma Arc Welding / Cutting** — high-temperature ionized gas; produces **NOx + metal oxide fume**
- **Oxy-Fuel Welding, Brazing, Soldering** — produces **Cu, Zn, Pb, Cd, Sn** oxide fume (Cd fume is acutely toxic)

### B. Foundry & Casting Operations
- **Cupola Furnace Charging & Tapping** — Fe, Si, Mn fume
- **Electric Arc Furnace (EAF)** — intense fume plume; **Fe, Cr, Ni, Mn, Zn, Pb, Cd**
- **Induction Furnace Melting** — lower fume rate than EAF; **Fe, alloying elements**
- **Pouring & Shake-out** — fume released from hot molds; **organic binder pyrolysis products mixed with metal fume**
- **Sand Reclamation** — fine silica + metal fume

### C. Smelting & Metal Refining
- **Primary Smelting (Ore → Metal)** — sulfide ore roasting emits **SO₂ + metal oxide fume (Cu, Zn, Pb, Ni)**
- **Secondary Smelting (Scrap → Metal)** — particularly for **Pb, Al, Cu**; emits toxic metal fume and dioxins
- **Electrolytic Refining** — anode slime vaporization; **As, Sb, Se, Te, Bi, Ag** fume

### D. Furnace & Kiln Operations
- **Electric Arc Furnace Steelmaking** — extremely high fume load; **Fe₂O₃, CaO, SiO₂**
- **Cement Kilns** — alkaline dust + fume
- **Glass Furnaces** — **B₂O₃, Na₂O, SiO₂, Pb, As** fume
- **Ceramic Kilns** — fluoride fume from certain glazes

### E. Hot & Cold Metalworking
- **Laser Cutting** — vaporizes metal along kerf; fume composition mirrors base metal
- **Plasma Cutting** — high velocity fume jet; **Fe, Cr, Ni, Mn, Zn, NOₓ**
- **Hot Rolling / Forging** — scale (Fe₃O₄) fume
- **Galvanizing & Tinning** — high-temperature **ZnO fume** (causes "metal fume fever")

### F. Chemical Process & Surface Treatment
- **Electroplating** — acid mist (H₂SO₄, HCl) with entrained **Cr, Ni, Cd, Zn** particulates
- **Pickling** — acid mist with metal salts
- **Polymer Processing** — heating plastics releases **PVC fume, isocyanates, acrolein** (technically a vapor that can re-condense as fume)
- **Rubber Vulcanization** — fume containing **ZnO, accelerators, antioxidants**

### G. Combustion-Based Sources
- **Coal-fired Boilers** — **fly ash + soot fume**
- **Incinerators** — **dioxin/furan + heavy metal fume (Hg, Cd, Pb)**
- **Diesel Engines (forklifts, mining)** — **DPM (Diesel Particulate Matter) + PAH** — classified as carcinogenic fume
- **Internal Combustion Engine Workshops** — CO, NOₓ, lead fume (legacy)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE numericals and short derivations.

| Symbol | Description | Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $v_s$ | Terminal settling velocity (Stokes' Law) | $v_s = \dfrac{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho)\,g}{18\,\mu}$ | m/s |
| $Q$ | Required dilution ventilation flow | $Q = \dfrac{G}{TLV - B}$ | m³/s |
| $ACH$ | Air changes per hour | $ACH = \dfrac{Q \times 3600}{V}$ | h⁻¹ |
| $C_{TWA}$ | Time-Weighted Average concentration | $C_{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum C_i\,t_i}{\sum t_i}$ | mg/m³ |
| $C_i$ | Instantaneous concentration at time $t_i$ | — | mg/m³ |
| $E$ | Emission rate of fume | $E = v \times A \times C$ | mg/s |
| $K_{st}$ | Dust/Fume explosion index | $K_{st} = \left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \sqrt{V}$ | bar·m/s |
| $L_{EL}$ | Lower Explosive Limit (fume/dust) | $L_{EL} = \dfrac{LEL_{100\%}}{SF}$ | g/m³ |

> **Where:** $d_p$ = particle diameter, $\rho_p$ = particle density, $\rho$ = air density, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $\mu$ = air viscosity, $G$ = generation rate, $B$ = background concentration, $V$ = room volume, $SF$ = safety factor (typically 4 – 10).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants to Memorize:**
> - TLV-TWA for **welding fume (total)** = **5 mg/m³** (ACGIH)
> - TLV-TWA for **Mn (as MnO₂)** = **0.1 mg/m³** (respirable)
> - TLV-TWA for **Pb (inorganic)** = **0.05 mg/m³**
> - TLV-TWA for **Cd (as CdO)** = **0.002 mg/m³** (suspected carcinogen)
> - Particle size for **respirable fraction** = < **4 μm** (alveolar penetration < 10 μm)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Occupational Hygiene Surveys:** Fume source identification is the **first step in NIOSH-style industrial hygiene surveys** — the **hierarchy of controls (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE)** is only meaningful after sources are mapped.
- **Ventilation Design:** Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) hood placement depends on **source geometry** and **thermal plume rise velocity**.
- **Atmospheric Emissions:** EPA / KSPCB compliance for **stack monitoring** requires characterization of fume streams from furnaces and smelters.
- **Fire & Explosion Engineering:** Many **metal fumes (Al, Mg, Zn, Ti)** are explosible; $K_{st}$ values are critical for **NFPA 652 / ATEX 2014/34/EU** zone classification.
- **Forensic Safety Investigation:** Identifying the source of fume exposure is essential in **occupational disease litigation** (e.g., welder's pneumoconiosis, hard-metal lung disease).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stokes' Law for Fume Settling

Fume particles (especially the larger agglomerates, 0.5 – 5 μm) settle under gravity. The settling velocity determines how long the fume remains airborne and therefore how far it can travel through a workplace.

**Starting Physics:** A particle falling under gravity experiences three forces — gravity (down), buoyancy (up), and drag (up).

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's Second Law (steady-state, $\sum F = 0$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{gravity} - F_{buoyancy} - F_{drag} &= 0 \\
\frac{\pi d_p^{\,3}}{6}\,\rho_p\,g \;-\; \frac{\pi d_p^{\,3}}{6}\,\rho\,g \;-\; 3\pi\mu d_p\,v_s &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange the drag term (Cunningham slip correction neglected for d_p > 1 μm):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\pi d_p^{\,3}}{6}\,(\rho_p - \rho)\,g &= 3\pi\mu d_p\,v_s \\
v_s &= \frac{\pi d_p^{\,3}\,(\rho_p - \rho)\,g}{6 \times 3\pi\mu d_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify by cancelling common factors:**

$$
v_s = \frac{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho)\,g}{18\,\mu}
$$

**Step 4 — Cunningham Slip Correction (for sub-micron fumes, d_p < 1 μm):**

For true fume particles (where d_p approaches the mean free path of air, λ ≈ 0.066 μm), the **slip correction factor C_c** must be introduced:

$$
v_s = \frac{d_p^{\,2}\,(\rho_p - \rho)\,g}{18\,\mu}\,C_c \quad ; \quad C_c = 1 + \frac{2\lambda}{d_p}\left( A_1 + A_2\,e^{-A_3 d_p / (2\lambda)} \right)
$$

With empirical constants $A_1 = 1.142$, $A_2 = 0.558$, $A_3 = 0.999$ (for air at 1 atm, 20 °C).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** Because settling velocity scales as $d_p^{\,2}$, a 0.1 μm fume particle settles **100× slower** than a 1 μm particle. This is why **fume persists in air for hours** and contaminates the entire breathing zone of a workshop.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Dilution Ventilation Flow

To keep fume concentration below TLV, dilution air must be supplied at rate $Q$.

**Step 1 — Mass balance on the control volume (steady state, well-mixed):**

The rate of fume generation ($G$, mg/s) must equal the rate of fume exhausted:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= Q \times C_{ss}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $C_{ss}$ = steady-state concentration (mg/m³).

**Step 2 — Enforce occupational limit: $C_{ss} \le TLV - B$** (B = background):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &\ge \frac{G}{TLV - B}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Air Changes per Hour (KTU examiners love this step):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACH &= \frac{Q \times 3600}{V_{room}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Where $V_{room}$ is the workshop volume in m³.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sources vs. Fume Constituents vs. TLVs

This is the **single most important KTU table** for viva and 14-mark answers:

| Source / Process | Primary Fume Constituents | TLV-TWA (mg/m³) | Key Health Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SMAW (mild steel electrode)** | Fe₂O₃, MnO₂, F, SiO₂ | 5 (total) / **0.1 (Mn)** | Manganism, siderosis |
| **GMAW stainless steel** | Fe₂O₃, **CrO₃ (hexavalent Cr)**, NiO, MnO₂ | 5 / **0.005 (Cr VI)** | Lung cancer (Cr VI) |
| **GTAW stainless steel** | W, Cr, Ni, Mo | 5 / 0.5 (W) | Asthma, dermatitis |
| **Brazing with Cd-silver alloy** | **CdO, ZnO, CuO** | **0.002 (Cd)** | Acute pneumonitis, lung cancer |
| **Soldering (Pb-tin)** | **PbO, SnO₂, rosin** | **0.05 (Pb)** | Neurotoxicity, anemia |
| **Galvanizing** | **ZnO** | 5 (ZnO) | **Metal fume fever** |
| **EAF steelmaking** | Fe₂O₃, CaO, ZnO, PbO, CdO | 5 / 0.05 (Pb) / 0.002 (Cd) | Acute respiratory distress |
| **Secondary Pb smelting** | **PbO, PbSO₄, PbCO₃** | **0.05 (Pb)** | Plumbism, chronic kidney disease |
| **Aluminum smelting (Soderberg)** | **Al₂O₃, PAH, HF, CO** | 5 (Al₂O₃) | Asthma, **potroom palsy** |
| **Foundry shake-out** | Fe₂O₃, SiO₂, CO, **PAH** | 0.025 (benzo[a]pyrene) | Lung cancer, silicosis |
| **Electroplating (Cr bath)** | **CrO₃ mist** | 0.005 (Cr VI) | **Nasal septum perforation**, lung cancer |
| **Plastic welding / PVC cutting** | **HCl, isocyanate, dioxin** | 0.005 (TDI) | Asthma, hypersensitivity pneumonitis |
| **Diesel engine exhaust (mining)** | **DPM + NO₂ + PAH** | 0.02 (NO₂) / 0.0002 (diesel) | **Lung cancer (Group 1 IARC)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mn** and **Cr VI** are the two constituents examiners *almost always* include in questions on welding fume hazards. Mn targets the **basal ganglia** (irreversible Parkinsonian syndrome); Cr VI is a **Group 1 IARC carcinogen**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fume Generation–Dispersion–Control Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **process-level flow** of fume from generation to control. Note the strict node-ID convention (alphanumeric, prefixed) and the absence of markdown formatting inside quoted labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Heat Source: Electric Arc, Flame, Induction] --> A2[Stage 1: Vaporization of Base Metal and Flux]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 2: Reaction with Atmospheric Oxygen]
    A3 --> A4[Stage 3: Homogeneous Condensation as Metal Oxide]
    A4 --> A5[Primary Particles 0.01 to 0.1 micrometer]
    A5 --> A6[Agglomeration into Chain Clusters 0.1 to 1.0 micrometer]
    A6 --> B1[Buoyant Thermal Plume Rise]
    B1 --> B2[Entrainment of Room Air]
    B2 --> B3[Dispersion into Workshop Breathing Zone]
    B3 --> C1[Pathway 1: Inhalation of Respirable Fraction]
    B3 --> C2[Pathway 2: Settling onto Skin and Work Surfaces]
    B3 --> C3[Pathway 3: Stack Release to Ambient Air]
    C1 --> D1[Engineering Control: Local Exhaust Ventilation]
    C1 --> D2[Engineering Control: General Dilution Ventilation]
    C1 --> D3[Administrative Control: Rotation and Training]
    C1 --> D4[PPE: Powered Air Purifying Respirator]
    D1 --> E1[Capture Hood at Source]
    E1 --> E2[Duct Transport]
    E2 --> E3[Air Cleaning Unit: Fabric Filter or ESP]
    E3 --> E4[Induced Draft Fan]
    E4 --> E5[Stack Discharge to Atmosphere]
    E5 --> F1[Compliant if Concentration below KSPCB Norms]
    C3 --> F1
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- **Left column (A):** the three-stage physical–chemical mechanism (vaporization → oxidation → condensation).
- **Middle column (B):** the in-plant dispersion pathway driven by buoyancy.
- **Right column (C):** the three human/environmental exposure pathways.
- **Bottom row (D):** the **hierarchy of controls**, from most effective (engineering at source) to least (PPE).
- **Final stage (E → F):** the air-handling train — hood, duct, filter, fan, stack — ending in **regulatory compliance check**.

## 4.2 Source-Process Hierarchy (Block Architecture)

A second, more compact Mermaid block summarising the **industrial source taxonomy** introduced in Section 2.2:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Process Family: Welding] --> W1[SMAW]
    P1 --> W2[GMAW]
    P1 --> W3[GTAW]
    P1 --> W4[FCAW]
    P1 --> W5[Plasma]
    P1 --> W6[Oxy-fuel Brazing]
    P2[Process Family: Foundry] --> F1[Cupola Furnace]
    P2 --> F2[Electric Arc Furnace]
    P2 --> F3[Induction Furnace]
    P2 --> F4[Pouring and Shakeout]
    P3[Process Family: Smelting] --> S1[Primary Ore Smelting]
    P3 --> S2[Secondary Scrap Smelting]
    P3 --> S3[Electrolytic Refining]
    P4[Process Family: Hot Working] --> H1[Laser Cutting]
    P4 --> H2[Plasma Cutting]
    P4 --> H3[Hot Rolling]
    P4 --> H4[Galvanizing]
    P5[Process Family: Surface Treatment] --> T1[Electroplating]
    P5 --> T2[Pickling]
    P5 --> T3[Anodizing]
    P6[Process Family: Combustion] --> C1[Coal Fired Boiler]
    P6 --> C2[Incinerator]
    P6 --> C3[Diesel Engine]
    W1 --> X1[Fume Composition Fe Mn F]
    W2 --> X2[Fume Composition Fe Mn Cu Zn]
    W3 --> X3[Fume Composition W Cr Ni Mo]
    W6 --> X4[Fume Composition Cd Zn Cu]
    F2 --> X5[Fume Composition Fe Cr Ni Pb Cd]
    S2 --> X6[Fume Composition PbO dominant]
    H4 --> X7[Fume Composition ZnO dominant]
    T1 --> X8[Fume Composition CrO3 acid mist]
    C3 --> X9[Fume Composition DPM PAH NO2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' note:** A question phrased as *"Identify the process most likely responsible for ZnO fume exposure"* should be answered with reference to **galvanizing, brass welding, or oxy-fuel brazing** — never with reference to a non-metallic process.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]
**Define "fumes" as an industrial air contaminant. How do they differ from dusts and mists?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Fumes** are finely divided solid airborne particulates, usually of sub-micron size, formed by the **condensation of vapors** generated from heating, welding, smelting, or chemical reactions.
>
> **Differences:**
>
> | Parameter | Fume | Dust | Mist |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Origin | **Condensed vapor** | **Mechanical disintegration** | **Condensed liquid** |
> | Size | < 1 μm | 1 – 100 μm | 1 – 10 μm |
> | Example | Welding fume (Fe₂O₃) | Coal dust | Sulfuric acid mist |
> | Formation | Vaporization → oxidation → condensation | Crushing, grinding, blasting | Spraying, bubbling |
> | Composition | Usually **metal oxide** | Parent solid | Parent liquid |

**(3 marks — one mark each for definition, size distinction, and formation mechanism.)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]
**List any six major industrial sources of fumes with one example of the toxic constituent from each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **SMAW welding** — Manganese (MnO₂)
2. **Galvanizing** — Zinc (ZnO)
3. **Cadmium brazing** — Cadmium (CdO)
4. **Secondary lead smelting** — Lead (PbO)
5. **Electroplating (chrome bath)** — Hexavalent Chromium (CrO₃)
6. **Diesel engine exhaust (mining)** — Diesel Particulate Matter (DPM) and PAH

**(3 marks — 0.5 per correctly named source–constituent pair.)**

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Source of Fumes in Metal Processing

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the various **sources of fumes in metal processing industries** with suitable examples. Discuss the **two-stage physical–chemical mechanism** of fume formation.  **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **health hazards associated with welding fumes** and outline the **engineering control measures** used to mitigate them.  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The principal metal-processing fume sources fall under six families:

1. **Welding operations** — SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, FCAW, plasma, and oxy-fuel processes all vaporize the electrode and base metal. The vapor oxidises in air and condenses as fine metal-oxide fume. Example: SMAW on mild steel emits **Fe₂O₃, MnO₂, and fluorides** from the flux coating.

2. **Foundry and casting** — cupola, EAF, and induction furnaces release Fe, Si, Mn, Zn, and Pb fume during melting, tapping, and pouring. Shake-out of molds adds **organic binder pyrolysis products** (PAH, CO) to the metallic fume.

3. **Smelting and refining** — primary smelting of sulfide ores emits metal-oxide fume along with **SO₂ gas**; secondary smelting of scrap (especially **Pb and Al**) concentrates **PbO and dioxin** in the off-gas.

4. **Hot metalworking** — laser/plasma cutting, hot rolling, forging, and galvanizing produce high-temperature fume plumes. **Galvanizing** is a textbook example of **ZnO fume generation** (the "zinc fever" process).

5. **Surface treatment** — electroplating and pickling generate **acid mist** with entrained metal salt fume. Chrome plating emits **hexavalent chromium mist** (CrO₃ in H₂SO₄).

6. **Combustion** — coal-fired boilers, incinerators, and diesel engines emit fly-ash fume, dioxins, and **diesel particulate matter (DPM)**.

**Two-stage formation mechanism:**

- **Stage 1 — Vaporization:** Heat from an arc, flame, or induction coil raises the metal above its vaporization temperature, producing a metal-vapor cloud.
- **Stage 2 — Oxidation and Condensation:** The vapor reacts almost instantaneously with atmospheric O₂ to form metal oxides, which then **homogeneously condense** as primary particles (~0.01 – 0.1 μm). These primary particles **agglomerate** into chain-like clusters (0.1 – 1.0 μm), which is the size that enters the alveolar region of the lungs.

**(7 marks — 5 marks for sources list, 2 marks for mechanism.)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Health Hazards of Welding Fume:**

- **Manganese (MnO₂):** chronic exposure causes **manganism** — an irreversible Parkinsonian syndrome affecting the basal ganglia.
- **Hexavalent Chromium (CrO₃ from stainless-steel welding):** **Group 1 IARC carcinogen**; causes lung cancer and nasal septum ulceration.
- **Iron oxide (Fe₂O₃):** **siderosis** (benign pneumoconiosis) but synergistic with smoking in causing lung cancer.
- **Nickel oxide (NiO):** respiratory sensitizer; asthma, dermatitis, lung cancer.
- **Fluorides (from flux):** acute respiratory irritation, fluorosis.
- **Phosgene, NOₓ, ozone:** generated by the UV radiation of the arc acting on shielding gas; cause pulmonary oedema.
- **Cadmium (from brazing):** acute pneumonitis at high exposure; chronic bronchitis and lung cancer.

**Engineering Control Measures:**

1. **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** at the welding torch — fume extractor guns, on-torch extraction, or moveable hoods with capture velocity 0.5 – 1.0 m/s.
2. **General dilution ventilation** — minimum ACH = 6 to 10 in enclosed welding bays; airflow should be cross-ventilated to avoid dead zones.
3. **Substitution of process** — replacing **SMAW** (high-flux fume) with **GMAW or FCAW** using low-fume consumables; using low-Mn and low-Cr VI electrodes.
4. **Enclosure and isolation** — automated welding in robot cells reduces operator exposure.
5. **Welding curtains and barriers** — protects adjacent workers but does not protect the welder.
6. **PPE:** Powered Air Purifying Respirator (PAPR) with P100/TH3 cartridges for the welder when LEV is inadequate.
7. **Routine air monitoring** — personal sampling (NIOSH 7300 method) to confirm compliance with TLV.

**(7 marks — 4 marks for hazards, 3 marks for controls.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Welding & Foundry Fume Deep-Dive (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Describe the **formation mechanism of welding fumes**. Discuss the **composition of fumes** from SMAW, GMAW stainless, and oxy-fuel brazing. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **sources of fumes in foundry operations** with reference to the cupola furnace, electric arc furnace, and shake-out. Discuss the **engineering control measures** specific to each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Formation Mechanism of Welding Fume:**

The welding arc reaches a temperature of **6 000 – 20 000 °C** at its core, well above the vaporization point of every engineering metal. The process proceeds in **four sub-steps**:

1. **Vaporization:** the electrode tip and a thin layer of the base metal are vaporized; for flux-coated electrodes, the flux components (F, Si, K, Na) also vaporize.
2. **Oxidation:** the metal vapor mixes with surrounding air and oxidises almost instantaneously; for stainless steel, this produces **hexavalent chromium (CrO₃)**, the most hazardous constituent.
3. **Condensation:** the oxide vapour becomes supersaturated, and **homogeneous nucleation** forms primary particles ~0.01 μm.
4. **Agglomeration:** primary particles collide and form chain aggregates 0.1 – 1.0 μm — the **respirable fraction** that penetrates to the alveoli.

**Composition by Process:**

| Process | Electrode / Gas | Key Fume Constituents | Notable Hazard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SMAW (mild steel)** | Cellulosic / rutile flux | Fe₂O₃, **MnO₂**, SiO₂, NaF, KF | Manganism; fluorosis |
| **GMAW (stainless)** | Solid wire + Ar/CO₂ | Fe₂O₃, **CrO₃**, NiO, MnO₂ | Lung cancer (Cr VI) |
| **Oxy-fuel brazing** | Brass / bronze rod + O₂-C₂H₂ | **CdO** (if Cd-silver), **ZnO**, CuO | Metal fume fever; acute Cd pneumonitis |

**(7 marks — 3 marks for mechanism, 4 marks for composition table.)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Foundry Fume Sources:**

1. **Cupola Furnace:** Charges of pig iron, scrap, coke, and limestone release **FeO, SiO₂, MnO, CO, and PAH** during combustion. The off-gas is hot (~600 °C) and requires **after-burning** to destroy the CO and PAH.

2. **Electric Arc Furnace (EAF):** During melting, the arc produces an intense plume of **Fe₂O₃, CaO, ZnO (from galvanized scrap), PbO, and CdO**. The fume is highly alkaline due to entrained lime flux. EAF fume is a **K_{st} Class 1** explosible dust.

3. **Induction Furnace:** Lower fume rate than EAF, but **alloying elements** (Cr, Ni, Mo) vaporise at the melt surface.

4. **Pouring and Shake-out:** When the mold is broken to release the casting, the still-hot sand and binder release **CO, PAH, and SiO₂ fume**. **Silica fume** exposure is the most significant long-term hazard (silicosis risk).

**Engineering Controls:**

- **Cupola:** wet scrubber or fabric-filter baghouse after **afterburner**; capture hood at charging door and tap hole.
- **EAF:** direct-shell evacuation system with **water-cooled duct** → baghouse with PTFE membrane (high-temperature resistant); capture via 4th-hole off-take.
- **Induction furnace:** canopy hood with **LEV** ducted to a **high-efficiency cartridge filter**.
- **Pouring and shake-out:** enclosure with **localised exhaust** + **water spray** to suppress dust; **vibratory conveyor with enclosure**.
- **Auxiliary controls:** **process enclosure, automated pouring, sinter coolers, and substitution of silica sand with chromite or zircon** to reduce long-term risk.

**(7 marks — 3 marks for sources, 4 marks for control measures.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating "fume" with "smoke" or "dust".** Marks are deducted when students describe welding fume as "smoke" or fail to state that fume is **condensation aerosol of metal oxide** (not combustion product).
> 2. **Forgetting the oxidation step.** Many students correctly describe vaporization → condensation but miss the **instantaneous reaction with atmospheric oxygen** that explains why fume is *metal oxide* and not *pure metal*.
> 3. **Confusing Cr (III) with Cr (VI).** Only the **hexavalent form** is the carcinogen; total Cr is not. Spelling out "hexavalent" or using the symbol **Cr(VI)** is mandatory.
> 4. **Skipping the TLV value.** KTU examiners award full marks for hazards only when **both the toxic constituent AND its TLV-TWA** are quoted.
> 5. **Naming a process but not a constituent.** "SMAW produces fume" is a **half-answer**; the examiner expects "SMAW on mild steel emits **MnO₂ and Fe₂O₃**, with Mn TLV of **0.1 mg/m³**."
> 6. **Ignoring non-welding sources.** A 7-mark question that only discusses welding loses marks if **galvanizing, secondary smelting, and electroplating** are omitted.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Source of Fumes**

- **Definition:** Fume = **sub-micron (< 1 μm) solid aerosol formed by condensation of (typically metallic) vapor**.
- **Three-stage mechanism:** **Vaporize → Oxidize → Condense → Agglomerate**.
- **Size threshold:** Respirable fraction = < **4 μm**; alveolar-penetrable fume = < **1 μm**.
- **TLV anchor values to memorize:**
   - Total welding fume = **5 mg/m³**
   - **Mn = 0.1 mg/m³**
   - **Cr(VI) = 0.005 mg/m³**
   - **Pb = 0.05 mg/m³**
   - **Cd = 0.002 mg/m³**
- **Biggest industrial sources:** **Welding > Foundry > Smelting > Hot working > Surface treatment > Combustion**.
- **Most hazardous constituents:** **Mn (CNS), Cr(VI) (cancer), Cd (acute pneumonitis), Pb (neuro), ZnO (metal fume fever), Ni (asthma).**
- **Signature ZnO process:** **Galvanizing, brass welding, oxy-fuel brazing**.
- **Signature Cr(VI) process:** **Stainless-steel GMAW / GTAW, chrome electroplating**.
- **Signature CdO process:** **Cadmium-silver brazing** (highest TLV violation risk).
- **Hierarchy of controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering (LEV at source) → Administrative → PPE.
- **LEV design rule:** capture velocity **0.5 – 1.0 m/s** at the torch; minimum duct transport velocity **15 – 20 m/s** to keep fume in suspension.
- **Stokes' Law:** $v_s = d_p^{\,2}(\rho_p - \rho)g / (18\mu)$ — explains why fine fume stays airborne.
- **Dilution formula:** $Q = G / (TLV - B)$ — required exhaust flow to stay compliant.
- **KTU cross-links:** This topic feeds into **Module 4 (Ventilation & LEV design)** and **Module 5 (Personal Protective Equipment — respirator selection)**. Master it well — it is a recurring 14-mark favorite.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ventilation and Fume Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT601 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Ventilation and Fume Protection # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Ventilation — Formal Academic Definition **Ventilation** is the deliberate, engineered supply and removal of air from a workplace by natural or mechanical means to control airborne contaminants (dusts, fumes, vapours, mists, gases), to dilute those that cannot be captured at source, and to maintain a breathable atmosphere compliant with occupational exposure limits such as **TLV-TWA**, **PEL**, or Indian **PEL limits under the Factories Act, 1948**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 — Syllabus Highlight:** > Industrial ventilation is broadly classified into two engineering streams: > 1. **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** — captures the contaminant *at the source* (preferred). > 2. **Dilution / General Ventilation (GV)** — dilutes already-mixed contaminants in the room air (last resort). ## 1.2 Fume Protection — Formal Definition **Fume protection** is the subset of industrial hygiene engineering that controls airborne **metal fumes, welding fumes, soldering fumes, acid mists, organic vapours and process dusts** at the point of generation or at the worker's breathing zone, using a combination of source capture, ducting, air-cleaning devices, and personal protection. > [!NOTE] > **Fume vs. Dust vs. Vapour** (Frequently asked in KTU): > - **Fume**: Solid particles (< 1 μm) formed by condensation of vaporised material (e.g., welding fume, zinc fume). > - **Dust**: Solid particles (1–100 μm) formed by mechanical disintegration (e.g., grinding, sanding). > - **Vapour**: Gaseous phase of a substance normally liquid (e.g., solvent vapour). > - **Mist**: Liquid droplets (5–100 μm) formed by condensation or atomisation. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vacuum Cleaner of the Factory" Imagine a **kitchen chimney (exhaust hood) over a gas stove**. The chimney captures the smoke and oil vapour *right at the pan* before it spreads into the room. That is exactly how **Local Exhaust Ventilation** works in a factory — a hood is placed as close as possible to the polluting process (welding torch, grinding wheel, plating tank, solvent bath), and a fan pulls the contaminated air through ducts into a cleaning device and then safely outside. If the chimney is turned off and instead you open the kitchen window to let the smoke drift away, that is **Dilution / General Ventilation** — much less efficient, much more energy-wasteful, and it still exposes the cook to contaminated air for a longer time. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb (Industrial Hygiene):** > **LEV removes ≥ 90% of contaminant at the source.** A 1 cm shift of the hood away from the source can **halve** its capture efficiency. This is why hood placement dominates all other design choices. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Functional Elements of an LEV System Every Local Exhaust Ventilation installation — whether for a welding bay, a foundry shakeout, or a chemistry lab — consists of the same five serial components, often memorised in KTU exams as **H–D–F–C–S** or **"Hood → Duct → Fan → Cleaner → Stack"**: 1. **Hood (Capture Element):** The inlet that *encloses* or *captures* the contaminant. Types include enclosing hoods, exterior hoods, slots, and high-velocity/low-volume (HVLV) capture devices. 2. **Duct (Conveying Element):** Pipes or rectangular trunks that transport contaminated air from the hood to the cleaner. Carries *static pressure* and *velocity pressure*. 3. **Fan (Prime Mover):** Provides the energy to overcome duct friction, hood entry loss, and cleaner pressure drop. Two categories: **belt-driven** (large systems) and **direct-drive** (small/portable). 4. **Air Cleaner (Decontamination Element):** Removes the contaminant before discharge. Types: cyclone, bag filter, electrostatic precipitator (ESP), wet scrubber, HEPA filter, activated-carbon adsorber. 5. **Stack (Discharge Element):** Releases cleaned air at a height and velocity that prevents re-entrainment into air intakes (typically 3 m above the roof, with exit velocity ≥ 8–10 m/s). ## 2.2 Hoods — Capture Mechanism and the "Why" The hood creates a **capture velocity** $V_c$ at the point where the contaminant is released. The **Volumetric Flow Rate (Q)** required is governed by: - For a **plain round hood** (no flange), point source of contaminant at distance $x$: $$ Q = 10 \cdot \pi \cdot x^{2} \cdot V_c $$ - For a **flanged round hood** (with a wide flat lip, ≈ 50% less flow needed): $$ Q = 10 \cdot \left( \pi \cdot x^{2} + A_f \right) \cdot V_c $$ where $A_f$ is the flange area projected beyond the hood opening. - For a **slot hood** (common on welding, grinding benches): $$ Q = 0.83 \cdot L \cdot W \cdot V_c \quad \text{(slot width W at distance L from source)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the "10" factor?** The capture velocity at distance $x$ is **not** the same as the duct velocity. By continuity, the air drawn through a sphere of radius $x$ must equal the duct flow. The factor 10.0 (in SI) comes from the empirical relationship between the centreline velocity at $x$ and the average velocity at the hood face for a plain round opening. ## 2.3 Recommended Capture Velocities (ACGIH, frequently tested in KTU) | Contaminant / Process | Capture Velocity $V_c$ (m/s) | |---|---| | Vapour, gas, smoke (low generation) | 0.25 – 0.50 | | Solvent vapour degreasing, plating mist | 0.50 – 1.00 | | Welding fumes, light grinding | 1.00 – 2.50 | | General grinding, polishing, buffing | 2.50 – 5.00 | | Heavy grinding, abrasive blasting, crushing | 5.00 – 10.00 | | Hot processes releasing rising plumes (furnaces, ladles) | 1.00 – 2.00 (with enclosing hood) | ## 2.4 Duct Design — The Two Classic Methods **A. Equal-Friction Method (Velocity-Reduced Method):** A constant static pressure loss per metre of duct is maintained (~ 0.8 – 1.0 mm of water per m). The duct **cross-section decreases** as branches are added because the cumulative flow drops, and the velocity is allowed to fall. The advantage: the fan must overcome the *same* friction at every section, so balancing is automatic. **B. Balanced (or "Velocity") Method:** A constant transport velocity is maintained throughout (e.g., 15 m/s for dust, 10 m/s for fumes, 7 m/s for vapours). The duct area changes inversely with cumulative flow, but the friction loss per metre *varies*. Damping vanes or blast gates are required to balance the branches. This is the **preferred industrial method** for process dusts. ### Required Transport Velocities (Industrial Hygiene Standard) | Material Conveyed | Minimum Transport Velocity (m/s) | |---|---| | Vapours, gases, smoke | 5 – 7 | | Fine dust (< 10 μm) | 7 – 10 | | Fumes, welding fume | 10 – 13 | | General industrial dust | 15 – 18 | | Heavy moist dust, chips | 18 – 22 | | Lead, cadmium, mercury-bearing dust | ≥ 20 | ## 2.5 Hood Entry Loss (Static Pressure Loss at Hood) The air entering a hood from the room **loses pressure** because the streamlines contract at the hood mouth.
