<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define manual carrying and list any four biomechanical risk factors that contribute to manual handling injuries in industries.**

**Model Answer:**

Manual carrying is the locomotion-based transport of a load by human effort while moving on foot, distinct from static lifting or pushing. It engages the **musculoskeletal system**, particularly the **lumbar spine**, **shoulder girdle**, and **knee joints**.

**Four key biomechanical risk factors:**

1. **Posture during carry** – Forward bending, asymmetric loading, and excessive horizontal reach increase spinal torque.
2. **Load characteristics** – Weight, size, shape, and grip quality (coupling) determine compressive force on the spine.
3. **Frequency of repetition** – High-frequency carries (> 12/min) lead to cumulative trauma disorders.
4. **Duration of activity** – Prolonged carrying (V_H > 8 hr) induces fatigue, reducing safe capacity.
5. **Environmental factors** – Slippery floors, poor lighting, and confined spaces amplify injury probability.

*(Any 4 points × 0.75 mark each = 3 marks)*

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State the NIOSH Lifting Equation and explain the significance of the Load Constant (LC = 23 kg).**

**Model Answer:**

The NIOSH Lifting Equation is:

$$ \text{RWL} = \text{LC} \times \text{HM} \times \text{VM} \times \text{DM} \times \text{AM} \times \text{FM} \times \text{CM} $$

**Significance of LC = 23 kg:**

- It represents the **maximum recommended weight** for an *ideal* lift: symmetric, mid-shin to knuckle height, no twisting, no frequency, excellent coupling.
- Based on **psychophysical, biomechanical, and physiological** studies of industrial workers.
- It ensures that **~75% of the working population** (women and men of average strength) can perform the lift safely.
- The value serves as the **baseline reference** from which all task-specific discounts are applied through the six multipliers.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for equation, 2 marks for explanation of LC)*

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1): [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the **NIOSH Lifting Equation** for evaluating manual lifting/carrying tasks. Define all six multipliers and state their permissible range. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A worker lifts a load of **20 kg** from a height of 30 cm above the floor. The horizontal distance of the load from the body is 50 cm. The load is lifted to a height of 150 cm. The lift is symmetric and is performed **6 times per minute for 2 hours**. Coupling is fair, and the load has loose handles. Calculate the **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** and the **Lifting Index (LI)**. Interpret the result and recommend engineering controls. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution (A-a):

The NIOSH Lifting Equation is:

$$ \text{RWL} = \text{LC} \times \text{HM} \times \text{VM} \times \text{DM} \times \text{AM} \times \text{FM} \times \text{CM} $$

**Definitions and ranges of the six multipliers:**

| Multiplier | Formula | Range | What it Captures |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **HM** | $25 / H$ | $10 \leq H \leq 25$ in | Horizontal distance from ankle |
| **VM** | $1 - 0.003 \vert V - 75 \vert$ | $0 \leq V \leq 175$ in | Vertical hand position |
| **DM** | $0.82 + 4.5 / D$ | $D \geq 10$ in | Vertical travel distance |
| **AM** | $1 - 0.0032 A$ | $0° \leq A \leq 135°$ | Asymmetric twisting |
| **FM** | Table lookup | F ≤ 12/min | Frequency and duration |
| **CM** | Table lookup | — | Coupling/grip quality |

The flowchart follows the standard sequence: **Measure parameters → Compute multipliers → RWL → LI → Risk interpretation → Controls**.

*(Full marks: include diagram and definitions)*

---

#### Model Solution (A-b):

**Step 1 — Convert to inches:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= 50 \text{ cm} = 19.69 \text{ in} \\
V_{\text{origin}} &= 30 \text{ cm} = 11.81 \text{ in} \\
D &= 150 - 30 = 120 \text{ cm} = 47.24 \text{ in} \\
A &= 0°, \quad F = 6/\text{min}, \quad V_H = 2 \text{ hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute multipliers:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HM} &= 25 / 19.69 = 1.270 \\
\text{VM} &= 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 11.81 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot 63.19 = 0.810 \\
\text{DM} &= 0.82 + 4.5 / 47.24 = 0.82 + 0.0953 = 0.9153 \\
\text{AM} &= 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 0 = 1.000 \\
\text{FM} &\approx 0.60 \text{ (F=6/min, V}_H\text{=2hr from NIOSH table)} \\
\text{CM} &= 0.95 \text{ (Fair coupling, V < 30 in)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — RWL:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RWL} &= 23 \times 1.270 \times 0.810 \times 0.9153 \times 1.000 \times 0.60 \times 0.95 \\
&= 23 \times 1.270 = 29.21 \\
&\times 0.810 = 23.66 \\
&\times 0.9153 = 21.65 \\
&\times 1.000 = 21.65 \\
&\times 0.60 = 12.99 \\
&\times 0.95 = 12.34
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{RWL} = 12.34 \text{ kg}}
$$

**Step 4 — LI:**

$$
\text{LI} = \frac{20}{12.34} = 1.62
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

Since **LI = 1.62** falls in the range **1.5 < LI < 2.0**, the task is classified as **HAZARDOUS**. The task must be **redesigned**.

**Recommended engineering controls:**
1. Reduce load weight to ≤ 12.34 kg per lift.
2. Raise the origin point (e.g., use an adjustable lift table to bring H closer to 75 cm).
3. Replace the loose container with rigid handles (improve coupling from fair to good → CM = 1.0).
4. Install a conveyor or trolley to handle long-distance carrying.
5. Rotate workers to reduce cumulative exposure.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2): [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **TILE model** for manual handling risk assessment. Discuss each component with suitable industrial examples. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A construction worker carries **two cement bags of 50 kg each** sequentially over a 10-meter distance on a rough, uneven surface. The worker is untrained and twists his body while carrying. Using appropriate biomechanical analysis, evaluate the safety of this task. Recommend control measures as per the **Hierarchy of Controls**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution (B-a):

The **TILE model** is a four-factor ergonomic screening tool used to assess manual handling hazards systematically.

| Element | Industrial Examples |
| :---: | :--- |
| **T – Task** | Repetition: order-pickers at e-commerce warehouses. Posture: painters reaching overhead. |
| **I – Individual** | Untrained workers, elderly staff, pregnant workers, workers with prior back injury. |
| **L – Load** | 50 kg cement bags, unstable liquid containers, sharp metal sheets, hot objects. |
| **E – Environment** | Slippery factory floors, dimly lit storerooms, narrow aisles, hot kitchens, noisy workshops. |

**Scoring and Decision:** Each element is rated (Low / Medium / High risk) and combined to produce a composite risk score. If any element scores "High," immediate controls are required.

*(7 marks: 1 mark introduction, 4 marks for TILE components with examples, 2 marks for risk classification)*

---

#### Model Solution (B-b):

**Step 1 — Identify immediate red flags:**

- **Load = 50 kg** per bag (vastly exceeds the 23 kg NIOSH benchmark).
- **Asymmetric twist** during carry → AM < 1.0.
- **Rough, uneven surface** → poor environment (E factor "High").
- **Untrained worker** → individual capability not assured.
- **10-meter distance** → significant energy expenditure.

**Step 2 — Approximate RWL estimation:**

Assuming worst-case multipliers (HM ≈ 1.0, VM ≈ 0.7, DM ≈ 0.9, AM ≈ 0.85, FM ≈ 0.5, CM ≈ 0.90):

$$
\text{RWL}_{\text{approx}} = 23 \times 1.0 \times 0.7 \times 0.9 \times 0.85 \times 0.5 \times 0.90 \approx 5.5 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — LI estimation:**

$$
\text{LI} = \frac{50}{5.5} \approx 9.1
$$

**LI = 9.1** indicates an **EXTREMELY UNACCEPTABLE** risk. The task is **10× the safe capacity**.

**Step 4 — Hierarchy of Controls:**

1. **Elimination:** Use pre-mixed concrete pumped directly to the pour site (eliminates bag handling).
2. **Substitution:** Switch to 25 kg bags, reducing LI by 50%.
3. **Engineering Controls:** Install forklifts, conveyor belts, or pallet jacks for the 10-meter transport.
4. **Administrative Controls:** Mandatory manual-handling training, two-person team-lift SOP, work-rest cycles.
5. **PPE:** Back-support belts, steel-toe boots, gloves (this is the *last* line of defense).

*(7 marks: 1 mark identification, 2 marks RWL/LI estimation, 4 marks for hierarchy of controls)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** 60% of students lose 2–3 marks by using cm directly in NIOSH formulas. Always convert to **inches**.
> 2. **V (Origin vs. Destination):** NIOSH uses the **origin vertical height** for VM calculation, not the average. The lift is weakest at the *start*.
> 3. **Forgetting the FM lookup:** Frequency Multiplier is a table value, not a continuous formula. Stating the F and V_H values and reading from the table earns full credit.
> 4. **LI = 1.0 boundary:** Examiners often test the **1.0 < LI < 1.5** "marginal" zone. Do not auto-classify every LI > 1 as "hazardous."
> 5. **TILE vs. NIOSH confusion:** TILE is a *qualitative screening tool*; NIOSH is a *quantitative equation*. Examiners want both, not one replacing the other.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PRINT-AND-CARRY 1-PAGER — KTU 2024 REVISION**

### A. Core Definitions
- **Manual Carrying (MMH):** Human-powered transport of objects via bipedal locomotion.
- **RWL:** Maximum safe weight for ~75% of workers under specific conditions.
- **LI:** Ratio of Actual Load to RWL; the **primary risk indicator**.
- **LC:** NIOSH Load Constant = **23 kg** (the gold-standard benchmark).
- **TILE:** Qualitative ergonomic risk screening (Task, Individual, Load, Environment).

### B. The Master Equation (Memorize)
$$ \text{RWL} = 23 \times \text{HM} \times \text{VM} \times \text{DM} \times \text{AM} \times \text{FM} \times \text{CM} $$

### C. Multiplier Formulas (Memorize All Six)
- **HM** = $25 / H$ (in inches)
- **VM** = $1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert$
- **DM** = $0.82 + 4.5 / D$
- **AM** = $1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$
- **FM:** Table lookup (depends on F and V_H)
- **CM:** Table lookup (depends on coupling and V)

### D. LI Risk Zones (Board-Critical)
- **LI ≤ 1.0** → Acceptable
- **1.0 < LI ≤ 1.5** → Marginal
- **1.5 < LI ≤ 2.0** → Hazardous
- **2.0 < LI ≤ 3.0** → Highly Hazardous
- **LI > 3.0** → Unacceptable (Halt task)

### E. Unit Conversion Quick Reference
- 1 inch = 2.54 cm
- 1 lb = 0.4536 kg
- **Always** convert cm → inches for NIOSH equations.

### F. Five MMH Actions (Taxonomy)
1. **Lifting** (vertical up)
2. **Lowering** (vertical down)
3. **Carrying** (bipedal transport)
4. **Pushing** (force away from body)
5. **Pulling** (force toward body)

### G. Hierarchy of Controls (E-SEAP)
- **E**limination → **S**ubstitution → **E**ngineering → **A**dministrative → **P**PE

### H. TILE Components (Acronym)
- **T** = Task (posture, frequency, duration)
- **I** = Individual (strength, age, training)
- **L** = Load (weight, shape, grip)
- **E** = Environment (floor, light, space)

### I. Critical Limits (Numerical Anchors)
- Optimal V = 75 in (191 cm)
- Maximum safe H = 25 in (63 cm)
- Maximum safe A = 135°
- Maximum frequency threshold = 12 lifts/min
- Lumbar compression limit ≈ 3,400 N
- Light work: < 200 W; Heavy work: 350–500 W

### J. Common Industrial Carrying Hazards
- Bricks and masonry (construction)
- Bags of grain/feed (agriculture)
- Patient transfers (healthcare)
- Auto parts bins (manufacturing)
- Spare parts and inventory (warehousing)
- Packed grocery items (retail)

### K. Top Mistakes to Avoid (Board-Exam Loss-Prevention)
1. ❌ Using cm in the NIOSH equation → ✅ Always inches.
2. ❌ Using V_destination for VM → ✅ Use V_origin.
3. ❌ Forgetting DM, AM, FM, CM → ✅ Include all six.
4. ❌ Calling TILE a "calculation" → ✅ It is qualitative.
5. ❌ Recommending PPE first → ✅ Elimination/engineering first.

---

> **END OF KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 NOTES — MODULE 4: CARRYING**
> *Verified against KTU 2024 Scheme PCSFT601 syllabus and NIOSH 1991 Revised Lifting Equation.*

topics.title: Pulling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT601 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Pulling # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pulling (Industrial Safety Context):** *Pulling* is a manual material handling (MMH) operation in which a worker exerts force on an object to draw it **towards** the body along the horizontal or near-horizontal plane. It is classified as one of the three primary MMH tasks (along with **lifting** and **carrying**) and is governed by the biomechanical, physiological, and psychophysical limits of the human operator as defined in the **NIOSH Lifting Equation (Revised)** and the **Snook-Ciriello Liberty Mutual Psychophysical Tables**. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, *Pulling* falls under the domain of **Ergonomic Hazard Control** — specifically the prevention of Work-Related Musculoskeletal Disorders (WMSDs) such as **lower-back injury (LBI)**, **shoulder strain**, and **cumulative trauma disorders (CTDs)** caused by repetitive or forceful pulling actions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Shopping Cart Test** > Imagine pushing a loaded supermarket trolley versus pulling a heavy suitcase behind you at an airport. With the trolley, your body weight adds to the push force. With the suitcase, your **back muscles and spine** must generate every Newton of force — and you cannot see directly behind you. Industrial pulling (e.g., dragging a pallet, pulling a hand truck toward you, hauling a cable drum) is biomechanically the **less safe** of the two directional tasks. The worker is positioned **behind the load**, twisting the lumbar spine, with no assistance from body weight. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants | Constant | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Coefficient of Static Friction (rubber on concrete) | $\mu_s$ | **0.60 – 0.80** | dimensionless | | Coefficient of Kinetic Friction (rubber on concrete) | $\mu_k$ | **0.40 – 0.65** | dimensionless | | Coefficient of Friction (steel on steel, lubricated) | $\mu$ | **0.10 – 0.20** | dimensionless | | Recommended Maximum Initial Pull Force (men) | $F_{ip}$ | **204 N (≈ 21 kgf)** | Newton | | Recommended Maximum Sustained Pull Force (men) | $F_{sp}$ | **127 N (≈ 13 kgf)** | Newton | | Recommended Maximum Initial Pull Force (women) | $F_{ip}$ | **138 N (≈ 14 kgf)** | Newton | | Recommended Maximum Sustained Pull Force (women) | $F_{sp}$ | **89 N (≈ 9 kgf)** | Newton | | Load Constant (Snook-Ciriello, LC for pulling) | $LC$ | **23 kgf** | kgf | | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Two Critical Force Zones in Pulling:** > 1. **Initial Pull Force ($F_{ip}$):** The peak force required to **break static friction** and start the load moving. > 2. **Sustained Pull Force ($F_{sp}$):** The lower, continuous force required to **maintain motion** against kinetic friction. > The initial force is typically **1.5× to 2× the sustained force** and is the primary cause of acute back injuries during pulling. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Mechanics of a Pulling Task A pulling operation is decomposed by the industrial hygienist into **four discrete sub-events**: 1. **Coupling Phase** — Worker's hand grasps the handle/object. Posture assumed. 2. **Force Generation Phase** — Muscles (latissimus dorsi, biceps, deltoids, erector spinae) generate peak tensile force. 3. **Translation Phase** — Load moves across the floor; sustained (sub-maximal) force is maintained. 4. **Deceleration / Stop Phase** — Force may be required to bring the load to rest or to control coasting. ## 2.2 The Why & How Behind Each Phase - **Why initial force > sustained force?** Because static friction coefficient $\mu_s$ is always greater than kinetic friction coefficient $\mu_k$ (Amontons' First Law of Friction). - **How does posture affect force?** As the distance between the worker and the load increases (e.g., pulling a long rope, dragging a pallet with a strap), the **moment arm** at the lumbar spine increases, multiplying compressive load on the L5/S1 intervertebral disc (often exceeding the **NIOSH 3400 N action limit** and the **6400 N maximum permissible limit**). - **Why is pulling considered riskier than pushing?** Because: (a) The worker is **behind** the load → **restricted visual field** → trip hazards. (b) The **spine is hyperextended** in the typical pulling posture. (c) Body weight **cannot assist** force generation. (d) The lumbar spine undergoes **torsional loading** as the worker twists to begin the pull. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All High-Yield Equations for Pulling — Master These for the 14-Mark Question.** | # | Equation | Variables | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F_{ip} = \mu_s \cdot W$ | $F_{ip}$ = initial pull force, $\mu_s$ = static friction, $W$ = weight | Peak force to break static friction | | 2 | $F_{sp} = \mu_k \cdot W$ | $F_{sp}$ = sustained pull force, $\mu_k$ = kinetic friction, $W$ = weight | Force to maintain motion | | 3 | $F_{friction} = \mu \cdot N$ | $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $N$ = normal force | General friction (Amontons) | | 4 | $L5/S1 \text{ Compressive Force} = F_{pull} \times \cos(\theta) \times L_{arm}$ | $\theta$ = trunk angle from vertical, $L_{arm}$ = moment arm to L5/S1 | Spinal loading estimation | | 5 | $RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$ | LC = Load Constant (23 kgf for pulling), HM = Horizontal Multiplier, VM = Vertical Multiplier, DM = Distance Multiplier, AM = Asymmetry Multiplier, FM = Frequency Multiplier, CM = Coupling Multiplier | NIOSH-style recommended weight limit (Snook-Ciriello adaptation) | | 6 | $Lifting Index (LI) = \dfrac{Actual \ Weight}{RWL}$ | LI > 1.0 = increased risk, LI > 3.0 = high risk | Risk assessment ratio | | 7 | $Work = F \times d$ | $F$ = sustained pull force, $d$ = pull distance | Total mechanical work (Joules) | | 8 | $Power = \dfrac{F \times d}{t} = F \times v$ | $v$ = velocity of pull | Energy expenditure rate (Watts) | | 9 | $\text{Oxygen Cost} = 5.8 \times W + 1.5$ | $W$ = sustained force in kgf (Ayoub's equation, approx.) | Physiological cost of pull | | 10 | $\text{Metabolic Rate} = \dfrac{V_{O_2}}{3.5} + 1$ | $V_{O_2}$ in mL/kg/min | MET estimation | ## 2.4 Multiplier Values for the RWL Equation (Pulling Task) | Multiplier | Definition | Typical Range | Best Value | |---|---|---|---| | $HM$ (Horizontal) | Distance of hands from ankles at start of pull | 25 cm = 1.0; 63 cm = 0.40 | 0.40 – 1.00 | | $VM$ (Vertical) | Height of hands above floor | 0 cm = 0.87; 100 cm = 1.00 | 0.87 – 1.00 | | $DM$ (Distance) | Total vertical travel / pull distance | 1 m = 1.00; 50 m = 0.40 | 0.40 – 1.00 | | $AM$ (Asymmetry) | Angle of twist from sagittal plane | 0° = 1.00; 135° = 0.30 | 0.30 – 1.00 | | $FM$ (Frequency) | Lifts/pulls per minute | 1 F/min = 1.00; > 12 F/min = 0.10 | 0.10 – 1.00 | | $CM$ (Coupling) | Quality of hand-hold | Good = 1.00; Poor = 0.90 | 0.90 – 1.00 | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > The principles of *Pulling Hazard Control* are applied in: > - **Warehouse & Logistics:** Designing pallet jacks, hand trucks, and conveyor systems. > - **Construction:** Safe handling of rebar, scaffolding components, and cable drums. > - **Healthcare:** Patient transfer and repositioning tasks (manual handling regulations). > - **Manufacturing:** Tool design for assembly-line workstations. > - **Maritime/Offshore:** Mooring line handling — a classic high-risk pulling task. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Initial Pull Force to Break Static Friction **Problem Setup:** A worker must pull a steel toolbox of mass $m = 50$ kg across a concrete floor. The static coefficient of friction between the steel toolbox and concrete is $\mu_s = 0.65$. Compute the **initial pull force** required to break static friction. **Step 1 — Identify the force resisting motion.** When the toolbox is at rest on a horizontal surface, the normal force $N$ exerted by the floor on the box is equal to the weight of the box: $$N = m \cdot g$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.** $$N = 50 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$ $$N = 490.5 \text{ N}$$ This is the perpendicular force the floor exerts upward on the box to support it against gravity. **Step 3 — Apply Amontons' First Law of Friction.** The maximum static friction force (the threshold that must be overcome) is: $$F_{s,max} = \mu_s \cdot N$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values for the pull force.** $$F_{ip} = 0.65 \times 490.5 \text{ N}$$ $$F_{ip} = 318.825 \text{ N}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to kilogram-force for ergonomic comparison.** $$F_{ip} = \dfrac{318.825 \text{ N}}{9.81 \text{ m/s}^2} \approx 32.5 \text{ kgf}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** The recommended **sustained pull limit for men is 13 kgf (127 N)**. Our calculated $F_{ip} = 32.5$ kgf is **2.5× the safe limit**. This task is **UNSAFE** and requires engineering controls (e.g., replace casters, lubricate wheels, use mechanical aid). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Lifting Index (LI) for a Pulling Task **Problem Setup:** A female worker performs a pulling task on a hand truck with the following parameters: - Load weight $L = 15$ kg - Horizontal distance of hands from ankles $H = 50$ cm → $HM = 0.50$ - Vertical height of hands $V = 75$ cm → $VM = 0.93$ - Pull distance $D = 8$ m → $DM = 0.75$ - Asymmetry angle $A = 30°$ → $AM = 0.90$ - Frequency $F = 1$ pull/min → $FM = 0.94$ - Coupling = Poor (no handle) → $CM = 0.90$ **Step 1 — State the Snook-Ciriello RWL equation.** $$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the Load Constant (LC) for pulling.** For pulling tasks, the **Load Constant (LC) is 23 kgf** (this is the maximum acceptable weight for an ideal pulling task under perfect conditions). $$RWL = 23 \times 0.50 \times 0.93 \times 0.75 \times 0.90 \times 0.94 \times 0.90 \text{ kgf}$$ **Step 3 — Multiply progressively (showing all transitions).** $$RWL = 23 \times 0.50 = 11.50 \text{ kgf}$$ $$RWL = 11.50 \times 0.93 = 10.695 \text{ kgf}$$ $$RWL = 10.695 \times 0.75 = 8.02125 \text{ kgf}$$ $$RWL = 8.02125 \times 0.90 = 7.219125 \text{ kgf}$$ $$RWL = 7.219125 \times 0.94 = 6.7859775 \text{ kgf}$$ $$RWL = 6.7859775 \times 0.90 = 6.10737975 \text{ kgf}$$ **Step 4 — Round to two decimal places.** $$RWL \approx 6.11 \text{ kgf}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the Lifting Index (LI).** $$LI = \dfrac{L}{RWL} = \dfrac{15 \text{ kg}}{6.11 \text{ kg}}$$ $$LI \approx 2.46$$ **Step 6 — Interpret the result.** | LI Value | Risk Level | Action Required | |---|---|---| | $LI \leq 1.0$ | Low | Task is acceptable | | $1.0 < LI \leq 2.0$ | Moderate | Administrative controls, training | | $2.0 < LI \leq 3.0$ | High | Engineering controls needed | | $LI > 3.0$ | Very High | Task redesign mandatory | > [!WARNING] > With $LI = 2.46$, the task is in the **HIGH RISK** category. Immediate engineering controls (e.g., install proper handle, reduce pull distance, use powered hand truck) are required. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Spinal Compressive Force During Pulling (Biomechanical Model) **Problem Setup:** A worker pulls a 200 N force at a horizontal distance of 40 cm from the L5/S1 disc, with the trunk inclined at 60° from vertical. Body weight above L5/S1 is approximated as 350 N with a moment arm of 15 cm. Estimate the **compressive force on the L5/S1 disc** using a simplified static equilibrium model. **Step 1 — Free body diagram components.** The forces acting on the trunk above the L5/S1 disc are: - Pulling force $F_{pull} = 200$ N at horizontal distance 40 cm - Upper body weight $W_{ub} = 350$ N at horizontal distance 15 cm - Trunk inclination angle from vertical $\theta = 60°$ **Step 2 — Resolve the pull force into horizontal and vertical components.** $$F_{pull,h} = F_{pull} \cdot \sin(\theta) = 200 \times \sin(60°)$$ $$F_{pull,h} = 200 \times 0.8660 = 173.20 \text{ N}$$ $$F_{pull,v} = F_{pull} \cdot \cos(\theta) = 200 \times \cos(60°)$$ $$F_{pull,v} = 200 \times 0.5 = 100 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Sum moments about L5/S1 disc to find the erector spinae muscle force ($F_{es}$).** Moment equilibrium: $\sum M_{L5/S1} = 0$ $$F_{es} \times d_{es} = F_{pull,h} \times 40 + W_{ub} \times 15$$ Assume the erector spinae moment arm $d_{es} = 5$ cm (0.05 m). $$F_{es} \times 5 = 173.20 \times 40 + 350 \times 15$$ $$F_{es} \times 5 = 6928 + 5250 = 12178 \text{ N·cm}$$ $$F_{es} = \dfrac{12178}{5} = 2435.6 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Compute total compressive force on L5/S1 disc.** The compressive force is the vertical sum of all downward forces plus the vertical pull component: $$F_{comp} = F_{es} \cdot \cos(\theta) + W_{ub} \cdot \cos(0°) + F_{pull,v}$$ $$F_{comp} = 2435.6 \times \cos(60°) + 350 \times 1 + 100$$ $$F_{comp} = 2435.6 \times 0.5 + 350 + 100$$ $$F_{comp} = 1217.8 + 350 + 100$$ $$F_{comp} = 1667.8 \text{ N}$$ **Step 5 — Compare to NIOSH limits.** | Threshold | Value | Status | |---|---|---| | NIOSH Action Limit | 3400 N | **Safe (below AL)** | | NIOSH Maximum Permissible Limit | 6400 N | Safe | > [!NOTE] > In this case, $F_{comp} = 1667.8$ N is well within the safe range. However, doubling the pull force (400 N) or doubling the horizontal distance would push the compressive force into the **action limit zone**, demonstrating how quickly pulling tasks escalate in risk. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy Expenditure and Metabolic Cost **Step 1 — Compute mechanical work done during the pull.** $$W = F_{sp} \times d$$ Assume $F_{sp} = 127$ N and $d = 10$ m: $$W = 127 \text{ N} \times 10 \text{ m} = 1270 \text{ J}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the power (rate of work).** If the pull takes 30 seconds: $$P = \dfrac{W}{t} = \dfrac{1270}{30} = 42.33 \text{ W}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to oxygen consumption using Ayoub's regression.** Ayoub's model for sustained pulling: $$V_{O_2} = 5.8 \times W_{kgf} + 1.5 \text{ (mL/kg/min)}$$ Where $W_{kgf} = 127 / 9.81 = 12.95$ kgf: $$V_{O_2} = 5.8 \times 12.95 + 1.5 = 75.11 + 1.5 = 76.61 \text{ mL/kg/min}$$ **Step 4 — Compare to permissible energy expenditure limits.** | Workload | Sustained $V_{O_2}$ Limit | |---|---| | Light (8-hr shift) | < 12 mL/kg/min | | Moderate (8-hr shift) | 12 – 21 mL/kg/min | | Heavy (8-hr shift) | 21 – 32 mL/kg/min | | Very Heavy (max 4 hrs) | > 32 mL/kg/min | > [!WARNING] > Our $V_{O_2} = 76.61$ mL/kg/min places this task in the **VERY HEAVY** category — a maximum of 4 hours per shift, with rest pauses every 30 minutes. This is a strong indicator that the task needs redesign. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Pulling Hazard Analysis Framework ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Worker Initiates Pull Task] --> B[Coupling Phase
Hand Grip Acquired] B --> C[Force Generation Phase
Peak Force Fip Required] C --> D[Static Friction Broken
mu_s x N Overcome] D --> E[Translation Phase
Sustained Force Fsp] E --> F[Kinetic Friction Acts
Fsp = mu_k x W] F --> G[Deceleration Phase
Load Brought to Rest] G --> H{Outcome Assessment} H -->|Fip within RWL| I[ACCEPTABLE TASK
Continue Operation] H -->|Fip exceeds RWL| J[HAZARD IDENTIFIED
Trigger WMSD Risk] J --> K[Engineering Controls
Wheel Lubrication
Powered Hand Trucks] J --> L[Administrative Controls
Job Rotation
Reduced Frequency] J --> M[PPE / Training
Back Belts
Lifting Technique] K --> N[Re-evaluate Task
New LI Computed] L --> N M --> N I --> O[Periodic Monitoring
Every 6 Months] N --> O ``` ## 4.2 Force Decomposition Diagram — Pulling Task Biomechanics ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1[Input Forces on Worker] F1[Applied Pull Force
Fpull = 200 N] F2[Upper Body Weight
Wub = 350 N] F3[Gravity
g = 9.81 m/s2] end subgraph S2[Moment Arms] A1[d1 = 40 cm
Horizontal Pull Arm] A2[d2 = 15 cm
Trunk Weight Arm] A3[des = 5 cm
Erector Spinae Arm] end subgraph S3[Internal Spinal Loads] L1[Erector Spinae Force
Fes = 2435.6 N] L2[Compressive Force
Fcomp = 1667.8 N] L3[Shear Force
Fshear = 173.2 N] end subgraph S4[Comparison to Limits] Cmp1[NIOSH Action Limit: 3400 N] Cmp2[NIOSH MPL: 6400 N] end F1 --> A1 F2 --> A2 F3 --> A2 A1 --> L1 A2 --> L1 A3 --> L1 L1 --> L2 F1 --> L3 L2 --> Cmp1 Cmp1 --> Cmp2 ``` ## 4.3 Control Hierarchy Flowchart for Pulling Hazards ```mermaid flowchart TD H1[Hazard Identified
Pull Force Exceeds RWL] --> L1[Level 1: Elimination
Remove manual pull task] L1 --> L2[Level 2: Substitution
Use powered equipment] L2 --> L3[Level 3: Engineering Controls
Low friction wheels
Proper handles
Conveyors] L3 --> L4[Level 4: Administrative Controls
Job rotation
Training
Rest breaks] L4 --> L5[Level 5: PPE
Back support
Gloves
Safety footwear] L5 --> M[Residual Risk Acceptable?
LI less than 1.0] M -->|Yes| Done[Task Approved] M -->|No| Rev[Return to Level 1
Redesign Task] Rev --> L1 ``` ## 4.4 Force-Time Profile of a Typical Pull Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Phase A: 0 to 2 sec
Ramp Up
Force rises from 0 to Fip] P2[Phase B: 2 to 4 sec
Peak Plateau
Force at Fip = 318.8 N] P3[Phase C: 4 to 30 sec
Sustained Motion
Force drops to Fsp = 196 N] P4[Phase D: 30 to 32 sec
Deceleration
Force spikes to 220 N] P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> R1[Cycle Repeats
Every 60 sec approx] R1 --> P1 ``` > [!NOTE] > The force-time profile above is **biomechanically critical** — the **Peak Plateau (Phase B)** is the moment of greatest spinal injury risk. The 2-second peak duration is the industry-standard assumption for NIOSH calculations. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define "Pulling" as a manual material handling task. List the four phases of a pulling cycle and state the two force parameters used to evaluate pulling safety.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** Pulling is a manual material handling operation in which a worker exerts force on an object to draw it toward the body along a horizontal or near-horizontal plane, in order to translate the load. **Four Phases of a Pulling Cycle:** 1. **Coupling Phase** — Worker's hand engages with the object (grip acquisition). 2. **Force Generation Phase** — Muscles generate peak tensile force. 3. **Translation Phase** — The load is moved across the surface. 4. **Deceleration Phase** — The load is brought to rest. **Two Force Parameters:** - **Initial Pull Force ($F_{ip}$):** Peak force required to overcome static friction. - **Sustained Pull Force ($F_{sp}$):** Force required to maintain motion against kinetic friction. > **Examiner's Note (3-mark distribution):** Definition (1 mark) + Four phases (1 mark) + Two force parameters (1 mark). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **State the Snook-Ciriello Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) equation. List any four multipliers and explain the role of the Frequency Multiplier (FM) in detail.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Snook-Ciriello RWL Equation:** > $$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$ **Four Multipliers (out of six):** 1. **HM — Horizontal Multiplier** (reduces RWL as hands move further from ankles) 2. **VM — Vertical Multiplier** (relates to hand height above floor) 3. **AM — Asymmetry Multiplier** (penalizes trunk twisting) 4. **CM — Coupling Multiplier** (accounts for quality of hand-hold) **Role of Frequency Multiplier (FM):** The FM penalizes high-frequency pulling tasks. It is derived from the **physiological recovery time** required between pulls. As the number of pulls per minute increases (from 1 F/min to 12+ F/min), the FM decreases from **1.00 to 0.10**, thereby reducing the safe RWL. | Frequency | FM | |---|---| | 1 F/min (sustained) | 1.00 | | 2 F/min | 0.95 | | 6 F/min | 0.60 | | 12 F/min | 0.10 | | > 12 F/min | Not permissible | > **Examiner's Note:** Equation statement (1 mark) + Four multipliers named (1 mark) + FM explanation with table (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** **A worker is performing a pulling task on a wheeled cart with the following details:** - **Load weight:** $L = 25$ kg - **Horizontal distance of hands from ankles:** $H = 40$ cm → $HM = 0.60$ - **Vertical height of hands above floor:** $V = 90$ cm → $VM = 0.97$ - **Pull distance:** $D = 5$ m → $DM = 0.81$ - **Asymmetry angle of twist:** $A = 45°$ → $AM = 0.79$ - **Frequency of pulls:** $F = 2$ pulls/min → $FM = 0.95$ - **Coupling quality:** Fair → $CM = 0.95$ - **Coefficient of static friction:** $\mu_s = 0.55$ - **Coefficient of kinetic friction:** $\mu_k = 0.40$ **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compute the Recommended Weight Limit (RWL), the Lifting Index (LI), and classify the risk. Recommend appropriate engineering controls. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the initial pull force ($F_{ip}$) and sustained pull force ($F_{sp}$) required to move this load. Compare these to the Liberty Mutual recommended limits for a male worker and comment on safety. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]** **Step 1 — Apply the RWL equation.** [1 mark] $$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$ For a pulling task, the **Load Constant $LC = 23$ kgf**. **Step 2 — Substitute the multiplier values.** [1 mark] $$RWL = 23 \times 0.60 \times 0.97 \times 0.81 \times 0.79 \times 0.95 \times 0.95 \text{ kgf}$$ **Step 3 — Perform progressive multiplication.** [2 marks] $$RWL = 23 \times 0.60 = 13.80 \text{ kgf}$$ $$RWL = 13.80 \times 0.97 = 13.386 \text{ kgf}$$ $$RWL = 13.386 \times 0.81 = 10.8427 \text{ kgf}$$ $$RWL = 10.8427 \times 0.79 = 8.5657 \text{ kgf}$$ $$RWL = 8.5657 \times 0.95 = 8.1374 \text{ kgf}$$ $$RWL = 8.1374 \times 0.95 = 7.7306 \text{ kgf}$$ $$\boxed{RWL \approx 7.73 \text{ kgf}}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the Lifting Index.** [1 mark] $$LI = \dfrac{L}{RWL} = \dfrac{25}{7.73} = 3.23$$ **Step 5 — Classify the risk and recommend controls.** [2 marks] | LI Value | Risk Category | Required Action | |---|---|---| | $LI = 3.23$ | **Very High** (LI > 3.0) | **Task redesign mandatory** | **Recommended Engineering Controls:** 1. Install **ball-bearing casters** to reduce $\mu_s$ and $\mu_k$ (cuts required force by 50–70%). 2. Add a **powered drive system** to the cart (electric tug). 3. Improve coupling by adding a **proper ergonomic handle** (improves CM from 0.95 to 1.00). 4. Reduce pull distance to < 2 m by relocating the workstation. 5. Eliminate asymmetry (rotate the workstation so the cart approaches straight-on). --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]** **Step 1 — Calculate the weight force (normal force).** [1 mark] $$W = m \cdot g = 25 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 = 245.25 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate the initial pull force ($F_{ip}$).** [2 marks] $$F_{ip} = \mu_s \cdot W = 0.55 \times 245.25 = 134.89 \text{ N}$$ Convert to kgf: $$F_{ip} = \dfrac{134.89}{9.81} = 13.75 \text{ kgf}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate the sustained pull force ($F_{sp}$).** [2 marks] $$F_{sp} = \mu_k \cdot W = 0.40 \times 245.25 = 98.10 \text{ N}$$ Convert to kgf: $$F_{sp} = \dfrac{98.10}{9.81} = 10.0 \text{ kgf}$$ **Step 4 — Compare to Liberty Mutual recommended limits for a male worker.** [1 mark] | Parameter | Calculated | Recommended Limit (Male) | Status | |---|---|---|---| | Initial Pull Force | 13.75 kgf | **21 kgf** | ✓ Safe | | Sustained Pull Force | 10.0 kgf | **13 kgf** | ✓ Safe | **Step 5 — Safety Commentary.** [1 mark] > [!NOTE] > Both the initial and sustained pull forces are **within the Liberty Mutual recommended limits** for a male worker. The friction-based assessment suggests the task is **physically feasible** for an average male. However, the **Lifting Index (LI = 3.23) from Part (a) classifies the task as very high risk** due to **postural multipliers (HM, AM, DM)**. The two assessments provide **complementary** risk information: > - The **force-based** approach (Liberty Mutual) focuses on **physiological** limits. > - The **RWL/LI** approach (Snook-Ciriello) integrates **biomechanical, postural, and frequency** factors. > A comprehensive safety evaluation must consider **both** methods. In this case, although the raw force is within limits, the unfavorable posture (asymmetry 45°, distance 5 m) renders the task unsafe, and engineering controls are warranted. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **In a maritime mooring operation, a deckhand must pull a mooring rope to dock a vessel. The following data are provided:** - **Pull force exerted:** $F = 300$ N applied at a horizontal distance of 35 cm from the L5/S1 disc. - **Trunk inclination from vertical:** $\theta = 55°$ - **Upper body weight above L5/S1:** $W_{ub} = 380$ N, acting at 18 cm horizontal distance. - **Erector spinae moment arm:** $d_{es} = 5.5$ cm. - **Task duration:** 12 seconds per pull, repeated 4 times per minute for a 6-hour shift. **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the L5/S1 compressive force using static biomechanical equilibrium. Compare to NIOSH action limit (3400 N) and maximum permissible limit (6400 N). **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the oxygen consumption ($V_{O_2}$) using Ayoub's equation and the total energy expended per shift. Classify the workload and recommend rest-pause cycles per OSHA guidelines. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]** **Step 1 — Resolve the pull force into horizontal and vertical components.** [2 marks] $$F_{pull,h} = F \cdot \sin(\theta) = 300 \times \sin(55°) = 300 \times 0.8192 = 245.76 \text{ N}$$ $$F_{pull,v} = F \cdot \cos(\theta) = 300 \times \cos(55°) = 300 \times 0.5736 = 172.08 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — Apply moment equilibrium about L5/S1 to find erector spinae force.** [2 marks] $$\sum M_{L5/S1} = 0 \Rightarrow F_{es} \cdot d_{es} = F_{pull,h} \cdot 35 + W_{ub} \cdot 18$$ $$F_{es} \times 5.5 = 245.76 \times 35 + 380 \times 18$$ $$F_{es} \times 5.5 = 8601.6 + 6840 = 15441.6 \text{ N·cm}$$ $$F_{es} = \dfrac{15441.6}{5.5} = 2807.56 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Compute total compressive force on L5/S1 disc.** [2 marks] $$F_{comp} = F_{es} \cdot \cos(\theta) + W_{ub} + F_{pull,v}$$ $$F_{comp} = 2807.56 \times 0.5736 + 380 + 172.08$$ $$F_{comp} = 1610.43 + 380 + 172.08$$ $$\boxed{F_{comp} \approx 2162.51 \text{ N}}$$ **Step 4 — Compare to NIOSH limits and provide safety verdict.** [1 mark] | Threshold | Value | Status | |---|---|---| | NIOSH Action Limit | 3400 N | **Below AL (Safe)** | | NIOSH MPL | 6400 N | **Below MPL (Safe)** | > [!NOTE] > The compressive force of **2162.51 N** is **below the NIOSH Action Limit of 3400 N**, indicating acceptable spinal loading for a **single pull**. However, the task is **repeated 4 times per minute for 6 hours** (1440 pulls/shift), making the **cumulative trauma risk** a major concern. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]** **Step 1 — Calculate mechanical work per pull.** [1 mark] $$W_{pull} = F \cdot d = 300 \text{ N} \times 0.35 \text{ m} = 105 \text{ J}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate power (rate of work).** [1 mark] $$P = \dfrac{W_{pull}}{t} = \dfrac{105}{12} = 8.75 \text{ W}$$ **Step 3 — Convert pull force to kgf and apply Ayoub's equation.** [2 marks] $$F_{kgf} = \dfrac{300}{9.81} = 30.58 \text{ kgf}$$ $$V_{O_2} = 5.8 \times F_{kgf} + 1.5 = 5.8 \times 30.58 + 1.5 = 177.36 + 1.5 = 178.86 \text{ mL/kg/min}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate total energy expenditure per shift.** [2 marks] Effective work time per shift: 4 pulls/min × 12 sec/pull = 48 sec/min = 0.8 min/min Total active time: 0.8 × 6 × 60 = **288 minutes (4.8 hours) of active pulling**. $$V_{O_2,total} = 178.86 \text{ mL/kg/min} \times 288 \text{ min} = 51511.68 \text{ mL/kg}$$ $$E_{total} = V_{O_2,total} \times 20.1 \text{ J/mL O}_2 = 51511.68 \times 20.1 = 1035385 \text{ J} \approx 1035 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 5 — Classify workload and recommend rest-pause schedule.** [1 mark] | Workload Category | Sustained $V_{O_2}$ Range | Our Task | Status | |---|---|---|---| | Light | < 12 mL/kg/min | – | – | | Moderate | 12–21 mL/kg/min | – | – | | Heavy | 21–32 mL/kg/min | – | – | | **Very Heavy** | **> 32 mL/kg/min** | **178.86 mL/kg/min** | ✓ **Very Heavy** | > [!WARNING] > **OSHA Rest-Pause Recommendation for Very Heavy Work:** > - Maximum continuous work period: **30 minutes** > - Mandatory rest period: **15 minutes** > - Maximum total daily work duration: **4 hours** active (out of 6-hour shift) > - **Engineering controls are strongly recommended:** install **mechanical capstan/winch** to eliminate manual rope pulling entirely. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in "Pulling" Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing $F_{ip}$ and $F_{sp}$:** Students often use kinetic friction for initial force calculations. Remember: **Initial force uses STATIC friction ($\mu_s$); Sustained force uses KINETIC friction ($\mu_k$).** This single error costs 2-3 marks. > > 2. **Forgetting to use the Load Constant (LC = 23 kgf) for pulling:** The NIOSH equation uses **23 kgf** for pulling and **lifting** (in some textbooks it is shown as 51 kgf for lifting from the original 1991 NIOSH equation). Always verify the task type before using LC. > > 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing Newtons and kgf without conversion. A common error: writing $F = 127$ N and comparing it to a 13 kgf limit. Always convert: $127 \text{ N} / 9.81 = 12.95 \approx 13$ kgf. > > 4. **Ignoring Asymmetry Multiplier (AM):** Many students compute LI without including the AM, leading to an **overestimated RWL** and an **underestimated LI** — a safety-critical error. > > 5. **Oxygen Consumption Misapplication:** Ayoub's equation requires force in **kgf**, not Newtons. Failure to convert is a 1-mark deduction. > > 6. **Forgetting to state the conclusion:** Always end with a **risk classification** and a **recommendation**. Examiners award 1-2 marks for the conclusion alone. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Pulling" (Module 4, PCSFT601)** - [x] **Pulling** is a manual material handling task in which a worker draws a load **towards** the body. - [x] **Two key forces:** Initial Pull Force ($F_{ip} = \mu_s W$) and Sustained Pull Force ($F_{sp} = \mu_k W$). - [x] **Initial force is always greater** than sustained force (because $\mu_s > \mu_k$). - [x] **Snook-Ciriello RWL Equation:** $RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$. - [x] **Load Constant (LC) for pulling = 23 kgf.** - [x] **Lifting Index (LI) = Actual Load / RWL.** - [x] **LI > 1.0 = increased risk; LI > 3.0 = task redesign mandatory.** - [x] **Liberty Mutual Recommended Limits (Male):** $F_{ip}$ = 21 kgf (204 N); $F_{sp}$ = 13 kgf (127 N). - [x] **Liberty Mutual Recommended Limits (Female):** $F_{ip}$ = 14 kgf (138 N); $F_{sp}$ = 9 kgf (89 N). - [x] **NIOSH Action Limit (Spinal Compression):** 3400 N. - [x] **NIOSH Maximum Permissible Limit:** 6400 N. - [x] **Ayoub's Oxygen Equation:** $V_{O_2} = 5.8 \times F_{kgf} + 1.5$ mL/kg/min. - [x] **Pulling is riskier than pushing** because: (a) worker is behind the load, (b) spine hyperextended, (c) no body weight assist, (d) torsional loading. - [x] **Four phases of pulling cycle:** Coupling → Force Generation → Translation → Deceleration. - [x] **Hierarchy of controls for pulling hazards:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE. - [x] **High-risk industries for pulling injuries:** Maritime mooring, warehouse logistics, construction rebar handling, healthcare patient transfer, manufacturing assembly lines. - [x] **Spinal compression calculation** uses moment equilibrium about L5/S1 disc with erector spinae moment arm of ~5 cm. - [x] **Workload classification:** Light (<12), Moderate (12-21), Heavy (21-32), Very Heavy (>32) mL/kg/min. - [x] **OSHA rest-pause for very heavy work:** 30 min work + 15 min rest, max 4 hrs active per shift. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Tip:** For a 14-mark question on "Pulling," the examiner expects: > 1. **Correct identification** of the task type (pulling vs. lifting). > 2. **Use of the correct Load Constant (LC = 23 kgf).** > 3. **At least 2-3 multipliers calculated explicitly** in the RWL equation. > 4. **A numerical Lifting Index with risk classification.** > 5. **An engineering control recommendation.** > 6. **A clear, well-labeled free body diagram** for biomechanical problems. > 7. **Conversion to kgf** when comparing to Liberty Mutual limits. > 8. **A final concluding statement** identifying the safety verdict.

topics.title: Pushing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT601 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pushing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Industrial Hazard Control**, **Pushing** is defined as a manual material handling (MMH) operation in which a worker applies a directed muscular force to an object in order to move it **away** from the body along a horizontal trajectory, typically by exerting force with the hands, shoulders, or whole body against a stationary or slow-moving load (such as a cart, trolley, pallet jack, or wheeled container).

It is classified under the broader discipline of **Manual Handling Operations**, which includes lifting, lowering, pushing, pulling, carrying, and holding. From a hazard-control standpoint, pushing is considered a high-risk biomechanical activity because it generates compressive and shear forces on the lumbar spine, and when performed incorrectly, is a leading cause of cumulative trauma disorders (CTDs) and work-related musculoskeletal disorders (WMSDs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**
> *"Pushing is the exertion of force by an operator on an object so that the object moves away from the operator's body in a roughly horizontal direction, with the operator's feet remaining in contact with the ground and the force vector directed forward."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing behind a **supermarket shopping cart** loaded with groceries. When you lean forward and press the handle, the cart rolls ahead — that is **pushing**. Now imagine standing in front of a heavy office desk and sliding it across the floor with your palms — that is pushing too, but without wheels.

**Real-world industrial analogy:** A warehouse worker moving a loaded pallet jack from one rack to another across a smooth concrete floor. The act of "pushing" here is what the safety officer must evaluate, because the force the worker exerts can injure the back, shoulders, and wrists if it exceeds safe thresholds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition**
> Think of pushing as **"leaning the body forward against the load and walking after it."** The force you apply must overcome the resistance of the load (mostly friction), and once the load is moving, you only need to *sustain* that motion — not re-accelerate it. This distinction between **initial force** and **sustained (or sliding) force** is the single most important concept for KTU exams.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following constants and thresholds are universally cited in industrial ergonomics literature:

- **Coefficient of Friction (μ)** — dimensionless ratio of frictional force to normal force.
- **Initial Force Threshold for Safe Pushing** ≈ **230 N (≈ 23 kgf)** for a male operator in an optimal posture.
- **Sustained (Sliding) Force Threshold for Safe Pushing** ≈ **140 N (≈ 14 kgf)**.
- **Standard Wheel Diameter (industrial carts)** = **100 mm – 250 mm**.
- **Recommended Handle Height** = **900 mm – 1100 mm** from the floor.
- **Recommended Pushing Speed** ≤ **1.35 m/s** (≈ 5 km/h).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These thresholds come from recognized ergonomic standards such as the **NIOSH Manual Handling Guide**, **ISO 11228-2**, and the **Snook-Ciriello workload guide**. They are the **benchmark numerical values** you must memorize for KTU Part A and Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of a Pushing Task — Structured Logic Steps

A complete pushing operation can be decomposed into the following sequential phases:

1. **Preparation / Grip Phase** — The worker positions both hands on the handle (or contact surface), ensuring shoulder width stance and neutral wrist posture.
2. **Force Initiation Phase** — The worker generates an *initial force* (F_initial) large enough to overcome *static friction* and start the load moving from rest.
3. **Acceleration Phase** — The load accelerates from rest up to the desired travelling velocity. The required force here equals F_static + (m × a).
4. **Sustained Travel Phase** — The load is in motion; only *kinetic friction* must be overcome. Required force drops to F_sustained = μ_kinetic × N.
5. **Deceleration / Stopping Phase** — The worker gradually reduces the force to bring the load to rest, avoiding sudden jerky stops.
6. **Recovery / Reset Phase** — The worker releases the handle, repositions the body, and prepares for the next cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?**
> Examiners frequently ask: *"Why is the initial force greater than the sustained force?"* The answer lies in **static vs kinetic friction** — a concept frequently tested in Part B 14-mark questions.

## 2.2 Biomechanics of the Pushing Action

When a person pushes a load:

- The **spine** experiences **compressive forces** that can reach **2000 N – 3500 N** even in moderate pushing tasks. The **NIOSH Action Limit (AL)** for lumbar compression is **3400 N**.
- The **shoulders** generate the primary horizontal force vector through the **pectoralis major, deltoid, and triceps** muscle groups.
- The **legs** provide stabilization and partial forward drive.
- The **hands and wrists** transmit the force and are vulnerable to **carpal tunnel syndrome** if the handle design is poor.

The spinal compression formula is derived from a free-body diagram of the upper body:

$$
F_{spine} = F_{push} \times \tan(\theta_{trunk}) + W_{upper\,body}
$$

Where $\theta_{trunk}$ is the trunk flexion angle from vertical, and $W_{upper\,body}$ is the weight of the upper body acting at its center of mass.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Description | Units | Use in Exam |
|---|-------------------|----------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $F_{friction} = \mu \cdot N$ | Frictional resistance force | N (Newtons) | To compute resistance of load |
| 2 | $N = m \cdot g$ | Normal reaction force (horizontal load) | N | For load on flat floor |
| 3 | $F_{initial} = \mu_s \cdot N + m \cdot a$ | Initial pushing force to start motion | N | Distinguish from sustained force |
| 4 | $F_{sustained} = \mu_k \cdot N$ | Force to keep load moving at constant speed | N | Used for repetitive pushing |
| 5 | $\mu_s > \mu_k$ | Static coefficient always exceeds kinetic | dimensionless | Explains why start is harder |
| 6 | $W = F \cdot d$ | Work done during push | Joules (J) | Energy expenditure calc |
| 7 | $P = F \cdot v$ | Power required for sustained push | Watts (W) | Real-time work rate |
| 8 | $F_{spine} = F_{push} \tan\theta + W_{UB}$ | Lumbar compression estimate | N | Compare with NIOSH AL = 3400 N |
| 9 | Safe $F_{initial} \leq 230$ N | NIOSH-recommended initial force ceiling | N | Risk assessment benchmark |
| 10 | Safe $F_{sustained} \leq 140$ N | NIOSH-recommended sustained force ceiling | N | Risk assessment benchmark |
| 11 | $M = F_{push} \cdot h_{handle}$ | Moment about the load's wheel base | N·m | Stability & tipping analysis |
| 12 | $h_{handle} = 900$ to $1100$ mm | Recommended handle height | mm | Ergonomic design criterion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Alert:** In any KTU submission or table, never use the vertical bar `|` to mean absolute value or modulus. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` inside LaTeX expressions to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pushing analysis is critical in:

- **Warehouse & Logistics Engineering** — Designing cart handles, wheel diameters, and floor surfaces to minimize operator fatigue.
- **Hospital & Healthcare Ergonomics** — Bed pushing, wheelchair propulsion, stretcher handling.
- **Manufacturing Line Design** — Determining if conveyor belts, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), or manual trolleys are appropriate.
- **Construction Safety** — Wheelbarrow operation, slab pushing, equipment relocation.
- **Automotive Assembly** — Body panel movement on assembly-line skids.

In every application, the engineer must answer: **"Can a worker of average strength safely push this load over this distance at this frequency without exceeding NIOSH biomechanical limits?"**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Initial Pushing Force vs Sustained Pushing Force

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU Numericals):**
A worker pushes a loaded trolley of total mass $M = 250 \text{ kg}$ across a horizontal concrete floor. The coefficient of static friction is $\mu_s = 0.4$ and kinetic friction is $\mu_k = 0.3$. The worker accelerates the trolley from rest to a constant speed of $v = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$ in a distance of $s = 1.0 \text{ m}$. Compute (a) the initial force, (b) the sustained force, and (c) the work done during the first second.

**Given:**
- $M = 250 \text{ kg}$
- $\mu_s = 0.4$
- $\mu_k = 0.3$
- $v = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$
- $s = 1.0 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step (a): Compute Normal Reaction $N$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= M \cdot g \\
N &= 250 \times 9.81 \\
N &= 2452.5 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b): Compute Initial Pushing Force $F_{initial}$**

The worker must overcome static friction AND provide the acceleration. From kinematics:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v^2 &= u^2 + 2 a s \\
(0.5)^2 &= 0^2 + 2 \cdot a \cdot 1.0 \\
0.25 &= 2 a \\
a &= 0.125 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

By Newton's Second Law on the trolley:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{initial} - \mu_s \cdot N &= M \cdot a \\
F_{initial} &= M \cdot a + \mu_s \cdot N \\
F_{initial} &= (250)(0.125) + (0.4)(2452.5) \\
F_{initial} &= 31.25 + 981.0 \\
F_{initial} &= 1012.25 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The initial force required is approximately **1012 N**, which is about **4.4 times** the NIOSH safe threshold of **230 N**. This task is therefore **NOT safe** for a single worker in the current configuration.

**Step (c): Compute Sustained Pushing Force $F_{sustained}$**

Once the trolley is moving at constant speed, acceleration is zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{sustained} - \mu_k \cdot N &= M \cdot 0 \\
F_{sustained} &= \mu_k \cdot N \\
F_{sustained} &= (0.3)(2452.5) \\
F_{sustained} &= 735.75 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (d): Work Done During the First Second**

Distance covered in 1 second at final constant speed (assuming average of accelerated + sustained phase):

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{1s} &= \frac{1}{2} a t^2 + v t \\
d_{1s} &= \frac{1}{2}(0.125)(1)^2 + (0.5)(1) \\
d_{1s} &= 0.0625 + 0.5 \\
d_{1s} &= 0.5625 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Work done:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= F_{sustained} \cdot d_{1s} \\
W &= (735.75)(0.5625) \\
W &\approx 413.86 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spinal Compression During Pushing

**Setup:** A worker of upper-body weight $W_{UB} = 350 \text{ N}$ pushes a load with force $F_{push} = 200 \text{ N}$ while the trunk is flexed at $\theta = 20^\circ$ from vertical. Estimate the L5/S1 lumbar disc compression.

**Formula:**

$$
F_{spine} = F_{push} \cdot \tan(\theta) + W_{UB}
$$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(20^\circ) &= 0.364 \\
F_{spine} &= (200)(0.364) + 350 \\
F_{spine} &= 72.8 + 350 \\
F_{spine} &= 422.8 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compare with NIOSH Action Limit:**

$$
F_{spine} = 422.8 \text{ N} \;\ll\; AL = 3400 \text{ N}
$$

The task is biomechanically safe under this idealized assumption. However, if $F_{push}$ were raised to **1500 N** (a heavy pallet push), the same posture would yield:

$$
F_{spine} = (1500)(0.364) + 350 = 546 + 350 = 896 \text{ N}
$$

Still under AL, but combined with twisting or lifting, it could easily exceed safe limits.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Risk vs Control Decision Table

| Risk Factor | Low Risk | Moderate Risk | High Risk | Control Measure |
|-------------|----------|---------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Initial Force | < 100 N | 100–230 N | > 230 N | Power-assist wheels / motorized tugs |
| Sustained Force | < 70 N | 70–140 N | > 140 N | Reduce load; lubricate wheels |
| Handle Height | 900–1100 mm | 800–900 mm | < 800 mm | Adjustable handles |
| Trunk Flexion | < 10° | 10–25° | > 25° | Operator training + foot placement |
| Push Distance | < 5 m | 5–15 m | > 15 m | Use powered conveyors |
| Push Frequency | < 1/min | 1–4/min | > 4/min | Job rotation, micro-breaks |
| Floor Surface | Smooth, dry | Slightly rough | Cracked, oily | Resurface / anti-skid coating |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pushing Risk Assessment System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["WORKER INPUT"] --> nodeB["FORCE GENERATION"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["BIOMECHANICAL LOAD ON SPINE SHOULDERS WRISTS"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["TASK PARAMETERS LOAD WEIGHT FRICTION DISTANCE FREQUENCY"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["RISK EVALUATION MODULE"]
    nodeE --> nodeF{"EXCEEDS NIOSH LIMITS"}
    nodeF -- YES --> nodeG["HAZARD IDENTIFIED"]
    nodeF -- NO --> nodeH["TASK ACCEPTABLE"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["ELIMINATION"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["SUBSTITUTION POWERED TROLLEY"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["ENGINEERING CONTROLS WHEELS LUBRICATION HANDLES"]
    nodeI --> nodeM["ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS TRAINING ROTATION"]
    nodeI --> nodeN["PPE GLOVES BELT BACK SUPPORT"]
    H --> nodeO["DOCUMENT AND MONITOR"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Free Body of Worker Pushing a Trolley

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeW["WORKER"] -->|"Fp = Push Force"| nodeT["TROLLEY"]
    nodeT -->|"Ff = μN Friction Opposing Motion"| nodeG["FLOOR"]
    nodeT -->|"N = Mg Normal Reaction"| nodeG
    nodeW -->|"Wub = Upper Body Weight"| nodeL["L5 S1 LUMBAR DISC"]
    nodeT -->|"Fc = Compressive Load"| nodeL
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Decision Logic for Force Compliance Check

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["MEASURED INITIAL FORCE F_ini"] --> nodeCheck1{"F_ini ≤ 230 N"}
    nodeCheck1 -- YES --> nodeCheck2["INITIAL FORCE OK"]
    nodeCheck1 -- NO --> nodeAlert1["INITIAL FORCE EXCEEDS LIMIT — APPLY ENGINEERING CONTROL"]
    nodeCheck2 --> nodeSus{"MEASURED SUSTAINED FORCE F_sus"}
    nodeSus --> nodeSusCheck{"F_sus ≤ 140 N"}
    nodeSusCheck -- YES --> nodeOk["FULL TASK COMPLIANT WITH NIOSH"]
    nodeSusCheck -- NO --> nodeAlert2["SUSTAINED FORCE EXCEEDS LIMIT — REDUCE LOAD OR USE POWERED EQUIPMENT"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams Instead of Physical Sketches?**
> Pushing biomechanics involve complex 3-D body postures. A Mermaid **functional flow** captures the decision-making logic and the *interaction between forces, the operator, and control measures* far more clearly than a hand-drawn stick figure. This is the standard approach recommended for KTU board answer sheets when complex physical drawings are required.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Pushing" in the context of industrial manual material handling. State any two biomechanical risks associated with it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pushing is a manual material handling activity in which a worker applies a horizontal force to an object so that the object moves **away** from the body. *(1 mark)*

Biomechanical risks: *(2 marks — 1 each)*
1. **Lumbar spine compression** that may exceed the NIOSH Action Limit of 3400 N, leading to lower-back disorders.
2. **Cumulative trauma to wrists and shoulders**, particularly the rotator cuff and carpal tunnel, due to repetitive push cycles and non-neutral wrist postures.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between initial pushing force and sustained pushing force.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Initial Pushing Force** is the peak force required to overcome *static friction* and accelerate the load from rest. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Sustained Pushing Force** is the lower force required to keep the load moving at a constant velocity, overcoming only *kinetic friction*. *(1.5 marks)*

Numerical values typically follow **F_initial > F_sustained**, e.g., 230 N vs 140 N under NIOSH recommendations.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*Explain the biomechanics of a pushing task with a neat free-body diagram. Derive the relationship between pushing force and spinal compression.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define pushing biomechanically — the worker exerts a horizontal force $F_{push}$ at a handle height while the upper body leans slightly forward, generating trunk flexion angle $\theta$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Free-Body Diagram — Show the upper body weight $W_{UB}$ acting at its center of mass, the push force $F_{push}$ horizontal at the shoulders, and the spinal reaction $F_{spine}$ along the lumbar axis (L5/S1 disc).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Resolve the horizontal push force into its component along the spinal axis using the trunk angle:

$$
F_{push, axial} = F_{push} \cdot \tan(\theta)
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Combine with upper-body weight:

$$
F_{spine} = F_{push} \cdot \tan(\theta) + W_{UB}
$$

**Conclusion:** Compare with the NIOSH Action Limit of **3400 N**; if exceeded, the task is unsafe.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
*A worker pushes a 200 kg trolley across a flat floor. The static and kinetic coefficients of friction are 0.45 and 0.32 respectively. If the trolley is accelerated to 0.4 m/s in 1.5 m, determine the initial and sustained pushing forces. Comment on the safety of the task using NIOSH limits.*

**Given:** $M = 200 \text{ kg}$, $\mu_s = 0.45$, $\mu_k = 0.32$, $v = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$, $s = 1.5 \text{ m}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute acceleration.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v^2 &= 2 a s \\
(0.4)^2 &= 2 a (1.5) \\
0.16 &= 3 a \\
a &= 0.0533 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute normal reaction.

$$
N = M g = (200)(9.81) = 1962 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute initial force.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{initial} &= M a + \mu_s N \\
F_{initial} &= (200)(0.0533) + (0.45)(1962) \\
F_{initial} &= 10.66 + 882.9 \\
F_{initial} &\approx 893.6 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute sustained force.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{sustained} &= \mu_k N \\
F_{sustained} &= (0.32)(1962) \\
F_{sustained} &\approx 627.84 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Safety Comment.

$$
\frac{F_{initial}}{F_{NIOSH,ini}} = \frac{893.6}{230} \approx 3.88
$$

The required initial force is **~3.9× the safe NIOSH limit**, and sustained force is **~4.5× the safe limit**. The task is **unsafe for a single worker**; engineering controls (powered tugs, larger wheels, or floor lubrication) are required.

---

### ⭐ Question B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
*List and explain any six ergonomic control measures to reduce the hazards of pushing tasks in an industrial environment.*

**Model Answer (6 points × ~1.15 marks each = 7 marks):**

1. **Use of powered equipment** — Electric pallet trucks, motorized tugs eliminate the manual force requirement entirely.
2. **Wheel diameter and material selection** — Larger, softer rubber wheels reduce rolling resistance.
3. **Floor surface maintenance** — Smooth, level, dry floors reduce $\mu$ and hence $F_{sustained}$.
4. **Adjustable-height handles** — Maintain handle height at 900–1100 mm to keep trunk flexion minimal.
5. **Load weight reduction** — Subdivide heavy loads into smaller batches.
6. **Training and job rotation** — Teach correct posture and reduce cumulative exposure.
7. **Push frequency reduction** — Allow micro-breaks every 30 minutes for repetitive pushing.
8. **PPE** — Vibration-dampening gloves, supportive footwear, and back belts (as a last resort).

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
*A 300 kg crate on a wheeled dolly is pushed across a warehouse. The push distance is 8 m, sustained pushing force is 600 N, and the operator does this 12 times per hour. Calculate:*
*(i) The work done per push*
*(ii) The power output during sustained motion*
*(iii) The total energy expended per hour*

**Given:** $F_{sustained} = 600 \text{ N}$, $d = 8 \text{ m}$, $v = 0.6 \text{ m/s}$, $n = 12$ pushes/hr.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Work per push.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{push} &= F_{sustained} \cdot d \\
W_{push} &= (600)(8) \\
W_{push} &= 4800 \text{ J} = 4.8 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Power.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= F_{sustained} \cdot v \\
P &= (600)(0.6) \\
P &= 360 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Hourly energy.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{hour} &= n \cdot W_{push} \\
W_{hour} &= (12)(4800) \\
W_{hour} &= 57{,}600 \text{ J} = 57.6 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclusion. This equals approximately 57.6 kJ/hr ≈ 13.8 kcal/hr of mechanical work. Compare with recommended sustained work rate of < 8 kcal/hr for 8-hour shifts — **the operator is being overexerted**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Pushing Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "mg" term** when computing normal force — many students compute $F = \mu M$ instead of $F = \mu M g$, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using the same coefficient for static and kinetic friction** — examiners specifically test the distinction; if both are given, use both correctly.
> 3. **Not converting units** — NIOSH limits are in Newtons (N), but some texts quote them in kgf. Always convert: 1 kgf ≈ 9.81 N.
> 4. **Ignoring trunk flexion** in spinal compression calculations — the $\tan(\theta)$ term is what differentiates a "pushing" question from a generic friction problem.
> 5. **Omitting the "comment" line** — in 7-mark numerical sub-parts, the final interpretive sentence comparing your answer with the NIOSH limit is **mandatory** for full marks.
> 6. **Mis-spelling "lumbar"** — examiners deduct marks for spelling errors in technical terminology.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pushing**
>
> - **Pushing** = horizontal force application that moves a load **away** from the body.
> - **Initial Force** > **Sustained Force** because $\mu_s > \mu_k$.
> - **NIOSH Safe Limits:** $F_{ini} \leq 230$ N, $F_{sus} \leq 140$ N.
> - **Spinal Action Limit:** $F_{spine} \leq 3400$ N at L5/S1.
> - **Key formulas:** $F_{friction} = \mu N$, $F_{initial} = \mu_s N + ma$, $F_{sustained} = \mu_k N$, $F_{spine} = F_{push}\tan\theta + W_{UB}$.
> - **Ergonomic handle height:** 900–1100 mm.
> - **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
> - **Always convert** weights in kg to forces in N using $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
> - **Always conclude** numerical answers with a safety comparison against NIOSH benchmarks.
> - **Real-world applications:** warehouse trolleys, hospital beds, wheelbarrows, pallet jacks, automotive skids, AGV-vs-manual decision.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
