# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Trapezoidal Hook Design (Full KTU Board-Style Solution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a fully solved 14-mark problem in the exact KTU valuation format. Study the logical flow — not just the math.**

### Problem Statement (Typical KTU Pattern)

> *Design a forged steel lifting hook to carry a maximum load of **50 kN**. The hook is to be made of **EN-19 steel** (UTS = 700 MPa, $\sigma_y$ = 480 MPa) with a **factor of safety of 4.0**. The cross-section is **trapezoidal**, with $b_1 = 60$ mm (inner width), $b_2 = 100$ mm (outer width), and depth $h = 50$ mm. The inner radius of the hook is $r_i = 80$ mm. Verify the design using the Winkler–Bach theory.*

### Step 1 — List the Given Data and Convert Units

$$P = 50 \text{ kN} = 50{,}000 \text{ N}$$

$$b_1 = 60 \text{ mm}, \quad b_2 = 100 \text{ mm}, \quad h = 50 \text{ mm}$$

$$r_i = 80 \text{ mm}, \quad r_o = r_i + h = 80 + 50 = 130 \text{ mm}$$

$$UTS = 700 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_y = 480 \text{ MPa}, \quad FOS = 4.0$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Centroidal Radius $R̄$

For a trapezoidal section, the centroid lies at a distance:

$$R̄ = r_i + \dfrac{h}{3} \cdot \dfrac{b_1 + 2b_2}{b_1 + b_2}$$

This is the standard centroid-of-trapezoid formula measured from the inner fiber. Substituting:

$$R̄ = 80 + \dfrac{50}{3} \cdot \dfrac{60 + 2(100)}{60 + 100}$$

$$R̄ = 80 + \dfrac{50}{3} \cdot \dfrac{260}{160}$$

$$R̄ = 80 + \dfrac{50}{3} \cdot 1.625$$

$$R̄ = 80 + 27.083$$

$$\boxed{R̄ = 107.083 \text{ mm}}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Cross-Sectional Area $A$

For a trapezoid:

$$A = \dfrac{(b_1 + b_2) \cdot h}{2}$$

$$A = \dfrac{(60 + 100) \cdot 50}{2}$$

$$A = \dfrac{160 \cdot 50}{2} = 4000 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Eccentricity $e$ (Trapezoidal Section)

The eccentricity for a trapezoidal section is:

$$e = R̄ - \dfrac{r_o - r_i}{\ln(r_o/r_i)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - (b_1/b_2)}$$

> **Derivation logic:** For a curved trapezoid, the neutral-axis radius $r_n$ is found by integrating $(1/r)$ across the section, and then $e = R̄ - r_n$. The closed-form result for a trapezoid is the above.

Substitute the values:

$$\ln(r_o / r_i) = \ln(130 / 80) = \ln(1.625) = 0.4855$$

$$\dfrac{r_o - r_i}{\ln(r_o/r_i)} = \dfrac{130 - 80}{0.4855} = \dfrac{50}{0.4855} = 102.98 \text{ mm}$$

$$1 - \dfrac{b_1}{b_2} = 1 - \dfrac{60}{100} = 0.4$$

$$e = 107.083 - \dfrac{102.98}{0.4} = 107.083 - 257.45$$

$$\boxed{e = -150.37 \text{ mm}}$$

> **Sign convention note for examiner:** A **negative** $e$ means the neutral axis lies **outside** the centroidal axis on the **outer side** of the hook curve. This is normal for a trapezoidal hook where the outer fiber is wider.

### Step 5 — Compute the Bending Moment $M$ at the Critical Section

For a hook, the critical section is the straight shank just above the bowl. The moment arm is the distance from the load line (passing through the saddle) to this section. For a first design estimate, KTU convention uses:

$$h_{load} = r_i + h = r_o = 130 \text{ mm}$$

(For design conservatism, the moment arm is taken as the full outer radius because the load can swing toward the shank.)

$$M = P \cdot h_{load} = 50{,}000 \cdot 130 = 6.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Direct Tensile Stress $\sigma_d$

$$\sigma_d = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{4000} = 12.5 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 7 — Compute the Bending Stress at the Inner Fiber (Critical)

The Winkler–Bach formula:

$$\sigma_{b,inner} = \dfrac{M \cdot (R̄ - r_i)}{A \cdot e \cdot r_i}$$

Substitute carefully, noting the **negative** value of $e$:

$$\sigma_{b,inner} = \dfrac{6.5 \times 10^6 \cdot (107.083 - 80)}{4000 \cdot (-150.37) \cdot 80}$$

$$\sigma_{b,inner} = \dfrac{6.5 \times 10^6 \cdot 27.083}{-48{,}118{,}400}$$

$$\sigma_{b,inner} = \dfrac{1.7604 \times 10^8}{-4.81184 \times 10^7}$$

$$\sigma_{b,inner} = -3.659 \text{ MPa (compressive from bending)}$$

> **Interpretation:** At the inner fiber, the bending component is **compressive** because the neutral axis is on the outer side. This counteracts the direct tension, which is the engineering reason trapezoidal hooks are safe — the bending actually reduces the tensile stress at the dangerous inner fiber.

### Step 8 — Compute the Bending Stress at the Outer Fiber

$$\sigma_{b,outer} = -\dfrac{M \cdot (r_o - R̄)}{A \cdot e \cdot r_o}$$

$$\sigma_{b,outer} = -\dfrac{6.5 \times 10^6 \cdot (130 - 107.083)}{4000 \cdot (-150.37) \cdot 130}$$

$$\sigma_{b,outer} = -\dfrac{6.5 \times 10^6 \cdot 22.917}{-78{,}192{,}400}$$

$$\sigma_{b,outer} = -\dfrac{1.4896 \times 10^8}{-7.8192 \times 10^7}$$

$$\sigma_{b,outer} = +1.905 \text{ MPa (tensile from bending)}$$

### Step 9 — Superimpose Direct and Bending Stresses

**At inner fiber:**

$$\sigma_{inner} = \sigma_d + \sigma_{b,inner} = 12.5 + (-3.659) = 8.841 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

**At outer fiber:**

$$\sigma_{outer} = \sigma_d + \sigma_{b,outer} = 12.5 + 1.905 = 14.405 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

> **Important result:** The **maximum tensile stress** in this hook is **at the outer fiber**, not the inner. This is unique to trapezoidal sections and demonstrates the wisdom of the trapezoidal geometry.

### Step 10 — Verify Against the Allowable Stress

$$\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{FOS} = \dfrac{480}{4.0} = 120 \text{ MPa}$$

Comparison:

$$\sigma_{max} = 14.405 \text{ MPa} \;\ll\; 120 \text{ MPa} = \sigma_{allow}$$

> **Conclusion:** The hook is **over-designed** (safe with a large margin). The cross-section can be reduced to optimize material, or the load can be increased up to:

$$P_{max} = 50 \text{ kN} \times \dfrac{120}{14.405} \approx 416 \text{ kN}$$

> **However**, in practice, the **WLL (Working Load Limit) of 50 kN** is retained for safety, manufacturing standardization, and to allow for dynamic shock loading not modeled in this static analysis.

### Step 11 — Throat Opening Check (Inspection Criterion)

The throat opening stretch under load must remain below 5% of the original throat dimension. The throat opening $T$ is typically $1.5 \times r_i$ for a single hook:

$$T_{original} = 1.5 \times 80 = 120 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta T_{max} = 0.05 \times 120 = 6 \text{ mm}$$

This is verified during the **Proof Load Test** at the factory. If $\Delta T > 6$ mm, the hook is **rejected and discarded**.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison: Trapezoidal vs. Rectangular Cross-Section

> [!NOTE]
> **This comparison is a frequent 7-mark sub-question in KTU Module-4 exams.**

| Parameter | Rectangular Section | Trapezoidal Section |
|---|---|---|
| Material distribution | Uniform across depth | More material on the inner side (high-stress side) |
| Inner fiber stress | High | Reduced by design |
| Manufacturing | Easy (machined plate) | Requires forging / casting |
| Weight for same SWL | Higher | Lower (up to 30% lighter) |
| Failure mode if overloaded | Sudden, brittle | Gradual, with visible throat opening |
| Standard application | Light duty | Heavy industrial / crane service |
| Governing formula | $\sigma_i = \dfrac{P}{A} + \dfrac{M(R̄ - r_i)}{Aer_i}$ | Same, but $e$ computed differently |
| Preferred FOS range | 4.0 – 5.0 | 3.5 – 4.0 (more efficient geometry) |

## 3.3 Numerical Validation Table (Full 7-Mark Answer Template)

| Step | Quantity | Computed Value | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centroidal radius $R̄$ | 107.083 mm | Centroid of trapezoid from inner edge |
| 2 | Cross-section area $A$ | 4000 mm² | Trapezoid area formula |
| 3 | Neutral-axis offset $e$ | -150.37 mm | Negative ⇒ NA on outer side |
| 4 | Bending moment $M$ | 6.5 × 10⁶ N·mm | Static moment of 50 kN at 130 mm |
| 5 | Direct stress $\sigma_d$ | 12.5 MPa | $P/A$ |
| 6 | Bending stress at $r_i$ | -3.659 MPa | Counteracts tension |
| 7 | Bending stress at $r_o$ | +1.905 MPa | Adds to tension |
| 8 | Total $\sigma_{inner}$ | 8.84 MPa | Safe |
| 9 | Total $\sigma_{outer}$ | 14.41 MPa | **Critical stress** |
| 10 | Allowable $\sigma$ | 120 MPa | $\sigma_y / FOS$ |
| 11 | Conclusion | **Design is SAFE** | 14.41 << 120 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-Loss Alert (Examiner Pattern):** Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** for:
> - Forgetting the **dynamic load factor** (the examiner may add "sudden snatch lift" as a sub-condition, multiplying the load by 1.5×).
> - Using a **straight-beam** formula by mistake.
> - Failing to **comment on the sign of $e$** and what it means physically.
> - Not computing the **throat opening stretch** check.

## 3.4 Sample Numerical: Including Dynamic Load Factor

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic load factor $K_d$ is now a mandatory KTU 2024 requirement. Always apply it.**

For a **snatch lift** (sudden engagement of the load), $K_d = 2.0$:

$$P_{design} = P \times K_d = 50 \times 2.0 = 100 \text{ kN}$$

Re-running the entire calculation with $P = 100$ kN doubles the stresses:

$$\sigma_{max, dynamic} = 2 \times 14.41 = 28.82 \text{ MPa} \ll 120 \text{ MPa} \;\;\; \text{(Still Safe)}$$

> **For a Class IV heavy-service hook** (ladle crane, $K_d = 2.0$, FOS = 3.5):

$$\sigma_{allow} = 480 / 3.5 = 137.14 \text{ MPa} \;\;\; \text{(Even more permissive)}$$

This is why the **selection of FOS is driven by the duty classification** (Section 1.5 above).


> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Visual Mapping:** When a question says *"show the stress distribution diagram for a curved hook"*, the examiner expects a sketch where the **neutral axis is shown shifted toward the center of curvature** and the **inner-fiber stress block is wider than the outer-fiber stress block**. The above Mermaid block is a textual proxy; in your exam script, draw the classic **trapezoidal stress block** with $r_n$ marked to the right of $R̄$.

## 4.6 Diagram 6: Hook Failure Modes (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hook Failure Modes] --> B1[Hook Opening Overload]
    A --> B2[Bending Deformation]
    A --> B3[Throat Widening]
    A --> B4[Hook Tip Wear]
    A --> B5[Surface Cracking Fatigue]
    A --> B6[Corrosion Pitting]
    A --> B7[Saddle Wear Saddle Seat Erosion]

    B1 --> C1[Sudden Catastrophic Drop]
    B2 --> C2[Visible Bend Discard]
    B3 --> C3[Slippage of Sling Discard]
    B4 --> C4[Slings Can Slip Off]
    B5 --> C5[Fatigue Crack Growth]
    B6 --> C6[Stress Concentration Sites]
    B7 --> C7[Load Instability Tilting]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** All hooks must be inspected **at least once every 6 months** (or every 3 months in heavy service) and a record maintained in the **Hook Register** as per IS 13367. Visual inspection, dimensional check (throat, tip, wear), and NDT (Magnetic Particle or Dye Penetrant) are all required.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a "Hook" in the context of lifting appliances. List the three primary standards that govern its design and testing.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> A hook is a **curved, load-bearing end-fitting** used in cranes, hoists, chain blocks, and slings to suspend a load. It is forged or cast from alloy steel and is designed using **curved-beam (Winkler–Bach) stress theory** because of the eccentric application of load.
> 
> The three primary standards governing hook design, testing, and inspection are:
> 1. **IS 3815** – Specification for hooks used with lifting appliances.
> 2. **ASME B30.10** – Hooks (international design and use standard).
> 3. **BS 2902** – Specification for lifting hooks (used as reference for high-capacity designs).
> 
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming standards, 1 mark for explaining the design basis.)*

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Why is a straight-beam bending formula $My/I$ not applicable to a lifting hook? What is the correct theory, and what is the maximum tensile stress location?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> The straight-beam formula $My/I$ assumes a **linear stress distribution** and a **neutral axis passing through the centroid**. In a hook, the **radius of curvature is less than 5 times the depth** of the section, causing the **neutral axis to shift toward the center of curvature** (away from the centroid). The stress distribution becomes **non-linear (hyperbolic)**, and the inner fiber carries a stress up to **3–4× higher** than the straight-beam prediction.
> 
> The correct theory is the **Winkler–Bach curved-beam theory**:
> 
> $$\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A} + \dfrac{M(R̄ - r)}{A \cdot e \cdot r}$$
> 
> In a **trapezoidal** hook, the **maximum tensile stress** occurs at the **outer (convex) fiber**, not the inner fiber, because the bending component at the inner fiber is compressive and counteracts the direct tension. This is why trapezoidal geometry is preferred.
> 
> *(1 mark for explaining why, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the critical-fiber location.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, KTU Internal Choice)

---

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Design a forged steel hook to lift a load of 80 kN. The hook material is EN-24 (UTS = 800 MPa, Yield = 600 MPa). The cross-section is trapezoidal with $b_1 = 70$ mm (inner width), $b_2 = 110$ mm (outer width), and depth $h = 55$ mm. The inner radius of the hook is $r_i = 90$ mm. Adopt a factor of safety of 4.0. Verify the design using the Winkler–Bach theory and check the throat opening stretch. State clearly your assumption for the moment arm.**

#### **Part (a) — Hook Stress Analysis (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**

$$P = 80 \text{ kN} = 80{,}000 \text{ N}, \quad b_1 = 70 \text{ mm}, \quad b_2 = 110 \text{ mm}$$

$$h = 55 \text{ mm}, \quad r_i = 90 \text{ mm}, \quad r_o = 90 + 55 = 145 \text{ mm}$$

$$\sigma_y = 600 \text{ MPa}, \quad FOS = 4.0$$

**Step 2 — Centroidal Radius:**

$$R̄ = r_i + \dfrac{h}{3} \cdot \dfrac{b_1 + 2b_2}{b_1 + b_2}$$

$$R̄ = 90 + \dfrac{55}{3} \cdot \dfrac{70 + 220}{70 + 110}$$

$$R̄ = 90 + \dfrac{55}{3} \cdot \dfrac{290}{180}$$

$$R̄ = 90 + \dfrac{55}{3} \cdot 1.6111$$

$$R̄ = 90 + 29.537$$

$$R̄ = 119.537 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-Sectional Area:**

$$A = \dfrac{(b_1 + b_2) \cdot h}{2} = \dfrac{(70 + 110) \cdot 55}{2} = 4950 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Eccentricity (Trapezoidal):**

$$\ln(r_o/r_i) = \ln(145/90) = \ln(1.6111) = 0.4773$$

$$\dfrac{r_o - r_i}{\ln(r_o/r_i)} = \dfrac{55}{0.4773} = 115.24 \text{ mm}$$

$$1 - \dfrac{b_1}{b_2} = 1 - \dfrac{70}{110} = 0.3636$$

$$e = 119.537 - \dfrac{115.24}{0.3636} = 119.537 - 316.94 = -197.40 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Moment Arm Assumption & Bending Moment:**

> *Assumption:* The moment arm is taken as the full outer radius $h_{load} = r_o = 145$ mm to account for the worst-case load swing toward the shank.

$$M = P \cdot h_{load} = 80{,}000 \cdot 145 = 11.6 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 6 — Stresses at Inner and Outer Fibers:**

Direct stress:

$$\sigma_d = \dfrac{80{,}000}{4950} = 16.16 \text{ MPa}$$

Bending stress at inner fiber:

$$\sigma_{b,inner} = \dfrac{11.6 \times 10^6 \cdot (119.537 - 90)}{4950 \cdot (-197.40) \cdot 90}$$

$$\sigma_{b,inner} = \dfrac{11.6 \times 10^6 \cdot 29.537}{-87.92 \times 10^6} = -3.898 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

Total inner stress:

$$\sigma_{inner} = 16.16 - 3.898 = 12.26 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

Bending stress at outer fiber:

$$\sigma_{b,outer} = -\dfrac{11.6 \times 10^6 \cdot (145 - 119.537)}{4950 \cdot (-197.40) \cdot 145}$$

$$\sigma_{b,outer} = -\dfrac{11.6 \times 10^6 \cdot 25.463}{-141.79 \times 10^6} = +2.083 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

Total outer stress:

$$\sigma_{outer} = 16.16 + 2.083 = 18.24 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

**Step 7 — Allowable Stress and Conclusion:**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{600}{4.0} = 150 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = 18.24 \text{ MPa} \ll 150 \text{ MPa} \quad \therefore \text{Design is SAFE}$$

#### **Part (b) — Throat Opening and Inspection (7 marks)**

**Step 8 — Throat Opening Calculation:**

For a single hook, the throat opening is approximately:

$$T_{original} = 1.5 \cdot r_i = 1.5 \cdot 90 = 135 \text{ mm}$$

The 5% discard limit:

$$\Delta T_{max} = 0.05 \cdot 135 = 6.75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 9 — Hook Tip Wear Limit:**

Forged hook tips are typically 1.5× the section depth. The wear limit is **10% of the original tip length** before mandatory rejection. If the tip is worn below this, the hook can allow the sling to slip off.

**Step 10 — Inspection Schedule (IS 13367):**

| Item | Frequency |
|---|---|
| Visual inspection during use | Every shift |
| Detailed dimensional inspection | Every 3 months (heavy service) / 6 months (normal) |
| NDT (MPT/DPI) | Every 12 months |
| Proof load test (1.25 × WLL) | Every 48 months or after any incident |

**Step 11 — Discard Criteria Summary:**

A hook MUST be **discarded and destroyed** if any one of the following is true:
- Throat opening stretched by more than **5%**.
- Hook tip worn by more than **10%** of original length.
- Any visible **crack, nick, or gouge** deeper than 0.5 mm.
- Saddle wear creating a tilt of more than **10°** under no-load.
- Any **bend or twist** exceeding 10° from the original plane.
- **Permanent deformation** of more than 0.2% under proof load.
- **Missing or illegible** identification stamp.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part A):**
> 1. **Forgetting the moment arm assumption** costs **1 mark**. Always state: *"The moment arm is taken as the outer radius of the hook to account for worst-case load swing."*
> 2. **Not converting kN to N** when computing stress in MPa costs **1 mark**. Watch units: $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2$.
> 3. **Forgetting the negative sign of $e$** and dropping the sign in the formula gives the wrong bending-stress sign. The examiner checks the sign of $\sigma_{b,inner}$ explicitly — a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Skipping the throat opening check** loses **1.5 marks**, as it is a mandatory 2024-scheme requirement.

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Explain in detail the Winkler–Bach theory of curved beams as applied to the design of a lifting hook. Using this theory, derive expressions for the stresses at the inner and outer fibers of a trapezoidal hook. Compare the stresses for a rectangular and trapezoidal cross-section of equivalent load capacity, and discuss why the trapezoidal section is preferred in heavy-duty industrial hooks.**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation of Winkler–Bach Equations (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Why Curved-Beam Theory:**

In a straight beam, the neutral axis passes through the centroid, and the stress distribution is **linear**. In a curved beam (such as a hook), the **neutral axis shifts toward the center of curvature** and the stress distribution becomes **hyperbolic (non-linear)**. This shift is significant when $R̄/h < 5$, which is always the case for a hook.

**Step 2 — Bending of a Curved Beam — Mathematical Setup:**

Consider a small element of the curved beam subtending an angle $d\theta$ at the center of curvature $O$. Let:
- $r$ = radius of the fiber under consideration.
- $r_n$ = radius of the neutral axis.
- $R̄$ = radius of the centroidal axis.
- $A$ = cross-sectional area.
- $M$ = applied bending moment (positive when it tends to **decrease** the radius of curvature — i.e., to "open" the hook).

The strain at radius $r$ is:

$$\epsilon = \dfrac{\Delta(r \cdot d\theta)}{r \cdot d\theta} = \dfrac{r \cdot d\theta' - r_n \cdot d\theta}{r_n \cdot d\theta}$$

For small deformations, $d\theta' = d\theta - \dfrac{M \cdot d\theta}{EI \cdot r_n^2} \cdot r$ (a relation derived from the curvature change). Simplifying:

$$\epsilon = \dfrac{(r - r_n)}{r_n}$$

**Step 3 — Stress at a Fiber:**

By Hooke's Law, $\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \dfrac{r - r_n}{r_n}$$

**Step 4 — Force Equilibrium — Determining $r_n$:**

The net axial force on the cross-section must be zero (no external axial load from bending alone):

$$\int_A \sigma \, dA = 0$$

$$\int_A E \cdot \dfrac{r - r_n}{r_n} \, dA = 0$$

$$\int_A (r - r_n) \, dA = 0$$

$$\int_A r \, dA = r_n \cdot A$$

$$r_n = \dfrac{A}{\int_A \dfrac{dA}{r}}$$

This is the **fundamental definition of the neutral-axis radius** for a curved beam.

**Step 5 — Moment Equilibrium — The Winkler–Bach Formula:**

Taking moments about the neutral axis:

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot (r - r_n) \, dA = \int_A \dfrac{E(r - r_n)^2}{r_n} \, dA$$

Solving for the stress:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot (r - r_n)}{A \cdot e \cdot r}$$

where $e = R̄ - r_n$ is the **eccentricity**, and $R̄ = \dfrac{1}{A} \int_A r \, dA$ is the **centroidal radius**.

Rewriting in terms of the inner/outer fibers:

$$\sigma_i = \dfrac{M \cdot (R̄ - r_i)}{A \cdot e \cdot r_i} \quad \text{(inner fiber stress)}$$

$$\sigma_o = \dfrac{M \cdot (r_o - R̄)}{A \cdot e \cdot r_o} \quad \text{(outer fiber stress)}$$

**Step 6 — Adding the Direct Stress:**

For a hook, the load $P$ also produces a uniform direct tension $P/A$:

$$\sigma_{inner,total} = \dfrac{P}{A} + \dfrac{M(R̄ - r_i)}{A \cdot e \cdot r_i}$$

$$\sigma_{outer,total} = \dfrac{P}{A} + \dfrac{M(r_o - R̄)}{A \cdot e \cdot r_o}$$

> **This is the final design equation for a hook.** *(Examiner's note: presenting both derivation and final formula in your answer script scores full marks.)*

#### **Part (b) — Comparison of Trapezoidal vs. Rectangular Sections (7 marks)**

| Comparison Parameter | Rectangular Hook | Trapezoidal Hook | Preference Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cross-section shape | Constant width $b$ | Inner width $b_1 < b_2$ outer | Trapezoid matches stress profile |
| Stress at inner fiber (tensile) | High | Lower (bending is compressive) | Trapezoid safer |
| Stress at outer fiber (compressive) | Low | Slightly higher but well-distributed | Trapezoid still safer overall |
| Material efficiency | Wasted material at outer fiber | Material concentrated near inner fiber | Trapezoid lighter (20–30%) |
| Manufacturing cost | Low (machined) | High (forged) | Rectangle for low-budget use |
| Fatigue life | Lower (sharp stress gradient) | Higher (smooth stress gradient) | Trapezoid for fatigue-critical |
| Failure mode (overload) | Sudden hook opening | Gradual throat opening with warning | Trapezoid preferred for safety |
| Typical capacity range | Up to 25 tonnes | Up to 250 tonnes and beyond | Trapezoid for heavy duty |
| Inspection visibility of failure | Poor (cracks may be hidden) | Good (throat opening visible) | Trapezoid preferred |
| Standards preferred | IS 3815 (light) | IS 8610, ASME B30.10 (heavy) | Trapezoid for industrial |

**Numerical Comparison for the Same Load (80 kN):**

| Parameter | Rectangular ($b = 90$ mm) | Trapezoidal (b₁=70, b₂=110 mm) |
|---|---|---|
| Cross-section area | 90 × 55 = 4950 mm² | (70+110)/2 × 55 = 4950 mm² |
| $\sigma_{inner}$ (Winkler–Bach) | ~22 MPa | 12.26 MPa |
| $\sigma_{outer}$ (Winkler–Bach) | ~5 MPa | 18.24 MPa |
| Max stress | 22 MPa (inner) | 18.24 MPa (outer) |
| Mass of hook (approx.) | 100% | 70–80% |
| Verdict | Higher max stress, heavier | **Preferred** |

**Discussion — Why Trapezoidal Section is Preferred in Heavy-Duty Hooks:**

1. **Material economy:** The trapezoidal cross-section places more material near the **inner fiber** where the straight-beam intuition says stress is highest. In a curved beam, the inner fiber actually has compressive bending — so the extra material is not wasted; it provides a **reserve of safety against off-center loading**.

2. **Smooth stress gradient:** The stress varies hyperbolically across the depth. Trapezoidal geometry approximates this hyperbolic profile, giving a **more uniform utilization** of material.

3. **Visible failure mode:** When overloaded, a trapezoidal hook **opens at the throat** gradually. This gives the operator time to detect and abandon the lift — a critical safety advantage.

4. **Lower weight for the same SWL:** A 30% weight reduction at the hook translates to lower inertia in dynamic lifts, reducing shock loads and improving crane stability.

5. **Standardization with international codes:** ASME B30.10 and IS 8610 specify trapezoidal (or near-trapezoidal) cross-sections for all Class II and above hooks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B):**
> 1. **Writing the formula without the derivation** loses 3–4 marks. The Winkler–Bach derivation from $\int \sigma \, dA = 0$ is the **intellectual core** of this topic.
> 2. **Confusing $r$ and $R̄$** in the derivation costs 1 mark. $r$ is the radius of an arbitrary fiber; $R̄$ is specifically the centroidal radius.
> 3. **Skipping the sign convention** in the comparison table is a 1-mark deduction. Always state that the inner-fiber bending stress is **compressive** for a trapezoidal section.
> 4. **Not mentioning the discard criteria or inspection schedule** in the comparison loses 1 mark — KTU 2024 requires hazard-control context, not just mechanical design.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Block**

- [ ] **Hook definition:** Curved, forged/cast lifting end-fitting designed by **Winkler–Bach curved-beam theory**.

- [ ] **Why curved-beam theory?** Because $R̄/h < 5$ for any hook, the neutral axis shifts toward the center of curvature, producing a **hyperbolic (non-linear) stress distribution**.

- [ ] **Neutral axis radius:** $r_n = A / \int (dA/r)$; for a rectangle, $r_n = h / \ln(r_o/r_i)$.

- [ ] **Eccentricity:** $e = R̄ - r_n$. For a **rectangle**, $e$ is **positive**; for a **trapezoid** with $b_2 > b_1$, $e$ is **negative** (NA on the outer side).

- [ ] **Winkler–Bach stress at radius $r$:** $\sigma = P/A + M(R̄ - r)/(Aer)$.

- [ ] **Critical stress location:**
  - **Rectangular hook** → inner fiber (maximum tension).
  - **Trapezoidal hook** → outer fiber (maximum tension, but lower magnitude than rectangular case).

- [ ] **Factor of safety range:** 3.5 (Class III/IV heavy) to 5.0 (Class I light).

- [ ] **Dynamic load factor:** $K_d = 1.5$ (normal) to $2.0$ (snatch lift). **Always apply** in KTU 2024 problems.

- [ ] **Material:** EN-19, EN-24, AISI 4140, 34CrNiMo6. UTS 700–1000 MPa, $\sigma_y$ 480–750 MPa.

- [ ] **Throat opening discard limit:** **5% stretch** of original throat dimension. **Hard reject criterion.**

- [ ] **Hook tip wear limit:** **10% wear** of original tip length. **Hard reject criterion.**

- [ ] **Inspection frequencies (IS 13367):**
  - Visual — every shift.
  - Dimensional — 3 months (heavy) / 6 months (normal).
  - NDT (MPT/DPI) — annually.
  - Proof load test (1.25 × WLL) — every 4 years.

- [ ] **Failure modes (memorize the 7):** hook opening, bending, throat widening, tip wear, cracking, pitting, saddle wear.

- [ ] **Standards to cite:** IS 3815, IS 8610, IS 13367, ASME B30.10, BS 2902, Factories Act 1948 Sec 31/32.

- [ ] **Trapezoidal vs. Rectangular — which is better?** **Trapezoidal**, because it (a) lowers the maximum stress, (b) reduces weight, (c) gives a visible gradual failure, and (d) extends fatigue life.

- [ ] **Examiner's most-tested sub-questions:**
  1. Derive the Winkler–Bach stress formula (7 marks).
  2. Design a hook for a given load (7 marks).
  3. Compare rectangular and trapezoidal sections (5 marks).
  4. List discard criteria and inspection schedule (3–5 marks).

- [ ] **Sign of $e$ — memorize this:** For a hook loaded at the saddle, the **bending moment tries to open the hook**. This produces **tension on the inner side and compression on the outer side** of a *rectangular* section, but the relationship flips for a *trapezoidal* section because the wider outer face re-distributes the stress.

- [ ] **Units check before writing the final answer:** $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2$. Convert all forces to N and all lengths to mm.

- [ ] **Always state assumptions explicitly:** moment arm, load case (static/dynamic), FOS basis, material code. Examiners reward clear assumption statements with 0.5–1 mark per question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clamps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT601 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Clamps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of industrial hazard control, a **clamp** is defined as a mechanical fastening device designed to hold, secure, or immobilize components (such as pipes, hoses, cables, beams, or workpieces) firmly together or against a supporting structure, thereby preventing unintended movement, vibration-induced loosening, leakage, or structural failure that could lead to workplace hazards.

According to standard industrial safety and mechanical engineering references adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus, a clamp functions by applying a sustained **gripping force** (typically via screw threads, wedges, levers, or hydraulic actuation) around an object or between two surfaces. This force is converted into a normal contact pressure that generates frictional resistance sufficient to resist the applied service loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):**
> Clamps are studied under mechanical securing devices used in industrial safety engineering for the prevention of failure due to vibration, thermal expansion, pressure surges, and accidental displacement of pressurized or energized systems.

## 1.2 Key Engineering Characteristics of a Clamp

- **Material of construction:** Generally mild steel, stainless steel (SS 304, SS 316), brass, aluminium alloys, or engineering polymers (e.g., nylon, PEEK), depending on service environment.
- **Load-bearing capacity:** Ranging from a few Newtons (N) for hose clamps to several kiloNewtons (kN) for structural beam clamps.
- **Operating temperature range:** Typically **-40°C to +300°C** for metallic clamps; specialized cryogenic or high-temperature variants exist.
- **Standards governing clamp design:** ISO 2852, DIN 3017, ASME B31.3, ASTM F879.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are trying to fix a leaking garden hose onto a tap. You use a simple metal band and tighten a screw. That tiny device is a **clamp**. In a chemical plant, the same principle is scaled up — but instead of a garden hose, the clamp secures pipes carrying corrosive acids at **10 bar pressure** and **200°C** temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Think of a clamp as a "firm handshake" given to a component. A weak handshake (loose clamp) lets things slip and cause accidents; a strong, well-calibrated handshake (properly torqued clamp) ensures everything stays in place even under harsh industrial conditions like vibration, heat, or pressure surges.

## 1.4 Role of Clamps in Hazard Control

| Hazard Type | Role of Clamp |
|-------------|---------------|
| Leakage of fluids/gases | Seals hose/pipe joints |
| Vibration-induced loosening | Provides locking grip |
| Falling objects | Secures overhead fixtures |
| Electrical hazards | Holds cable trays and conduits |
| Fire hazards | Secures fire suppression piping |
| Mechanical failure | Distributes load on joints |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle of a Clamp

The working of a clamp is rooted in the principle of **friction** and **mechanical interference fit**. When a clamp is tightened, it produces a clamping force ($F_c$) that generates a normal contact pressure ($p$) on the surface of the component being held. This contact pressure, when multiplied by the coefficient of friction ($\mu$) between the clamp and the component, yields a **holding force** that resists slipping.

## 2.2 Classification of Clamps

Clamps used in industrial hazard control can be broadly classified into the following categories:

### A. Based on Application

1. **Hose Clamps** — Used to secure flexible hoses to rigid fittings.
2. **Pipe Clamps** — Used to support and restrain piping systems.
3. **Beam Clamps** — Used in structural steelwork to attach fixtures without drilling.
4. **Cable Clamps** — Used to organize and secure electrical cables.
5. **Work-Holding Clamps** — Used in machining to hold workpieces.
6. **Hose/Pipe Repair Clamps** — Used to seal leaks without shutting down systems.

### B. Based on Actuation Mechanism

1. **Screw-Actuated Clamps** (e.g., worm-drive hose clamps, T-bolt clamps)
2. **Spring-Loaded Clamps**
3. **Hydraulic Clamps**
4. **Pneumatic Clamps**
5. **Toggle Clamps**
6. **Cam-Actuated Clamps**
7. **Wedge-Type Clamps**

## 2.3 Detailed Description of Major Clamp Types

### 2.3.1 Worm-Drive Hose Clamp (DIN 3017 / ISO 2852)

This is the most common industrial hose clamp. It consists of a metal band with a screw thread (worm gear) that, when rotated, tightens or loosens the band around the hose.

**Components:**
- Band (stainless steel or zinc-plated steel)
- Housing
- Worm-gear screw
- Optional liner (to prevent hose damage)

**Torque range:** Typically **3 N·m to 8 N·m** depending on band width and diameter.

### 2.3.2 T-Bolt Clamp

Used for high-pressure applications (turbocharger hoses, industrial ducting, marine exhaust).

**Features:**
- Bolt with a T-shaped head that slides in the band.
- Higher torque capacity (up to **20 N·m**).
- Uniform pressure distribution around the hose circumference.

### 2.3.3 Pipe Clamp (Pipe Support Clamp)

Used to support vertical and horizontal piping in process industries. It transfers the pipe weight to structural supports.

**Types:**
- U-bolt clamp
- Riser clamp
- Clevis hanger
- Pipe shoe with clamp

### 2.3.4 Beam Clamp

Used to attach threaded rod, conduit, or cable tray to structural steel beams (I-beams, H-beams) without welding or drilling.

**Types:**
- Malleable iron beam clamp
- Steel beam clamp with safety pin
- Hammer-on beam clamp

### 2.3.5 Toggle Clamp

Used in work-holding applications (jigs and fixtures). Provides a quick-acting, positive-locking grip using a toggle linkage mechanism.

**Advantage:** Maintains holding force even if the actuating force is removed (over-center locking principle).

### 2.3.6 Hydraulic Clamp

Used in heavy industries (shipbuilding, structural steel fabrication). Hydraulic pressure is used to generate very high clamping forces (up to several hundred kN).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_{hold} = \mu \cdot F_c$ | Holding (friction) force equals coefficient of friction times clamping force | N |
| 2 | $F_c = \dfrac{T}{r_t \cdot \tan(\lambda + \phi)}$ | Clamping force from applied torque $T$ on a screw (square-thread approximation) | N |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{\tan(\lambda)}{\tan(\lambda + \phi)}$ | Mechanical efficiency of a power screw used in screw-driven clamps | dimensionless |
| 4 | $p = \dfrac{F_c}{A_{contact}}$ | Contact pressure on clamped surface | Pa (N/m²) |
| 5 | $\sigma_{bolt} = \dfrac{F_c}{A_{bolt}} = \dfrac{4 F_c}{\pi d^2}$ | Tensile stress in the bolt of a T-bolt or beam clamp | Pa |
| 6 | $T_{allow} = 0.5 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}$ | Allowable torque capacity of the clamp screw based on yield criterion | N·m |
| 7 | $F_{safety} = \dfrac{F_{ult}}{n}$ | Safe working load; $n$ is the factor of safety (typically **2 to 4** for industrial clamps) | N |
| 8 | $P_{int} \cdot D \leq \dfrac{2 \cdot F_c \cdot \mu}{\pi}$ | Hoop stress balance for pressurized hose secured by clamp | Pa·m |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{thermal} = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta\theta$ | Thermal expansion of clamped member affecting clamp preload | m |
| 10 | $S = \dfrac{F_{c,max}}{F_{c,applied}}$ | Safety factor formulation for clamp design | dimensionless |

Where:
- $T$ = applied torque on clamp screw
- $r_t$ = mean thread radius
- $\lambda$ = lead angle of screw thread
- $\phi$ = friction angle in threads ($\phi = \tan^{-1}\mu$)
- $\sigma_y$ = yield stress of clamp material
- $d$ = nominal bolt diameter
- $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion
- $L$ = length of clamped member
- $\Delta\theta$ = temperature change

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas Industry:** Clamps secure high-pressure flowlines and prevent hazardous hydrocarbon leaks.
- **Power Plants:** Pipe clamps support steam lines operating at **540°C / 170 bar** (super-critical conditions).
- **Automotive Manufacturing:** Hose clamps secure coolant, fuel, and turbocharger lines.
- **Shipbuilding:** Beam clamps support cable trays and piping in engine rooms.
- **Process Industries:** Clamps prevent vibration-induced loosening of flange bolts in pumps and compressors — a major source of LOPC (Loss of Primary Containment) incidents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** According to industry safety statistics, approximately **18% of mechanical failure incidents** in process plants are linked to inadequate fastening, including improper clamp selection, installation, or maintenance. This underscores the importance of clamp engineering in hazard control.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Clamping Force from Screw Torque (Square-Thread Model)

The screw mechanism in a worm-drive or T-bolt clamp is modeled as an inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder. The relationship between applied torque and the resulting axial clamping force is derived as follows.

**Given:**
- A square-thread screw with mean radius $r_t$
- Lead angle $\lambda$ such that $\tan(\lambda) = \dfrac{L}{\pi d_t}$ where $L$ is the lead and $d_t = 2 r_t$ is the mean thread diameter.
- Thread friction coefficient $\mu$ (so friction angle $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu$))

**Step 1 — Identify forces on the thread helix:**

When the screw is rotated to tighten the clamp, the normal reaction $N$ on the thread acts perpendicular to the thread surface. This force has two components:

- Horizontal (axial) component: $N \cos(\lambda)$ — contributes to clamping force.
- Vertical (tangential) component: $N \sin(\lambda)$ — opposes rotation.

**Step 2 — Apply equilibrium in the tangential direction:**

The applied torque must overcome both the thread's helix component and the friction at the threads.

$$
T = N \sin(\lambda) \cdot r_t + \mu \cdot N \cos(\lambda) \cdot r_t
$$

**Step 3 — Factor the equation:**

$$
T = N \cdot r_t \cdot \left[ \sin(\lambda) + \mu \cos(\lambda) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to friction angle form using $\mu = \tan(\phi)$:**

$$
T = N \cdot r_t \cdot \left[ \sin(\lambda) + \tan(\phi) \cos(\lambda) \right]
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\cos(\phi)$ to get the standard form:

$$
T = \dfrac{N \cdot r_t \cdot \cos(\phi)}{\cos(\phi)} \cdot \left[ \sin(\lambda) \cos(\phi) + \sin(\phi) \cos(\lambda) \right]
$$

Using the identity $\sin(A + B) = \sin(A)\cos(B) + \cos(A)\sin(B)$:

$$
T = \dfrac{N \cdot r_t \cdot \cos(\phi)}{\cos(\phi)} \cdot \sin(\lambda + \phi)
$$

Since the axial clamping force $F_c = N \cos(\lambda) - \mu N \sin(\lambda)$ (lifting the load), but for a power screw supporting a load, the relationship simplifies using the standard result:

**Step 5 — Final clamping force expression:**

$$
F_c = \dfrac{T}{r_t \cdot \tan(\lambda + \phi)}
$$

This is the **fundamental clamping-force-from-torque equation** for screw-actuated clamps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Because the denominator contains $\tan(\lambda + \phi)$, increasing the friction angle $\phi$ (i.e., using a rough or poorly lubricated thread) **dramatically reduces** the clamping force generated for a given input torque. This is why proper lubrication and surface finish are critical for clamp performance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hoop Stress Equilibrium for a Clamped Pressurized Hose

Consider a flexible hose of internal diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, secured at both ends by clamps. The internal pressure $P_{int}$ tends to push the hose off the fitting. The clamp must provide a frictional grip sufficient to prevent this.

**Step 1 — Force trying to push hose off fitting:**

The internal pressure acting on the projected area of the hose end gives the axial ejection force:

$$
F_{axial} = P_{int} \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Frictional grip from the clamp:**

The clamp provides a radial normal pressure $p_r$ on the hose circumference, which translates to a normal force per unit axial length as:

$$
dF_{normal} = p_r \cdot \pi D \cdot dx
$$

The frictional grip over the contact length $L_c$ is:

$$
F_{grip} = \mu \cdot p_r \cdot \pi D \cdot L_c
$$

**Step 3 — Equating for equilibrium (no slip condition):**

$$
\mu \cdot p_r \cdot \pi D \cdot L_c \geq P_{int} \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for the required contact pressure $p_r$:**

$$
p_r \geq \dfrac{P_{int} \cdot D}{4 \mu L_c}
$$

This gives the **minimum radial pressure** the clamp must apply to prevent hose blow-off.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** A short contact length $L_c$ (e.g., a poorly seated clamp) requires a much higher $p_r$, which can damage the hose. This is why proper clamp sizing and full axial engagement of the hose on the fitting is essential for hazard prevention.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Safety Factor for Clamp Design

**Given:**
- Ultimate tensile strength of clamp material: $F_{ult}$
- Maximum expected service load: $F_{service}$

**Step 1 — Define factor of safety:**

$$
n = \dfrac{F_{ult}}{F_{service}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply KTU 2024 industrial standards:**

For industrial clamps used in hazard-critical applications, $n$ is typically selected as:

| Application | Factor of Safety ($n$) |
|-------------|------------------------|
| General industrial hose | 2.0 |
| High-pressure hydraulic lines | 3.0 |
| Cryogenic / LNG service | 4.0 |
| Dynamic / vibrating load | 3.5 |

**Step 3 — Safe working load calculation:**

$$
F_{SWL} = \dfrac{F_{ult}}{n}
$$

For example, a stainless steel T-bolt clamp with $F_{ult} = 25$ kN used in a high-pressure hydraulic application ($n = 3$):

$$
F_{SWL} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{3} = 8333\ \text{N} \approx 8.33\ \text{kN}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting the Right Clamp

| Service Condition | Recommended Clamp | Rationale |
|-------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| Low-pressure water hose | Worm-drive hose clamp | Cost-effective, easy to install |
| High-pressure hydraulic line | T-bolt clamp | Higher torque, uniform pressure |
| Cryogenic LNG piping | Stainless steel pipe clamp (SS 316) | Low-temperature toughness, corrosion resistance |
| Vibrating machinery | Spring-lock washer + bolt clamp | Prevents self-loosening |
| Heavy structural load | Beam clamp with safety pin | High load capacity, no drilling required |
| Quick workpiece clamping in machining | Toggle clamp | Rapid actuation, positive locking |
| Heavy steel plate fabrication | Hydraulic clamp | Very high clamping force |
| Electrical cable management | Nylon cable clamp | Insulation, lightweight, non-corrosive |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define a clamp and list any four types of clamps used in industrial hazard control.**

**Model Answer:**
A clamp is a mechanical fastening device that applies a sustained gripping force to hold components together or to a supporting structure, preventing movement, leakage, or failure.

**Four types of clamps:**
1. Worm-drive hose clamp
2. T-bolt clamp
3. Pipe support clamp (U-bolt / riser clamp)
4. Beam clamp
5. Toggle clamp *(any four)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State two safety functions of clamps in a process plant.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Prevention of leakage:** Clamps secure hose/pipe joints, preventing the release of hazardous fluids or gases.
2. **Vibration control:** Clamps prevent loosening of fasteners in vibrating equipment (pumps, compressors), thereby reducing the risk of mechanical failure.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the working principle of a **worm-drive hose clamp** and a **T-bolt clamp**. Compare their suitability for low-pressure and high-pressure applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A T-bolt clamp is used to secure a hydraulic hose of internal diameter $D = 50$ mm carrying fluid at pressure $P_{int} = 80$ bar. The contact length between clamp and hose is $L_c = 25$ mm and the coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.35$. Calculate the **minimum radial contact pressure** required to prevent hose blow-off. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Working Principles:**

**Worm-Drive Hose Clamp:**
- A metal band encircles the hose, with a worm-gear screw housed in a metal casing.
- Rotating the screw shortens the effective circumference of the band, tightening it around the hose.
- The generated radial pressure grips the hose onto the fitting.
- **Suitable for:** Low to medium pressure (up to ~5 bar), general water and air lines.
- **Limitation:** Non-uniform pressure distribution, limited torque capacity.

**T-Bolt Clamp:**
- A bolt with a T-shaped head slides in slots on the band.
- Tightening the bolt (via nut) applies a uniform radial pressure.
- **Suitable for:** High-pressure applications (up to 30+ bar), turbocharger hoses, marine exhaust, industrial ducting.
- **Advantage:** Higher torque capacity (up to 20 N·m), uniform pressure, reliable sealing.

**Comparison:**

| Feature | Worm-Drive | T-Bolt |
|---------|-----------|--------|
| Pressure rating | Low–Medium | Medium–High |
| Torque capacity | 3–8 N·m | 10–20 N·m |
| Pressure uniformity | Moderate | High |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Typical use | Garden, water lines | Hydraulic, turbo, marine |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m
- $P_{int} = 80$ bar $= 80 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $L_c = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m
- $\mu = 0.35$

**Formula:**

$$
p_r \geq \dfrac{P_{int} \cdot D}{4 \mu L_c}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute values:**

$$
p_r \geq \dfrac{80 \times 10^5 \cdot 0.050}{4 \cdot 0.35 \cdot 0.025}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate numerator:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 80 \times 10^5 \cdot 0.050 = 4.0 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate denominator:**

$$
\text{Denominator} = 4 \cdot 0.35 \cdot 0.025 = 0.035
$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$
p_r \geq \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^5}{0.035} = 1.143 \times 10^7\ \text{Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{p_r \geq 11.43\ \text{MPa}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The clamp must apply a radial pressure of at least **11.43 MPa** to prevent blow-off of the hose at 80 bar internal pressure.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the **toggle clamp mechanism**. State two industrial applications of toggle clamps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A beam clamp of ultimate load capacity $F_{ult} = 30$ kN is to be used in a process plant where the design factor of safety is $n = 3$. Calculate the **safe working load** and comment on its suitability for supporting a cable tray weighing 7 kN under dynamic loading. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Toggle Clamp:**

**Working Principle:**
A toggle clamp uses a four-bar linkage with an over-center locking action. When the handle is moved past the dead-center (toggle) position, the linkage locks automatically, holding the workpiece without any further input force.

**Components:**
- Handle (actuating lever)
- Links (two or more)
- Pivot pins
- Clamping arm
- Workpiece contact pad
- Base plate

**Operation:**
- The handle is moved towards the clamping direction.
- The links move past their aligned (dead-center) position.
- The mechanical advantage increases sharply, locking the clamp in position.
- Even if external vibration or disturbance occurs, the linkage remains locked (self-locking).

**Industrial Applications:**
1. Used in **jigs and fixtures** for machining operations (drilling, milling, welding).
2. Used in **assembly lines** for rapid clamping of components (e.g., automotive body panels).
3. Used in **inspection fixtures** to hold parts during quality checks.

**Part (b) — Safe Working Load Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $F_{ult} = 30$ kN
- $n = 3$

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$
F_{SWL} = \dfrac{F_{ult}}{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
F_{SWL} = \dfrac{30}{3} = 10\ \text{kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment on suitability:**

- Applied load (cable tray) = 7 kN
- Safe working load of clamp = 10 kN
- Since 7 kN < 10 kN, the clamp is **safe under static loading**.

**However**, for **dynamic loading** (vibration, cyclic loads, seismic activity), a higher factor of safety is recommended (typically $n = 3.5$ to 4.0). With $n = 4$:

$$
F_{SWL, dynamic} = \dfrac{30}{4} = 7.5\ \text{kN}
$$

This is barely above the 7 kN applied load, indicating **marginal safety under dynamic conditions**. **Recommendation:** Use a higher-capacity beam clamp (e.g., $F_{ult} = 40$ kN) or implement vibration isolation measures.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Always convert bar to Pa, mm to m, kN to N before substituting into formulas. This is the **most common mistake** in numerical answers.
> 2. **Confusing $p_r$ (radial pressure) with $P_{int}$ (internal pressure):** They are not the same; the formula $p_r \geq \dfrac{P_{int} D}{4 \mu L_c}$ relates the two, but the answer must clearly state the calculated $p_r$ in MPa.
> 3. **Forgetting to draw the diagram:** In 7-mark sub-parts, a labeled sketch is mandatory. Marks are specifically allotted for the diagram (usually 1–2 marks).
> 4. **Ignoring the factor of safety:** Never present $F_{ult}$ as the working load. Always apply the appropriate safety factor.
> 5. **Omitting units in the final answer:** A numerical answer without units is treated as incomplete in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 4 - Clamps):**

- A **clamp** is a mechanical device that applies sustained gripping force to prevent movement, leakage, or failure of components.
- The fundamental operating principle is **friction** combined with **mechanical interference fit**.
- The governing equation for a screw-actuated clamp is: $F_c = \dfrac{T}{r_t \cdot \tan(\lambda + \phi)}$
- The minimum radial pressure for hose blow-off prevention is: $p_r \geq \dfrac{P_{int} \cdot D}{4 \mu L_c}$
- The safe working load formula is: $F_{SWL} = \dfrac{F_{ult}}{n}$
- Major clamp types: **Worm-drive, T-bolt, Pipe support, Beam, Toggle, Hydraulic, Spring, Wedge**.
- Worm-drive clamps are for **low–medium pressure**; T-bolt clamps for **high-pressure** applications.
- Toggle clamps use the **over-center locking principle** for quick, positive locking.
- Beam clamps attach to structural steel without **welding or drilling** (preferred in hazardous zones).
- Pipe clamps transfer **gravity loads** from piping to structural supports.
- **Material selection** depends on service environment: SS 316 for corrosive, mild steel for general, nylon for electrical insulation.
- **Factor of safety** ranges from **2.0 (general) to 4.0 (cryogenic/dynamic)**.
- **Standards to remember:** ISO 2852, DIN 3017, ASME B31.3, ASTM F879.
- Approximately **18% of mechanical failures** in process plants are linked to inadequate fastening — a key reason clamps are critical to **hazard control**.
- Always check **torque values** during installation; under-torquing causes leaks, over-torquing damages the hose or bolt.
- **Periodic inspection** of clamps is essential — clamps are not "fit-and-forget" components.
